title: CASPIAN: Online Detection and Attribution of Cascade Attacks in LLM Multi-Agent Systems via Cross-Channel Causal Monitoring
summary: Cascade attacks in LLM multi-agent systems (MAS) arise when adversarial influence propagates across agents and leads to escalated system-level failures through complex agent interactions. Detecting such cascades is challenging, as their signals are distributed, tightly coupled across interaction channels, and often appear plausibly benign locally but may unfold quickly either within a single turn or gradually across multiple turns. Existing defenses, being largely local and text-centric, fail to capture such cross-channel, temporally coordinated dynamics of cascade propagation. Therefore, we propose CASPIAN, the first framework that provides a unified, cross-channel causal analysis of cascade behavior in LLM-MAS through online monitoring of dynamic influence propagation across agents. CASPIAN models multi-agent interactions using a unified, dynamic causal influence matrix across channels, estimated efficiently via a late-interaction conditional transfer entropy (LI-CTE) formulation, thereby enabling the detection of cascade onset from emergent system-level structure rather than isolated anomalies. It further performs online causal attribution, identifying the origin, bridge, and amplifier agents driving the cascade and reconstructing its principal propagation pathways, capabilities not supported by existing methods. Across diverse multi-agent frameworks and benchmarks, CASPIAN consistently outperforms semantic guardrails, LLM-based judges, and graph-based anomaly detectors in both detection accuracy and early cascade identification while operating with sub-1% relative overhead latency. These results demonstrate that unified cross-channel causal modeling is essential for reliably detecting and understanding cascade failures in LLM multi-agent systems.

AI.tldr: CASPIAN: First framework for detecting cascade attacks in LLM multi-agent systems using unified cross-channel causal analysis and online monitoring of dynamic influence propagation.
AI.motivation: Cascade attacks in LLM multi-agent systems propagate adversarial influence across agents, leading to system-level failures. Existing defenses fail to capture cross-channel, temporally coordinated dynamics of cascade propagation because they are local and text-centric.
AI.method: Proposes CASPIAN framework that models multi-agent interactions using a unified, dynamic causal influence matrix across channels. Uses late-interaction conditional transfer entropy (LI-CTE) for efficient estimation, enabling detection of cascade onset from emergent system-level structure rather than isolated anomalies. Performs online causal attribution to identify origin, bridge, and amplifier agents.
AI.result: CASPIAN consistently outperforms semantic guardrails, LLM-based judges, and graph-based anomaly detectors in both detection accuracy and early cascade identification across diverse multi-agent frameworks and benchmarks, while operating with sub-1% relative overhead latency.
AI.conclusion: Unified cross-channel causal modeling is essential for reliably detecting and understanding cascade failures in LLM multi-agent systems, as demonstrated by CASPIAN's superior performance.

title: STAR-PólyaMath: Multi-Agent Reasoning under Persistent Meta-Strategic Supervision
summary: Frontier AI models and multi-agent systems have led to significant improvements in mathematical reasoning. However, for problems requiring extended, long-horizon reasoning, existing systems continue to suffer from fundamental reliability issues: hallucination accumulation, memory fragmentation, and imbalanced reasoning-tool trade-offs. In this paper, we introduce STAR-PólyaMath, a multi-agent framework that systematically addresses these challenges through meta-level supervision and structured Reasoner-Verifier interaction. STAR-PólyaMath is structured as an orchestrated state machine with nested challenge-step-replan loops, governed by a reasoning-free Python orchestrator that separates control from inference and bounds error propagation through trace-back and re-planning. Our key innovation is a persistent Meta-Strategist that maintains cross-attempt memory and exercises meta-level control by issuing high-level strategic guidance or mandatory directives, so the system can escape unproductive loops rather than stagnate or over-rely on tools. STAR-PólyaMath achieves state-of-the-art results on all eight top-tier competition benchmarks: AIME 2025-2026, MathArena Apex Shortlist, MathArena Apex 2025, Putnam 2025, IMO 2025, HMMT February 2026, and USAMO 2026. It obtains perfect scores on AIMEs, Putnam, and HMMT, and shows its largest margin on Apex 2025, scoring 93.75% compared with 80.21% by the strongest baseline GPT-5.5. Ablation studies show that the gains arise from the framework's orchestration rather than from model-level diversity since removing key components or substituting in mixed backbones consistently weakens performance. Code is available at https://github.com/Julius-Woo/STAR-PolyaMath.

AI.tldr: STAR-PólyaMath is a multi-agent framework with meta-level supervision that achieves SOTA results on top math competition benchmarks through structured orchestration and persistent meta-strategist control.
AI.motivation: Existing AI systems for mathematical reasoning suffer from reliability issues including hallucination accumulation, memory fragmentation, and imbalanced reasoning-tool trade-offs, especially in long-horizon problems.
AI.method: A multi-agent framework with orchestrated state machine design featuring nested challenge-step-replan loops, separated control from inference via Python orchestrator, and a persistent Meta-Strategist for cross-attempt memory and meta-level control.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on all eight top-tier competition benchmarks (AIME 2025-2026, MathArena Apex, Putnam 2025, IMO 2025, HMMT, USAMO 2026), with perfect scores on some and largest margin of 93.75% vs 80.21% on Apex 2025 compared to GPT-5.5 baseline.
AI.conclusion: The framework's orchestration, rather than model diversity, drives performance gains, as shown by ablation studies where removing key components consistently weakens performance. The system effectively addresses reliability issues in long-horizon mathematical reasoning.

title: PAVE: A Cognitive Architecture for Legitimate Violation in Generative Agent Societies
comment: Preprint. 23 pages, 4 figures. Code and environment will be released upon publication
summary: Generative agents based on large language models reproduce believable human behavior in cooperative settings, but how they should reason in situations where rule-breaking may be required, such as fire evacuation or authority-supervised emergency, remains poorly characterized. We propose PAVE (Perception, Assessment, Verdict, Emulation), a novel four-module cognitive architecture that addresses this gap end to end: (i) Perception extracts a structured context with explicit authority distance, peer behaviors, and severity-tagged situational cues; (ii) Assessment scores the context along five scalars including an explicit legitimacy judgment that checks necessity, proportionality, and absence of alternatives; (iii) Verdict decides to comply or violate under a hard legitimacy gate, with a per-agent threshold elicited from the persona; (iv) Emulation enacts the verdict and scopes the violation to the rule the trigger justifies. We instantiate PAVE in Voville, a tile-based traffic environment forked from Smallville, and evaluate across three scenarios, four LLM backbones, and a focused ablation. PAVE agents satisfy four properties simultaneously: legitimate violation (only when a trigger justifies it), authority deference (officer instructions override even high legitimacy), bounded scope (violations confined to the targeted rule), and recovery (baseline restored once the trigger ends). PAVE agents make more structured and interpretable decisions than vanilla across all four properties, and human evaluators rate them as more plausible. Ablating the legitimacy gate reproduces vanilla-like failures. We release Voville, the PAVE prompts and code, and the evaluation pipeline.

AI.tldr: PAVE: A cognitive architecture for LLM-based agents to reason about rule-breaking in emergencies, enabling legitimate violations when justified while maintaining authority deference and bounded scope.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based agents reproduce believable human behavior in cooperative settings but lack proper reasoning mechanisms for situations where rule-breaking may be required, such as fire evacuations or authority-supervised emergencies.
AI.method: PAVE (Perception, Assessment, Verdict, Emulation) - a four-module cognitive architecture: 1) Perception extracts structured context with authority distance, peer behaviors, and severity-tagged cues; 2) Assessment scores context along five scalars including legitimacy judgment; 3) Verdict decides compliance/violation with legitimacy gate and persona-based threshold; 4) Emulation enacts verdict and scopes violation to justified rules.
AI.result: PAVE agents satisfy four key properties: legitimate violation, authority deference, bounded scope, and recovery. They make more structured and interpretable decisions than vanilla agents, and human evaluators rate them as more plausible. Ablating the legitimacy gate reproduces vanilla-like failures.
AI.conclusion: PAVE successfully addresses the gap in rule-breaking reasoning for LLM-based agents, providing a structured cognitive architecture that enables legitimate, authority-respecting, and interpretable decision-making in emergency scenarios while maintaining proper bounds on violations.

title: LLM Agents Make Collective Belief Dynamics Programmable: Challenges and Research Directions
summary: Classical models of opinion dynamics assume human participants with bounded rationality and limited coordination. The rise of LLM-based agents introduces a qualitative shift: agents can now participate in online discussions at scale, maintain consistent persuasion strategies, and coordinate systematically. This paper argues that LLM agents make collective belief dynamics programmable, enabling deliberate steering of population-level beliefs. We term this emerging problem programmable collective belief control. Through controlled multi-agent simulations, we provide proof-of-concept evidence that coordinated AI agents can induce measurable belief shifts that stabilize within a few interaction rounds. We identify four structural properties (indistinguishability, persistence, contextuality, and configurability) that make detection and defense fundamentally difficult. Based on these findings, we outline a research agenda spanning theoretical foundations for adversarial belief dynamics, operational methods for system-level detection and intervention, and simulation infrastructure for scalable experimentation. Our goal is not to present a complete solution, but to articulate why this problem demands urgent attention and to provide a conceptual foundation for future work.

AI.tldr: LLM-based agents can programmatically steer collective beliefs in online discussions, making population-level belief dynamics controllable and introducing difficult detection/defense challenges.
AI.motivation: The rise of LLM-based agents represents a qualitative shift from classical opinion dynamics models where human participants have bounded rationality and limited coordination. LLM agents can now participate at scale, maintain consistent persuasion strategies, and coordinate systematically, making collective belief dynamics potentially programmable.
AI.method: Controlled multi-agent simulations to provide proof-of-concept evidence that coordinated AI agents can induce measurable belief shifts that stabilize within few interaction rounds. Analysis of structural properties that make detection/defense difficult.
AI.result: Proof-of-concept evidence shows coordinated AI agents can induce measurable belief shifts that stabilize quickly. Identified four key structural properties: indistinguishability (hard to detect), persistence (consistent strategies), contextuality (adapts to context), and configurability (easy to adjust). These properties make detection and defense fundamentally difficult.
AI.conclusion: LLM agents make collective belief dynamics programmable, enabling deliberate steering of population-level beliefs (programmable collective belief control). This problem demands urgent attention. The paper outlines a research agenda including theoretical foundations, operational methods for detection/intervention, and simulation infrastructure for scalable experimentation.

title: Dimensional Balance Improves Large Scale Spatiotemporal Prediction Performance
summary: Accurate spatiotemporal pattern analysis is critical in fields such as urban traffic, meteorology, and public health monitoring. However, existing methods face performance bottlenecks, typically yielding only incremental gains and often exhibiting limited cross-domain transferability. We analyze this bottleneck through spatial and temporal entropy measures, which are used as diagnostic indicators of spatiotemporal complexity mismatch rather than as guarantees that entropy alignment alone yields better forecasting. Empirically, larger mismatch is often accompanied by higher prediction uncertainty, especially under a fixed model-capacity budget. Guided by this diagnostic, we propose a scalable, adaptive framework that harmonizes spatial and temporal feature representations. Spatial dimensionality is compressed via low-rank matrix embedding to preserve essential structure, while an extended temporal horizon captures long-range dependencies and mitigates cumulative errors arising from temporal heterogeneity. Extensive experiments on urban traffic, meteorological, and epidemic datasets demonstrate substantial accuracy gains and broad applicability across the evaluated domains, suggesting that the framework is promising for a wide range of spatiotemporal tasks beyond the current study. The code is available on GitHub at https://github.com/ST-Balance/ST-Balance.

AI.tldr: A scalable framework harmonizing spatial and temporal representations for spatiotemporal forecasting, using entropy diagnostics to address complexity mismatches and achieving substantial accuracy gains across urban traffic, meteorological, and epidemic domains.
AI.motivation: Existing spatiotemporal pattern analysis methods face performance bottlenecks with limited cross-domain transferability and only incremental gains. Performance issues stem from mismatches between spatial and temporal complexities that are not adequately addressed by current approaches.
AI.method: Uses spatial and temporal entropy measures as diagnostic indicators to identify complexity mismatches. Proposes an adaptive framework that harmonizes spatial and temporal features: spatial dimensionality is compressed via low-rank matrix embedding to preserve structure, while extended temporal horizon captures long-range dependencies and mitigates cumulative errors from temporal heterogeneity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on urban traffic, meteorological, and epidemic datasets demonstrate substantial accuracy gains and broad applicability across domains. The framework shows promising performance for a wide range of spatiotemporal tasks beyond the studied applications.
AI.conclusion: The proposed scalable, adaptive framework effectively addresses spatiotemporal complexity mismatches through harmonized feature representations, achieving superior forecasting performance across multiple domains and showing potential for diverse spatiotemporal applications. Code is publicly available on GitHub.

title: Position: Let's Develop Data Probes to Fundamentally Understand How Data Affects LLM Performance
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026 Position Paper Track
summary: Data is fundamental to large language models (LLMs). However, understanding of what makes certain data useful for different stages of an LLM workflow, including training, tuning, alignment, in-context learning, etc., and why, remains an open question. Current approaches rely heavily on extensive experimentation with large public datasets to obtain empirical heuristics for data filtering and dataset construction. These approaches are compute intensive and lack a principled way of understanding the essence of how specific data characteristics drive LLM behavior. In this position paper, we advocate for the need of developing systematic methodologies for generating synthetic sequences from appropriately defined random processes, with the goal that these sequences can reveal useful characteristics when they are used in one or multiple stages of the LLM workflow. We refer to such sequences as data probes. By observing LLM behavior on data probes, researchers can systematically conduct studies on how data characteristics influence model performance, generalization, and robustness. The probing sequences exhibit statistical properties that can be viewed using theoretical concepts, such as typical sets, which are generalized to describe the behaviors of LLMs. This data-probe approach provides a pathway for uncovering foundational insights into the role of data in LLM training and inference, beyond empirical heuristics.

AI.tldr: This paper argues for developing systematic methodologies to create synthetic data probes from random processes to systematically study how data characteristics influence LLM behavior across different workflow stages, moving beyond empirical heuristics.
AI.motivation: Current understanding of what makes data useful for different LLM workflow stages (training, tuning, alignment, in-context learning) is limited. Existing approaches rely on extensive experimentation with large datasets to develop empirical heuristics, which are compute-intensive and lack principled understanding of how specific data characteristics drive LLM behavior.
AI.method: Proposes generating synthetic sequences (data probes) from appropriately defined random processes. These probes are designed to reveal useful characteristics when used in LLM workflows. Researchers can systematically observe LLM behavior on these probes to study how data characteristics influence performance, generalization, and robustness. The approach leverages theoretical concepts like typical sets to analyze the statistical properties of probing sequences.
AI.result: The paper presents a conceptual framework for data probes but doesn't report empirical results since it's a position paper. The key outcome is the proposal of a systematic approach to study data-LLM interactions through controlled synthetic sequences.
AI.conclusion: The data-probe approach provides a pathway for uncovering foundational insights into the role of data in LLM training and inference, moving beyond empirical heuristics toward more principled understanding of how data characteristics influence model behavior across different workflow stages.

title: DecisionBench: A Benchmark for Emergent Delegation in Long-Horizon Agentic Workflows
comment: 28 pages, 9 figures, 11 tables. Code and data: https://huggingface.co/decisionbench
summary: We introduce DecisionBench, a benchmark substrate for emergent delegation in long-horizon agentic workflows. The substrate fixes a task suite (GAIA, tau-bench, BFCL multi-turn), a peer-model pool (11 models, 7 vendor families), a delegation interface (call_model plus an optional read_profile channel), a deterministic skill-annotation layer, and a multi-axis metric suite covering quality, cost, latency, delegation rate, routing fidelity-at-k, vendor self-preference, and a counterfactual-delegation ceiling. The substrate is agnostic to how peer information is generated or delivered, so learned routers, richer peer memories, adaptive profile construction, and multi-step delegation can all be evaluated against it. We characterize the substrate with a five-condition reference sweep on the full pool (n=23,375 task instances). Three benchmark-level findings emerge: (i) mean end-task quality is statistically indistinguishable across the four awareness conditions (|beta| <= 0.010, p >= 0.21), so quality-only evaluation would miss the orchestration signal; (ii) routing fidelity-at-1 ranges from 7.5% to 29.5% across conditions at near-equal mean quality, with delivery channel (on-demand tool vs. preloaded description) dominating description content; (iii) a counterfactual ceiling places perfect delegation 15-31 percentage points above measured performance on every suite, locating large unrealized headroom for future orchestration methods. We release the substrate, annotation layer, reference intervention suite, analysis pipeline, and 220 per-condition run archives.

AI.tldr: DecisionBench is a benchmark for evaluating emergent delegation in long-horizon agentic workflows, providing tools to assess how AI agents delegate tasks to peer models across multiple performance axes including quality, cost, and routing accuracy.
AI.motivation: There's a need for systematic evaluation of delegation strategies in multi-agent workflows, as quality-only metrics fail to capture orchestration effectiveness. Current benchmarks lack comprehensive metrics for assessing how agents choose and route tasks to appropriate peer models.
AI.method: The authors create a benchmark substrate with: fixed task suite (GAIA, tau-bench, BFCL multi-turn), peer-model pool (11 models from 7 vendors), delegation interface (call_model with optional profile reading), deterministic skill annotation layer, and multi-axis metric suite covering quality, cost, latency, delegation rate, routing fidelity, vendor self-preference, and counterfactual-delegation ceiling.
AI.result: Key findings from 23,375 task instances: 1) End-task quality is statistically indistinguishable across awareness conditions, showing quality-only evaluation misses orchestration signals; 2) Routing fidelity-at-1 ranges from 7.5% to 29.5% with delivery channel dominating description content; 3) Perfect delegation shows 15-31 percentage points of unrealized headroom above current performance.
AI.conclusion: DecisionBench provides a standardized substrate for evaluating delegation strategies in agentic workflows, revealing that current methods miss significant orchestration improvements, with substantial headroom available for future methods to approach perfect delegation ceilings.

title: Robust Basis Spline Decoupling for the Compression of Transformer Models
summary: Decoupling is a powerful modeling paradigm for representing multivariate functions as compositions of linear transformations and univariate nonlinear functions. A single-layer decoupling can be viewed as a fully connected neural network with a single hidden layer and flexible activation functions, providing a direct link with neural networks. Because of this, the use of decoupling methods has gained increasing attention in neural network domains, particularly compression, since it enables structured approximations with reduced parameter complexity. Existing tensor-based decoupling methods typically rely on polynomial or piecewise-linear parameterizations of the internal nonlinear functions, which can suffer from numerical instability or limited expressiveness. In this work, we introduce a B-spline-based decoupling framework that generalizes these existing approaches. By exploiting the local support and flexible smoothness control of B-splines, the proposed formulation yields a more numerically stable and expressive representation. We derive a constrained coupled matrix-tensor factorization and propose a robust alternating least-squares algorithm, called R-CMTF-BSD, incorporating normalization and Tikhonov regularization. The proposed method is validated through experiments on synthetic data and transformer model compression. Results on the Vision and Swin Transformer architectures demonstrate that B-spline decoupling enables substantial parameter reduction while maintaining competitive accuracy, making the R-CMTF-BSD algorithm a promising tool for structured neural network compression.

AI.tldr: B-spline-based decoupling framework for neural network compression that provides stable, expressive representations with competitive accuracy and substantial parameter reduction.
AI.motivation: Existing tensor-based decoupling methods for neural network compression rely on polynomial or piecewise-linear parameterizations that suffer from numerical instability or limited expressiveness, creating a need for more robust and expressive decoupling approaches.
AI.method: Introduces a B-spline-based decoupling framework exploiting local support and flexible smoothness control, derives constrained coupled matrix-tensor factorization, and proposes R-CMTF-BSD algorithm with alternating least-squares incorporating normalization and Tikhonov regularization.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic data and transformer model compression show the method enables substantial parameter reduction while maintaining competitive accuracy on Vision and Swin Transformer architectures.
AI.conclusion: B-spline decoupling provides a numerically stable and expressive representation, making R-CMTF-BSD algorithm a promising tool for structured neural network compression with reduced parameter complexity.

title: Operationalizing Document AI: A Microservice Architecture for OCR and LLM Pipelines in Production
summary: Academic research tends to focus on new models for document understanding creating a wide gap in the literature between model definition and running models at production scale. To close that gap, we present a microservice architecture that encapsulates pipelines of multiple models for classification, optical character recognition (OCR), and large language model structured field extraction as well as our experience running this pipeline on thousands of multi-page documents per hour. We describe our primary design decisions, including a hybrid classification, separation of GPU-bound inference from CPU-bound orchestration, use of asynchronous processing for the many IO-bound operations in the pipeline, and an independent, horizontal scaling strategy. Using batch profiling, we identified two surprising qualitative findings that shape production deployments: OCR, not language-model parsing, dominates end-to-end latency, and the system saturates at a concurrency determined by shared GPU-inference capacity rather than worker count. Our goal is to provide practitioners with concrete architectural patterns for building document understanding systems that work beyond the benchmark; effectively operationalizing models in production.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a production-ready microservice architecture for document understanding that bridges the gap between model research and real-world deployment, handling thousands of multi-page documents per hour with insights on scaling bottlenecks.
AI.motivation: There's a significant gap between academic research on new document understanding models and practical implementation at production scale. Academic literature focuses on model definition but lacks guidance on running models efficiently in real-world systems handling thousands of documents.
AI.method: Developed a microservice architecture encapsulating pipelines of multiple models (classification, OCR, LLM structured field extraction). Key design decisions include: hybrid classification, separation of GPU-bound inference from CPU-bound orchestration, asynchronous processing for IO-bound operations, and independent horizontal scaling strategy.
AI.result: Through batch profiling, discovered two surprising findings: 1) OCR dominates end-to-end latency (not language-model parsing), and 2) the system saturates at concurrency determined by shared GPU-inference capacity rather than worker count. Successfully ran the pipeline on thousands of multi-page documents per hour.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides practitioners with concrete architectural patterns for building document understanding systems that work beyond benchmarks, effectively operationalizing models in production by addressing scaling bottlenecks and providing a scalable microservice framework.

title: How Far Are We From True Auto-Research?
summary: Recent auto-research systems can produce complete papers, but feasibility is not the same as quality, and the field still lacks a systematic study of how good agent-generated papers actually are. We introduce ResearchArena, a minimal scaffold that lets off-the-shelf agents (Claude Code using Opus 4.6, Codex using GPT-5.4, and Kimi Code using K2.5) carry out the full research loop themselves (ideation, experimentation, paper writing, self-refinement) under only lightweight guidance. Across 13 computer science seeds and 3 trials per agent-domain pair, ResearchArena yields 117 agent-generated papers, each evaluated under three complementary lenses: a manuscript-only reviewer (SAR), an artifact-aware peer review (PR) in which agents inspect the workspace alongside the manuscript, and an human conducted meta-review. Under SAR alone the picture is optimistic: Claude Code obtains the highest score, outperforms Analemma's FARS, and matches the weighted-average human ICLR 2025 submission, suggesting that minimally scaffolded agents can produce papers that look competitive on manuscript-only review. Manual inspection, however, reveals this picture is overstated: SAR scores are poorly aligned with its actual acceptance decisions and reward plausible framing without verifying experimental substance. Under artifact-aware PR scores drop sharply, and manual auditing identifies experimental rigor as the major bottleneck, decomposing into three failure modes (fabricated results, underpowered experiments, and plan/execution mismatch) that are highly agent-dependent: Codex 5%/8% paper-vs-artifact mismatch / fabricated references versus Kimi Code 77%/72%, a $\sim$15$\times$ spread that tracks distinct research personas the agents develop. None of the 117 agent-generated papers reaches the acceptance bar of a top-tier venue. This suggests that we are still gapped from the true auto-research.

AI.tldr: Agent-generated research papers look promising in manuscript-only review but fail under artifact-aware evaluation due to experimental rigor issues.
AI.motivation: While auto-research systems can produce complete papers, there's a lack of systematic study on the actual quality of agent-generated papers and whether they can match human research standards.
AI.method: Introduced ResearchArena, a minimal scaffold allowing off-the-shelf agents (Claude Code, Codex, Kimi Code) to conduct full research loops (ideation, experimentation, writing, self-refinement) across 13 CS domains with 3 trials per agent-domain pair, producing 117 papers evaluated under three lenses: manuscript-only review (SAR), artifact-aware peer review (PR), and human meta-review.
AI.result: Under SAR, Claude Code performed best and matched human ICLR submissions, suggesting competitive manuscript quality. However, SAR scores poorly aligned with actual acceptance decisions. Under artifact-aware PR, scores dropped sharply with experimental rigor as major bottleneck - revealing three failure modes: fabricated results, underpowered experiments, and plan/execution mismatch. Agent failure rates varied dramatically (Codex 5-8% vs Kimi Code 72-77%). None of 117 papers reached top-tier acceptance bar.
AI.conclusion: Current auto-research systems are still far from producing genuinely rigorous research papers; manuscript-only evaluation overestimates quality while artifact-aware review reveals critical experimental deficiencies, indicating we're still "gapped from true auto-research."

title: HELLoRA: Hot Experts Layer-Level Low-Rank Adaptation for Mixture-of-Experts Models
summary: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) dominates parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models, yet most variants target dense architectures. Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models scale parameters at near-constant per-token compute, and their sparse activation patterns create untapped opportunities for more efficient adaptation. We propose Hot-Experts Layer-level Low-Rank Adaptation (HELLoRA), which attaches LoRA modules only to the most frequently activated experts at each layer. This simple mechanism reduces trainable parameters and adapter-induced FLOPs while improving downstream performance, an effect we attribute to a form of structured regularization that preserves pretrained expert specialization. To stress-test HELLoRA under extreme parameter budgets, we further compose it with LoRI to form HELLoRI, which freezes the up-projection and sparsifies the down-projection. Across three MoE backbones, namely OlMoE-1B-7B, Mixtral-8x7B, and DeepSeekMoE, and three task families covering mathematical reasoning, code generation, and safety alignment, HELLoRA consistently outperforms strong PEFT baselines. Relative to vanilla LoRA on OlMoE, HELLoRA uses 15.7% of the trainable parameters, reduces adapter FLOPs by 38.7%, achieves 1.9x the training throughput, and improves accuracy by 9.2%. On DeepSeekMoE, HELLoRA outperforms LoRA while using only 23.2% of its trainable parameters. These results demonstrate that activation-aware adapter placement is an effective and practical route to scaling PEFT for MoE language models.

AI.tldr: HELLoRA is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method for Mixture-of-Experts models that attaches LoRA adapters only to the most frequently activated experts per layer, reducing parameters and FLOPs while improving performance.
AI.motivation: Current LoRA variants focus on dense architectures, but MoE models have sparse activation patterns that create untapped opportunities for more efficient adaptation. The authors aim to exploit these patterns to reduce trainable parameters and computational costs while maintaining or improving performance.
AI.method: HELLoRA attaches LoRA modules only to the most frequently activated experts at each layer. For extreme parameter budgets, they combine it with LoRI to form HELLoRI, which freezes the up-projection and sparsifies the down-projection. The method selects experts based on activation frequency during training.
AI.result: HELLoRA consistently outperforms strong PEFT baselines across three MoE backbones (OlMoE-1B-7B, Mixtral-8x7B, DeepSeekMoE) and three task families. On OlMoE, it uses 15.7% of trainable parameters, reduces adapter FLOPs by 38.7%, achieves 1.9x training throughput, and improves accuracy by 9.2%. On DeepSeekMoE, it outperforms LoRA with only 23.2% of parameters.
AI.conclusion: Activation-aware adapter placement is an effective and practical approach for scaling parameter-efficient fine-tuning for MoE language models, offering significant parameter reduction, computational efficiency, and performance improvements over standard LoRA.

title: Evaluating the Utility of Personal Health Records in Personalized Health AI
summary: Patient-managed Personal Health Records (PHRs) promises to empower patients to better understand their health; but information in the record is complex, potentially hindering insights. In this study, we assess the potential of large language models (LLMs, Gemini 3.0 Flash) to provide helpful answers to user health queries, when provided clinical data from PHRs as context. A total of 2,257 user queries were drawn from 3 different distributions to represent patient questions: shorter web search queries, longer questions derived from templates of chatbot conversations, and questions patients asked to their healthcare team (patient calls). Queries were matched with de-identified PHRs (from a pool of 1,945). Gemini responses were generated (1) without PHR context; (2) with a basic summary of demographics, conditions, and medications; (3) with full, extensive clinical notes. For evaluation, we leveraged an existing rating framework (SHARP), and developed a new framework for specific error modes when interpreting PHRs. Evaluation was performed using autoraters for the full set, and with clinician ratings for a subset (n=95), with both sets of raters knowing the full PHR context. We see significant improvements in the helpfulness of answers to all question types with PHR data (p < 0.001, paired t-test). We also observe potential gains in safety, accuracy, relevance and personalization of answers. Our PHR evaluation framework further identifies gaps in LLM understanding of particular aspects of complex PHRs, such as temporal disorientation, and rare but meaningful confabulations. These results suggest potential for PHR data to help people with a wide range of user needs; and provide a framework for monitoring for gaps in LLM answers based on PHR context. This study motivates further work to assess and realize potential benefits to users from understanding their health records.

AI.tldr: LLMs with PHR context significantly improve answer helpfulness for patient health queries across different question types, though gaps remain in temporal understanding and rare confabulations.
AI.motivation: Personal Health Records (PHRs) are complex and may hinder patient insights despite promising empowerment. The study aims to assess if LLMs can provide helpful answers to health queries when given PHR context.
AI.method: Used Gemini 3.0 Flash LLM with 2,257 queries from three distributions (web search, chatbot templates, patient calls) matched with 1,945 de-identified PHRs. Generated responses under three conditions: no context, basic summary, full clinical notes. Evaluated using SHARP framework plus new framework for PHR-specific error modes via autoraters and clinician ratings.
AI.result: Significant improvement in helpfulness with PHR data (p<0.001). Potential gains in safety, accuracy, relevance, and personalization. Identified gaps in LLM understanding of complex PHR aspects like temporal disorientation and rare confabulations.
AI.conclusion: PHR data shows potential to help people understand their health records better. Provides a framework for monitoring LLM answer gaps based on PHR context and motivates further work to realize user benefits.

title: AQuaUI: Visual Token Reduction for GUI Agents with Adaptive Quadtrees
summary: Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) have recently emerged as promising backbones for GUI-agent models, where high-resolution GUI screenshots are introduced to the prompts at each iteration step. However, these screenshots exhibit highly non-uniform spatial information density: large regions may carry little information and are visually homogeneous, while key text and icons may require high visual fidelity. Existing approaches to this problem either require additional training or rely on attention-based token compression, ignoring the structured layout and spatial redundancy of GUI screenshots. To fill the gap, this paper proposes AquaUI, a training-free inference-time token reduction method for GUI agent models that utilizes the non-uniform information density in screenshots. AQuaUI constructs an adaptive quadtree on each screenshot input and keeps one representative merged token per leaf of the quadtree. AQuaUI preserves the spatial positions of retained tokens throughout the pipeline to ensure that all position-encoding stages remain consistent. To further improve temporal consistency across multi-step GUI interactions, we propose a conditional quadtree algorithm that leverages the continuity between consecutive screenshots within a single request. Specifically, it refines the current quadtree using previous quadtrees as references, helping preserve fine-grained regions across static or mildly shifted GUI states. We implement AQuaUI on state-of-the-art GUI agent models and conduct experiments on standard grounding and navigational benchmarks. AQuaUI consistently shows improved accuracy-efficiency trade-offs over prior baselines. Notably, on GUI-Owl-1.5-32B-Instruct, AQuaUI achieves up to 13.22% speedup and 29.52% fewer visual tokens while retaining 99.06% of full-token performance, suggesting that the spatial redundancy of GUI screenshots can be exploited at inference without retraining.

AI.tldr: AQuaUI is a training-free, quadtree-based token reduction method for GUI agent models that exploits spatial redundancy in GUI screenshots without sacrificing performance.
AI.motivation: Existing GUI agent models using LMMs face efficiency issues due to high-resolution GUI screenshots with non-uniform information density - large homogeneous regions waste tokens while critical elements require high fidelity. Current solutions either need retraining or ignore GUI structural layout.
AI.method: Proposes AQuaUI: adaptive quadtree construction on screenshots, keeping one representative merged token per leaf while preserving spatial positions. Adds conditional quadtree algorithm for temporal consistency across multi-step interactions by refining current quadtree using previous ones as references.
AI.result: AQuaUI achieves up to 13.22% speedup and 29.52% fewer visual tokens while retaining 99.06% of full-token performance on GUI-Owl-1.5-32B-Instruct. Consistently shows improved accuracy-efficiency trade-offs across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: GUI screenshot spatial redundancy can be effectively exploited at inference time without retraining. AQuaUI offers an efficient, training-free solution for GUI agent models by leveraging GUI structure and temporal consistency.

title: UCCI: Calibrated Uncertainty for Cost-Optimal LLM Cascade Routing
summary: LLM cascades and model routing promise lower inference cost by sending easy queries to a small model and escalating hard ones to a large model, but most deployed routers use uncalibrated confidence scores and require per-workload threshold tuning. We present UCCI, a calibration-first router that maps token-level margin uncertainty to a per-query error probability via isotonic regression and selects the escalation threshold by constrained cost minimization. Under three explicit assumptions, threshold policies on the calibrated score are cost-optimal, and isotonic calibration achieves O(n^{-1/3}) sample complexity for expected calibration error (ECE). On a production named entity recognition workload of 75,000 queries served by 4B and 12B instruction-tuned LLMs on H100 GPUs, UCCI cuts inference cost by 31% (95% CI: [27%, 35%]) at micro-F1 = 0.91 while reducing ECE from 0.12 to 0.03. At the same operating point, UCCI beats entropy thresholding, split-conformal routing, and a FrugalGPT-style learned threshold. All cascade results use end-to-end routing on actual model outputs and measured H100 latency, not simulated routing from global accuracies or nominal API prices.

AI.tldr: UCCI is a calibration-first router for LLM cascades that maps token-level uncertainty to query error probabilities, achieving 31% cost reduction while maintaining high accuracy and reducing calibration error.
AI.motivation: Current LLM cascade routers use uncalibrated confidence scores and require per-workload threshold tuning, which is inefficient and suboptimal. There's a need for a systematic approach to optimize the cost-accuracy trade-off in model routing without manual threshold adjustments.
AI.method: UCCI uses isotonic regression to calibrate token-level margin uncertainty into per-query error probabilities, then selects escalation thresholds through constrained cost minimization. It operates on three explicit assumptions to ensure threshold policies on calibrated scores are cost-optimal.
AI.result: On a production named entity recognition workload with 75,000 queries using 4B and 12B LLMs on H100 GPUs, UCCI reduces inference cost by 31% (95% CI: [27%, 35%]) at micro-F1 = 0.91 while reducing expected calibration error from 0.12 to 0.03. It outperforms entropy thresholding, split-conformal routing, and FrugalGPT-style learned thresholds.
AI.conclusion: Calibration-first routing with UCCI provides significant cost savings while maintaining accuracy in LLM cascades, with formal optimality guarantees under specified assumptions and practical deployment advantages over existing methods.

title: Learn-by-Wire Training Control Governance: Bounded Autonomous Training Under Stress for Stability and Efficiency
summary:
Modern language-model training is increasingly exposed to instability, degraded runs, and wasted compute, especially under aggressive learning-rate, scale, and runtime-stress conditions. This paper introduces Learn-by-Wire Guard (LBW-Guard), a bounded autonomous training-control governance layer that operates above AdamW. Rather than replacing the optimizer update rule, LBW-Guard observes training telemetry, interprets instability-sensitive regimes, and applies bounded control to optimizer execution while preserving fixed training objectives.
  We evaluate LBW-Guard in a Qwen2.5-centered stress-and-robustness suite using WikiText-103, with Qwen2.5-7B as the empirical anchor, model-size comparisons against Qwen2.5-3B and Qwen2.5-14B, learning-rate stress tests, gradient-clipping baselines, and a no-LoRA TinyLlama-1B full-parameter sanity check. In the 7B reference setting, LBW-Guard reduces final perplexity from 13.21 to 10.74, an 18.7% improvement, while reducing end-to-end time from 392.54s to 357.02s, a 1.10x speedup. Under stronger learning-rate stress, AdamW degrades to 1885.24 final perplexity at LR=3e-3 and 659.76 at LR=1e-3, whereas LBW-Guard remains trainable at 11.57 and 10.33, respectively. Gradient-clipping baselines do not reproduce this effect.
  These results support a scoped systems conclusion that stability-sensitive LLM training can benefit from a governance plane above the optimizer. LBW-Guard provides evidence that bounded runtime control can preserve productive compute under stress while remaining distinct from optimizer replacement and local gradient suppression.

AI.tldr: LBW-Guard is a governance layer above AdamW that improves training stability under stress conditions, reducing perplexity by 18.7% while speeding up training, without replacing the optimizer.
AI.motivation: Modern language model training suffers from instability, degraded runs, and wasted compute under aggressive learning rates, scale, and runtime stress conditions. There's a need for better training stability without fundamentally changing optimizers.
AI.method: LBW-Guard operates as a bounded autonomous training-control governance layer above AdamW. It observes training telemetry, interprets instability-sensitive regimes, and applies bounded control to optimizer execution while preserving fixed training objectives, distinct from optimizer replacement or gradient clipping.
AI.result: For Qwen2.5-7B: reduced perplexity from 13.21 to 10.74 (18.7% improvement) and training time from 392.54s to 357.02s (1.10x speedup). Under LR stress: AdamW degraded to 1885.24 perplexity at LR=3e-3, while LBW-Guard maintained 11.57. At LR=1e-3, AdamW: 659.76, LBW-Guard: 10.33. Gradient clipping didn't achieve similar results.
AI.conclusion: Stability-sensitive LLM training benefits from a governance plane above the optimizer. Bounded runtime control can preserve productive compute under stress while remaining distinct from optimizer replacement and local gradient suppression.

title: Swimming with Whales: Analysis of Power Imbalances in Stake-Weighted Governance
summary: Voting methods weighted by stakes are the fundamental governance paradigm in Proof-of-Stake (PoS) blockchains. Such a paradigm is known to be prone to power distortions: a few users possessing large stakes may completely control decision making, even without owning the totality of the stakes. We study this phenomenon through the lens of computational social choice, focusing on the extent of power imbalances in stake-weighted voting when power is quantified using the Penrose-Banzhaf power index. Our work presents both analytical and empirical contributions. Analytically, we demonstrate that while a perfect alignment between power and relative stake ownership is generally unattainable, it can be approximated in expectation under specific conditions. Empirically, using data from a real-world on-chain governance system (Project Catalyst), we provide a more fine-grained understanding of the power imbalances that are likely to occur in current stake-weighted governance systems.

AI.tldr: Analysis of power imbalances in stake-weighted voting on PoS blockchains using Penrose-Banzhaf power index, showing imperfect alignment between stakes and voting power.
AI.motivation: Stake-weighted voting in PoS blockchains is prone to power distortions where a few large stakeholders can control decision-making, creating governance imbalances that need systematic study.
AI.method: Combined computational social choice approach using Penrose-Banzhaf power index to quantify power imbalances, with analytical proofs and empirical analysis of real-world on-chain governance data (Project Catalyst).
AI.result: Perfect alignment between power and relative stake ownership is generally unattainable but can be approximated in expectation under specific conditions; empirical analysis reveals fine-grained power imbalances in current stake-weighted systems.
AI.conclusion: Stake-weighted voting systems exhibit inherent power imbalances, and understanding these through computational social choice methods provides insights for designing more equitable blockchain governance mechanisms.

title: Simply Stabilizing the Loop via Fully Looped Transformer
summary: Scaling model performance typically requires increasing model size. Looped Transformer offers a compelling alternative by iteratively reusing the same Transformer blocks, trading additional computation for improved performance without increasing parameter count or context length. Because the number of loop iterations can be adjusted at inference, it also provides a natural mechanism for balancing performance and test-time compute. However, Looped Transformer still suffers from training instability when the number of loop iterations increases. Our analysis reveals that this instability stems from two sources: gradient oscillation and residual explosion. To address these two problems, we propose the Fully Looped Transformer, which introduces two parameter-free modifications: (1) Fully Looped Architecture, which distributes inter-loop signals across all layers to mitigate residual explosion; (2) Attention Injection, which reuses the existing attention block to suppress gradient oscillation. These modifications stabilize training dynamics, enabling the Fully Looped Transformer to be trained stably up to 12 loop iterations, whereas other baseline looped models collapse in this regime. In milder settings where Looped Transformer does not collapse, Fully Looped Transformer still improves average downstream-task performance by up to 13.2\%. Overall, our experiments demonstrate that Fully Looped Transformer improves training stability, enhances downstream performance, and provides preliminary adaptability under different test-time compute budgets by varying loop iterations at inference.

AI.tldr: Fully Looped Transformer addresses training instability in looped Transformers through two parameter-free modifications: Fully Looped Architecture to prevent residual explosion and Attention Injection to suppress gradient oscillation.
AI.motivation: Looped Transformers offer computational efficiency by reusing transformer blocks, but suffer from training instability when increasing loop iterations due to gradient oscillation and residual explosion.
AI.method: Two parameter-free modifications: 1) Fully Looped Architecture distributes inter-loop signals across all layers to mitigate residual explosion; 2) Attention Injection reuses existing attention blocks to suppress gradient oscillation.
AI.result: Fully Looped Transformer trains stably up to 12 loop iterations (where baseline looped models collapse), improves average downstream-task performance by up to 13.2%, and enables adaptable inference compute by varying loop iterations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Fully Looped Transformer addresses fundamental stability issues in looped architectures, enabling stable training, enhanced performance, and flexible compute-performance trade-offs at inference time.

title: AgentNLQ: A General-Purpose Agent for Natural Language to SQL
summary: Natural language to SQL (NL2SQL) conversion is an important problem for researchers and enterprises due to the ubiquitous importance of relational databases in broad-ranging practical problems. Despite the rapid advancements in the capabilities of LLMs, NL2SQL has not reached parity in accuracy with human expert SQL writers, hence needing additional improvements in NL2SQL algorithms. This study presents a new multi-agent method for NL2SQL that achieves 78.1% semantic accuracy on the BIg Bench for LaRge-scale Database (BIRD) benchmark. Our method leverages a semantically enriched representation of user-provided schema, adds user-provided business rules, and produces accurate SQL queries. The main contributions of this study are (a) We designed an optimized new orchestrator in a multi-agent solution that uses LLMs to plan, orchestrate, reflect, and self-correct to generate accurate SQL queries, (b) We developed an advanced schema enrichment method that creates context-aware metadata to improve accuracy, and (c) We demonstrated the accuracy and generalizability of the method across different domains and datasets by evaluating it on the BIRD-SQL benchmark.

AI.tldr: A novel multi-agent NL2SQL method achieves 78.1% semantic accuracy on the BIRD benchmark by leveraging semantically enriched schema representation, business rules integration, and an intelligent orchestrator that plans, reflects, and self-corrects.
AI.motivation: Despite rapid advancements in LLM capabilities, NL2SQL conversion has not yet reached parity with human expert SQL writers in accuracy, indicating a need for further improvement in NL2SQL algorithms to bridge this gap.
AI.method: Multi-agent approach with an optimized orchestrator that uses LLMs to plan, orchestrate, reflect, and self-correct; includes an advanced schema enrichment method creating context-aware metadata; incorporates user-provided business rules and semantically enriched schema representation.
AI.result: Achieved 78.1% semantic accuracy on the BIRD benchmark, demonstrating strong performance and generalizability across different domains and datasets in the challenging BIRD-SQL evaluation framework.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-agent NL2SQL method effectively addresses accuracy limitations through intelligent orchestration, schema enrichment, and business rule integration, showing promising results for practical database query generation applications.

title: EngiAI: A Multi-Agent Framework and Benchmark Suite for LLM-Driven Engineering Design
comment: 26 pages, 10 figures, to be published at IDETC 2026
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) agents are increasingly applied to engineering design tasks, yet existing evaluation frameworks do not adequately address multi-agent systems that combine simulation, retrieval, and manufacturing preparation. We introduce a benchmark suite with three evaluation dimensions: (1) a workflow benchmark with seven prompt styles targeting distinct cognitive demands-including direct tool use, semantic disambiguation, conditional branching, and working-memory tasks; (2) a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) benchmark with gated scoring isolating retrieval contributions to parameter selection; and (3) an High Performance Computing (HPC) benchmark evaluating end-to-end ML training orchestration on a SLURM cluster. Alongside the benchmark we present EngiAI, a Multi-Agent System (MAS) reference implementation built on LangGraph that operationalizes the benchmark by coordinating seven specialized agents through a supervisor architecture, unifying topology optimization, document retrieval, HPC job orchestration, and 3D printer control. Across four LLM backends and two EngiBench problems, proprietary models achieve 96-97% average task completion on Beams2D, while open-source 4B-parameter models reach 55-78%, with clear generational improvement. Conditional branching proves most challenging, with task completion dropping to 20-53% for the conditional style on Photonics2D. RAG gating confirms near-perfect retrieval-augmented scores ($\approx 1.0$) versus near-zero without retrieval, validating the evaluation design. On HPC orchestration, one model completes all pipeline steps in 100% of runs while another drops to 50%, revealing that multi-step instruction following degrades over long-running workflows.

AI.tldr: A new benchmark suite (EngiBench) evaluates LLM agents in engineering design tasks across workflow complexity, RAG effectiveness, and HPC orchestration, accompanied by EngiAI reference implementation showing proprietary models outperform open-source ones.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation frameworks for LLM agents in engineering design don't adequately address multi-agent systems combining simulation, retrieval, and manufacturing preparation, creating a need for comprehensive benchmarking.
AI.method: Created a benchmark suite with three dimensions: (1) workflow benchmark with seven prompt styles targeting different cognitive demands, (2) RAG benchmark with gated scoring to isolate retrieval contributions, and (3) HPC benchmark evaluating ML training orchestration on SLURM clusters. Also developed EngiAI, a multi-agent reference implementation with seven specialized agents coordinated through supervisor architecture.
AI.result: Proprietary models achieved 96-97% average task completion on Beams2D, while open-source 4B-parameter models reached 55-78%. Conditional branching was most challenging (20-53% completion). RAG gating showed near-perfect retrieval-augmented scores versus near-zero without retrieval. HPC orchestration varied widely between models.
AI.conclusion: The EngiBench suite effectively evaluates LLM agents in complex engineering workflows, revealing performance gaps between proprietary and open-source models, identifying conditional branching as a key challenge, and demonstrating the importance of retrieval augmentation and orchestration reliability in multi-step workflows.

title: Accurate Evaluation of Quickest Changepoint Detectors via Non-parametric Survival Analysis
summary: We propose non-parametric estimators for the average run length (ARL) and average detection delay (ADD) in quickest changepoint detection (QCD) under finite and irregular sequence lengths. Although ARL and ADD are widely used as optimality criteria in theoretical and simulation studies, their application to real-world datasets is hindered by limited and irregular sequence lengths. To address this issue, we propose non-parametric estimators for the ARL and ADD, termed KM-ARL and KM-ADD, by drawing an analogy between QCD and survival analysis to model detection probabilities under sequence truncation. We derive estimation bias bounds and prove that they are asymptotically unbiased unless extrapolation is required. Experiments on simulated and real-world datasets demonstrate their practical utility, enhancing robustness against limited and irregular sequence lengths, improving interpretability, and facilitating empirical, intuitive model selection. Our Python code is provided at https://github.com/TaikiMiyagawa/Kaplan-Meier-Average-Run-Length, offering ready-to-use implementations for practitioners.

AI.tldr: Non-parametric estimators for Average Run Length (ARL) and Average Detection Delay (ADD) in quickest changepoint detection, designed to handle finite and irregular sequence lengths by adapting survival analysis methods.
AI.motivation: ARL and ADD are standard optimality criteria in changepoint detection theory and simulations, but they're impractical for real-world datasets due to limited and irregular sequence lengths. Current methods struggle with these practical constraints, creating a gap between theoretical metrics and real-world applicability.
AI.method: Proposes KM-ARL and KM-ADD estimators by drawing analogy between quickest changepoint detection and survival analysis. Uses Kaplan-Meier approach to model detection probabilities under sequence truncation, handling censored detection times due to finite sequences.
AI.result: Derived estimation bias bounds and proved estimators are asymptotically unbiased unless extrapolation is needed. Experiments on simulated and real-world datasets show practical utility, enhanced robustness against limited/irregular sequences, improved interpretability, and facilitated empirical model selection.
AI.conclusion: The proposed non-parametric estimators bridge the gap between theoretical changepoint detection metrics and practical applications, making ARL and ADD applicable to real-world datasets with finite/irregular sequences. Open-source Python implementation makes method accessible to practitioners.

title: KAN-MLP-Mixer: A comprehensive investigation of the usage of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) for improving IMU-based Human Activity Recognition
summary: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) have demonstrated an exceptional ability to learn complex functions on clean, low-dimensional data but struggle to maintain performance on noisy and imperfect real-world datasets. In contrast, conventional multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) are far more tolerant to noise and computationally efficient. Replacing all MLP components with KANs in HAR models often degrades accuracy and computation efficiency, highlighting an open challenge: how to combine KANs' precision with MLPs' noise robustness and efficiency. To address this, we systematically explore various placements of KAN modules within deep HAR networks and propose a hybrid architecture that strategically synergizes the strengths of both paradigms, which uses a KAN-based input embedding layer, retains MLP layers for intermediate feature mixing, and introduces a specialized LarctanKAN module for final activity classification. Across eight public HAR datasets, the hybrid KAN-MLP model achieves an average macro F1 score relative improvement of 5.33\% compared pure-MLP model, significantly outperforming standalone KAN and MLP baselines. Furthermore, integrating this hybrid strategy into other state-of-the-art HAR architectures consistently boosts their performance. Our findings demonstrate that a carefully orchestrated combination of KAN, MLP, or other conventional neural components yields more robust and accurate HAR models for real-world wearable sensing environments.

AI.tldr: Hybrid KAN-MLP architecture for Human Activity Recognition achieves 5.33% average improvement in macro F1 score by strategically combining KAN's precision with MLP's noise robustness and efficiency.
AI.motivation: KANs excel at learning complex functions on clean low-dimensional data but struggle with noisy real-world datasets, while MLPs are more noise-tolerant and computationally efficient. The challenge is combining KAN's precision with MLP's robustness for better HAR performance.
AI.method: Systematically explores KAN module placements in deep HAR networks, proposing a hybrid architecture with KAN-based input embedding layer, MLP layers for intermediate feature mixing, and specialized LarctanKAN module for final activity classification.
AI.result: Across eight public HAR datasets, the hybrid KAN-MLP model achieves 5.33% average relative improvement in macro F1 score compared to pure-MLP models, outperforming both standalone KAN and MLP baselines. The hybrid strategy also boosts performance when integrated into other state-of-the-art HAR architectures.
AI.conclusion: Carefully orchestrated combinations of KAN, MLP, and other neural components yield more robust and accurate HAR models for real-world wearable sensing environments, demonstrating the value of hybrid architectures over pure approaches.

title: Memory-Augmented Reinforcement Learning Agent for CAD Generation
comment: 26 pages; multilingual submission: English version first, followed by Chinese version
summary: Automatic generation of computer-aided design (CAD) models is a core technology for enabling intelligence in advanced manufacturing. Existing generation methods based on large language models (LLMs) often fall short when handling complex CAD models characterized by long operation sequences, diverse operation types, and strong geometric constraints, primarily because reasoning chains break and effective error-correction mechanisms are lacking. To address this problem, this paper proposes a memory-augmented reinforcement learning framework for CAD generation agents. The framework encapsulates the underlying geometric kernel into a structured toolchain callable by the agent and builds a closed-loop mechanism of design intent understanding, global planning, execution, and multi-dimensional verification. It also designs a dual-track memory module consisting of a case library and a skill library, and proposes a dynamic utility retrieval algorithm. By introducing reinforcement learning into retrieval and policy optimization, the agent can effectively avoid retrieval traps in which examples are semantically similar but geometrically infeasible, enabling online self-correction and continual evolution without additional large-scale annotated data. Experiments show that the proposed method significantly improves both the success rate and geometric consistency on complex CAD model generation tasks.

AI.tldr: Memory-augmented RL framework for CAD generation that uses structured toolchain and dual-track memory with dynamic retrieval to handle complex CAD models better than LLM-based methods.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based CAD generation methods struggle with complex models having long operation sequences, diverse operations, and strong geometric constraints due to broken reasoning chains and lack of effective error-correction mechanisms.
AI.method: Memory-augmented reinforcement learning framework that encapsulates geometric kernel into structured toolchain, builds closed-loop mechanism (design intent understanding, planning, execution, verification), and uses dual-track memory (case library + skill library) with dynamic utility retrieval algorithm and RL-based policy optimization.
AI.result: Experiments show significant improvement in both success rate and geometric consistency on complex CAD model generation tasks compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables effective avoidance of retrieval traps (semantically similar but geometrically infeasible examples), online self-correction, and continual evolution without requiring additional large-scale annotated data.

title: ReCrit: Transition-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Scientific Critic Reasoning
summary: Large language models can fail in critic interaction not only by answering incorrectly, but also by abandoning an initially correct scientific solution after user criticism. This is especially risky in scientific reasoning, where user criticism can turn a valid answer into an incorrect one. We frame critic interaction as an inter-turn correctness-transition problem rather than a final-answer accuracy problem, and identify three challenges: transition awareness, decoupling useful correction from harmful sycophancy, and scalable rollout. We propose ReCrit, a transition-aware reinforcement learning framework that decomposes Initial-to-Critic behavior into four quadrants: Correction, Sycophancy, Robustness, and Boundary. ReCrit rewards correction and robustness, penalizes sycophancy, and treats persistent errors as weak boundary signals. To make interaction training practical, ReCrit further uses dynamic asynchronous rollout with tail-adaptive completion to reduce rollout waiting. On three scientific reasoning benchmarks, ChemBench, TRQA, and EarthSE, ReCrit improves average Critic accuracy from 38.15 to 51.49 on Qwen3.5-4B and from 45.40 to 55.59 on Qwen3.5-9B. Ablations show that final-answer rewards provide little interaction-level gain, while transition-aware rewards and quadrant weighting produce more distinguishable training signals and larger net Critic-stage improvement. The code is available at https://github.com/black-yt/ReCrit .

AI.tldr: ReCrit is a reinforcement learning framework that improves LLMs' ability to maintain correct scientific solutions during critic interaction by focusing on correctness transitions between turns rather than just final answers.
AI.motivation: Large language models often abandon initially correct scientific solutions after user criticism, turning valid answers into incorrect ones. Current approaches focus on final-answer accuracy but fail to address the dynamic nature of critic interactions where correctness can change between turns.
AI.method: ReCrit frames critic interaction as an inter-turn correctness-transition problem and decomposes Initial-to-Critic behavior into four quadrants: Correction (good change), Sycophancy (bad change), Robustness (staying correct), and Boundary (staying incorrect). The method uses transition-aware reinforcement learning with quadrant-specific rewards/penalties and introduces dynamic asynchronous rollout with tail-adaptive completion for efficient training.
AI.result: On scientific reasoning benchmarks (ChemBench, TRQA, EarthSE), ReCrit improved Critic accuracy from 38.15 to 51.49 on Qwen3.5-4B and from 45.40 to 55.59 on Qwen3.5-9B. Ablation studies show transition-aware rewards and quadrant weighting provide more distinguishable training signals than final-answer rewards.
AI.conclusion: Critic interaction should be treated as a dynamic correctness-transition problem rather than static final-answer evaluation. ReCrit's quadrant decomposition and transition-aware reinforcement learning effectively improve LLMs' ability to maintain scientific correctness during critic interactions while distinguishing useful corrections from harmful sycophancy.

title: Trustworthy Agent Network: Trust in Agent Networks Must Be Baked In, Not Bolted On
comment: Accepted by SIGKDD 2026 Blue Sky Ideas Track
summary: The rapid advancement of Large Language Models has given rise to autonomous LLM-based agents capable of complex reasoning and execution. As these agents transition from isolated operation to collaborative ecosystems, we witness the emergence of the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) network, a paradigm where heterogeneous agents autonomously coordinate to solve multi-step tasks. While these networks may offer better task performance compared to simply using one agent to complete the entire task, they introduce systemic vulnerabilities, such as adversarial composition, semantic misalignment, and cascading operational failures, that existing agent alignment techniques cannot address. In this vision paper, we argue that the trustworthiness of A2A networks cannot be fully guaranteed via retrofitting on existing protocols that are largely designed for individual agents. Rather, it must be architected from the very beginning of the A2A coordination framework. We present a comprehensive conceptual framework that situates trust in A2A systems through four design pillars.

AI.tldr: A2A networks need trust-by-design architecture, not retrofitted solutions, to address systemic vulnerabilities in collaborative LLM agents.
AI.motivation: As autonomous LLM agents shift from isolated operation to collaborative ecosystems (Agent-to-Agent networks), they introduce systemic vulnerabilities like adversarial composition, semantic misalignment, and cascading failures that existing agent alignment techniques cannot address, creating trustworthiness concerns.
AI.method: Proposes architecting trust from the beginning of A2A coordination frameworks through a comprehensive conceptual framework with four design pillars (though the specific pillars aren't detailed in the abstract).
AI.result: Presents a vision for trust-by-design A2A networks, arguing that trustworthiness cannot be fully guaranteed by retrofitting existing protocols designed for individual agents.
AI.conclusion: Trust in A2A systems must be architected from the ground up in coordination frameworks through systematic design principles rather than relying on solutions retrofitted from individual agent protocols.

title: When Skills Don't Help: A Negative Result on Procedural Knowledge for Tool-Grounded Agents in Offensive Cybersecurity
comment: Accepted as a poster at ACM CAIS 2026 AgentSkills Workshop
summary: Agent Skills, structured packages of procedural knowledge loaded into an LLM agent at inference time, are widely reported to improve task pass rates by an average of 16.2~percentage points across diverse domains. Yet the same benchmarks show wide variance, with 16 of 84 tasks suffering negative deltas when Skills are introduced. The community has not yet articulated a clean mechanism for \emph{when} Skills help and when they are merely redundant overhead. We re-analyze a recently published 180-run controlled study of an MCP-grounded autonomous Capture-the-Flag (CTF) agent under four documentation conditions of increasing richness (55, 1{,}478, 1{,}976, and 4{,}147 lines), and show that these conditions correspond almost exactly to a No-Skills, Experiential-Skills, Curated-Skills, and Comprehensive-Skills ablation. In offensive cybersecurity, a domain not deeply covered by existing Skills benchmarks, the marginal benefit of Skills collapses. The spread between the no-Skills and full-Skills conditions is only 8.9~pp ($p = 0.71$, $χ^2$; $p = 0.25$, Cochran--Armitage trend test; five of six pairwise Cohen's $h$ values fall below the $0.2$ small-effect threshold). We argue that the missing variable is \emph{environment-feedback bandwidth}. When an agent's tool layer returns strict, schema-validated, low-latency observations, the environment itself supplies the procedural correction signal that Skills are normally needed to provide. As a result, the marginal benefit of curated Skills diminishes substantially, and, in some cases (e.g., our timing side-channel setting), actively degrades performance. We articulate a falsifiable hypothesis, sketch its design implications for compound AI systems, and will release the reanalysis pipeline to support replication.

AI.tldr: Agent Skills improve task performance in LLM agents but show inconsistent results; in environments with rich real-time feedback, Skills provide minimal benefit and can sometimes degrade performance.
AI.motivation: The research aims to understand when Agent Skills (structured procedural knowledge) actually help LLM agents versus when they're redundant overhead, given inconsistent results showing 16 of 84 tasks suffering negative performance with Skills despite an average 16.2 percentage point improvement.
AI.method: Re-analyzed a 180-run controlled study of an MCP-grounded autonomous Capture-the-Flag (CTF) agent under four documentation conditions corresponding to No-Skills, Experiential-Skills, Curated-Skills, and Comprehensive-Skills. Used statistical tests including χ², Cochran-Armitage trend test, and Cohen's h effect sizes in offensive cybersecurity domain.
AI.result: The marginal benefit of Skills collapses in offensive cybersecurity - only 8.9 percentage point difference between no-Skills and full-Skills conditions with no statistical significance (p=0.71, χ²; p=0.25 trend test). Most effect sizes fell below small-effect threshold. Skills sometimes actively degrade performance (e.g., in timing side-channel settings).
AI.conclusion: Environment-feedback bandwidth is key: when agents receive strict, schema-validated, low-latency observations from their tools, the environment provides the procedural correction that Skills normally supply, making curated Skills minimally beneficial and sometimes harmful.

title: Theory-optimal Quantization Based on Flatness
summary: Post-training quantization has emerged as a widely adopted technique for compressing and accelerating the inference of Large Language Models (LLMs). The primary challenges in LLMs quantization stem from activation outliers, which significantly degrade model performance especially at lower bit precision. While recent approaches attempt to mitigate outliers through linear transformations across feature dimensions, our analysis reveals that the transformed weights and activations still exhibit persistent outlier patterns with concentrated magnitude distributions. In this paper, we first model the mathematical relationship between quantization error and outliers, and then introduce a new metric Flatness to quantify the distribution of outliers. Based on this, we derive the theoretical optimal solution with respect to Flatness. Building on these insights, we propose Bidirectional Diagonal Quantization (BDQ), a novel post-training quantization framework that effectively disperses outlier patterns through optimized matrix transformations. BDQ strategically distributes outlier magnitudes across matrix dimensions via learned diagonal operations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BDQ establishes a new quantization benchmark. It achieves less than 1\% accuracy drop in W4A4 quantization on the LLaMA-3-8B model. In the more challenging W2A4KV16 experiment, compared to state-of-the-art approaches, BDQ reduces the performance gap by 39.1\% on the DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-70B model.

AI.tldr: BDQ is a new post-training quantization method that uses optimized diagonal matrix transformations to disperse activation outliers, achieving state-of-the-art results for low-bit LLM quantization.
AI.motivation: Current post-training quantization methods for LLMs struggle with activation outliers that severely degrade performance at lower bit precision. Existing linear transformation approaches fail to fully address persistent outlier patterns with concentrated magnitude distributions.
AI.method: 1) Modeled mathematical relationship between quantization error and outliers, 2) Introduced "Flatness" metric to quantify outlier distribution, 3) Derived theoretical optimal solution for Flatness, 4) Proposed Bidirectional Diagonal Quantization (BDQ) framework using learned diagonal operations to strategically distribute outlier magnitudes across matrix dimensions.
AI.result: BDQ achieves <1% accuracy drop in W4A4 quantization on LLaMA-3-8B. In the challenging W2A4KV16 experiment on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-70B, BDQ reduces the performance gap by 39.1% compared to state-of-the-art approaches, establishing a new quantization benchmark.
AI.conclusion: BDQ effectively addresses the outlier problem in LLM quantization through optimized diagonal matrix transformations, providing a theoretically grounded and practically effective solution for low-bit quantization that significantly outperforms existing methods.

title: Interference-Aware Multi-Task Unlearning
summary: Machine unlearning aims to remove the contribution of designated training data from a trained model while preserving performance on the remaining data. Existing work mainly focuses on single-task settings, whereas modern models often operate in multi-task setups with shared backbones, where removing supervision for one task or instance can unintentionally affect others. We introduce multi-task unlearning with two settings: full-task unlearning, which removes a target instance from all tasks, and partial-task unlearning, which removes supervision only from selected tasks. We show that shared parameters couple the forget and retain sets, causing task-level interference on non-target tasks and instance-level interference on other instances.
