To address this issue, we propose an interference-aware framework that combines task-aware gradient projection, which constrains updates within task-specific subspaces, with instance-level gradient orthogonalization, which reduces conflicts between forget and retain signals. Experiments on two multi-task computer vision benchmarks across five tasks show that our method achieves effective unlearning while maintaining strong generalization, reducing UIS compared with the strongest baseline by 30.3% in full-task unlearning and 52.9% in partial-task unlearning.

AI.tldr: A novel multi-task unlearning framework that removes data from specific tasks in shared-backbone models while minimizing interference on other tasks, achieving significant improvements over baselines.
AI.motivation: Modern models often use shared backbones for multi-task learning, but current unlearning methods focus only on single-task settings. Removing supervision for one task or instance in multi-task setups can unintentionally affect performance on other tasks due to parameter coupling.
AI.method: Proposes an interference-aware framework with two key components: 1) task-aware gradient projection to constrain updates within task-specific subspaces, and 2) instance-level gradient orthogonalization to reduce conflicts between forget and retain signals. Handles both full-task unlearning (removing instances from all tasks) and partial-task unlearning (removing supervision only from selected tasks).
AI.result: Experiments on two multi-task computer vision benchmarks across five tasks show the method achieves effective unlearning while maintaining strong generalization. Reduces UIS (unlearning interference score) by 30.3% in full-task unlearning and 52.9% in partial-task unlearning compared to the strongest baseline.
AI.conclusion: The proposed interference-aware framework successfully addresses the challenge of multi-task unlearning in shared-backbone models by mitigating both task-level and instance-level interference, enabling selective data removal while preserving performance on non-target tasks.

title: PROWL: Prioritized Regret-Driven Optimization for World Model Learning
summary: Modern action-conditioned video world models achieve strong short-horizon visual realism, yet remain unreliable on rare, interaction-critical transitions that dominate downstream planning and policy performance. Because passive demonstration data systematically under-samples these high-impact regimes, improving robustness requires actively eliciting model failures rather than relying on their natural occurrence. We introduce a KL-constrained adversarial curriculum in which a policy is trained to expose high-error trajectories of a diffusion-based world model while remaining close to the behavior distribution. The world model is continuously fine-tuned on these adversarially discovered trajectories, yielding an adversarial training loop that converts rare failures into a stable, near-distribution training signal without drifting into out-of-distribution exploitation. To maintain pressure on unresolved weaknesses as the model improves, we propose a Prioritized Adversarial Trajectory (PAT) buffer that re-ranks trajectories based on prediction error, action fidelity, and learning progress, focusing training on unresolved failure modes rather than repeatedly revisiting solved cases. We implement our approach in the MineRL framework and evaluate it on held-out out-of-distribution trajectories; PROWL improves robustness over models trained on passive data alone, reveals reward-hacking behaviors under weak behavioral constraints, and demonstrates that effective adversarial world-model training critically depends on balancing exploratory failure discovery with explicit behavioral regularization. Our results suggest that scalable world models benefit not only from larger datasets, but also from selectively generating informative training data.

AI.tldr: PROWL introduces a KL-constrained adversarial curriculum where a policy exposes high-error trajectories of a diffusion-based world model while staying close to behavior distribution, with prioritized adversarial trajectory buffering to focus on unresolved failure modes.
AI.motivation: Modern action-conditioned video world models have strong short-horizon visual realism but remain unreliable on rare, interaction-critical transitions that dominate downstream planning and policy performance. Passive demonstration data systematically under-samples these high-impact regimes, requiring active elicitation of model failures rather than relying on their natural occurrence.
AI.method: Introduces a KL-constrained adversarial curriculum: a policy is trained to expose high-error trajectories of a diffusion-based world model while remaining close to the behavior distribution. The world model is continuously fine-tuned on these adversarially discovered trajectories. Uses a Prioritized Adversarial Trajectory (PAT) buffer that re-ranks trajectories based on prediction error, action fidelity, and learning progress to focus training on unresolved failure modes. Implemented in the MineRL framework.
AI.result: PROWL improves robustness over models trained on passive data alone when evaluated on held-out out-of-distribution trajectories. Reveals reward-hacking behaviors under weak behavioral constraints. Demonstrates that effective adversarial world-model training critically depends on balancing exploratory failure discovery with explicit behavioral regularization.
AI.conclusion: Scalable world models benefit not only from larger datasets, but also from selectively generating informative training data through adversarial training loops that convert rare failures into stable, near-distribution training signals without drifting into out-of-distribution exploitation.

title: Embedding by Elicitation: Dynamic Representations for Bayesian Optimization of System Prompts
summary: System prompts are a central control mechanism in modern AI systems, shaping behavior across conversations, tasks, and user populations. Yet they are difficult to tune when feedback is available only as aggregate metrics rather than per-example labels, failures, or critiques. We study this aggregate feedback setting as sample-constrained black-box optimization over discrete, variable-length text. We introduce ReElicit, a Bayesian optimization framework based on \emph{embedding by elicitation}. Given a task description, previously evaluated prompts, and scalar scores, an LLM elicits a compact, interpretable feature space and maps prompts into it. Leveraging a probabilistic Gaussian process surrogate, an acquisition function then selects target feature vectors, which the LLM realizes and refines into deployable system prompts. Re-eliciting the feature space as new evaluations arrive lets the representation adapt to the observed prompt-score history. We evaluate the setting using offline benchmark accuracy as a controlled aggregate proxy: the optimizer observes one scalar score per prompt and no per-example labels, errors, or critiques. Across ten system prompt optimization tasks with a 30 total evaluation budget, ReElicit achieves the strongest aggregate performance profile among representative aggregate-only prompt-optimization baselines. These results suggest that LLMs can serve as adaptive semantic representation builders, not only prompt generators, for Bayesian optimization over natural-language artifacts.

AI.tldr: ReElicit uses LLMs to create adaptive feature spaces for Bayesian optimization of system prompts using only aggregate feedback, achieving strong performance with limited evaluations.
AI.motivation: System prompts are crucial for controlling AI behavior but difficult to optimize when only aggregate feedback metrics are available (no per-example labels or critiques).
AI.method: Bayesian optimization framework where an LLM elicits interpretable feature spaces, maps prompts into them, uses Gaussian process surrogate models, selects target features, and refines them into deployable prompts, with adaptive re-elicitation as new data arrives.
AI.result: Across ten system prompt optimization tasks with 30 evaluation budget, ReElicit achieves strongest aggregate performance among representative aggregate-only prompt-optimization baselines.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can serve as adaptive semantic representation builders for Bayesian optimization over natural-language artifacts, not just as prompt generators.

title: Adaptive Multi-Scale Goodness Aggregation for Forward-Forward Learning
summary: We propose Adaptive Multi-Scale Goodness Aggregation (AMSGA), a novel extension of the Forward-Forward (FF) algorithm designed to improve stability, robustness, and generalization in local-learning neural networks. AMSGA addresses several limitations of the original FF framework by introducing multi-scale goodness aggregation across local, intermediate, and global representations; adaptive curriculum-guided hard negative mining; layer-dependent adaptive thresholds; and a warm-up cosine annealing learning-rate schedule for improved optimization stability. Together, these modifications strengthen the FF paradigm while preserving its biologically plausible and memory-efficient properties. Experiments on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST demonstrate consistent performance improvements over the baseline FF algorithm, achieving up to +1.45% improvement on MNIST and +1.50% improvement on Fashion-MNIST without significant computational overhead. Our results suggest that local learning methods can become substantially more competitive when goodness estimation and training dynamics are carefully designed.

AI.tldr: AMSGA extends the Forward-Forward algorithm with multi-scale goodness aggregation, adaptive training techniques, and improved optimization to enhance stability and performance in local-learning neural networks.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address limitations in the original Forward-Forward (FF) algorithm, specifically its stability, robustness, and generalization issues in local-learning neural networks, while preserving FF's biologically plausible and memory-efficient properties.
AI.method: The authors propose Adaptive Multi-Scale Goodness Aggregation (AMSGA) which introduces: 1) multi-scale goodness aggregation across local, intermediate, and global representations, 2) adaptive curriculum-guided hard negative mining, 3) layer-dependent adaptive thresholds, and 4) a warm-up cosine annealing learning-rate schedule for optimization stability.
AI.result: Experiments on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST show consistent improvements over baseline FF, achieving up to +1.45% improvement on MNIST and +1.50% improvement on Fashion-MNIST without significant computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: Local learning methods can become substantially more competitive when goodness estimation and training dynamics are carefully designed, and AMSGA successfully strengthens the FF paradigm while maintaining its biologically plausible and memory-efficient advantages.

title: Block-Based Double Decoders
summary: Encoder-decoder models offer substantial inference-time savings over decoder-only models, but their pretraining objectives suffer from sparse supervision and dynamic sequence lengths, keeping them out of practice at scale. We propose block-based double decoders, a novel transformer architecture that utilizes doubly-causal block-based attention masks to train with full loss supervision and static sequence packing, combining decoder-only training efficiency with encoder-decoder inference efficiency. In scaling law experiments, block-based double decoders strongly outperform encoder-decoders and closely track decoder-only models across scales. At inference time, they cut KV-cache memory and per-token compute by at least 2/3 without sacrificing prefill caching or other existing inference optimizations available to decoder-only models.

AI.tldr: Block-based double decoders combine decoder-only training efficiency with encoder-decoder inference efficiency using doubly-causal block-based attention masks, outperforming encoder-decoders and closely tracking decoder-only models while reducing KV-cache memory and compute by at least 2/3.
AI.motivation: Encoder-decoder models have inference-time advantages over decoder-only models, but their pretraining objectives suffer from sparse supervision and dynamic sequence lengths, preventing widespread adoption at scale. There's a need to combine the training efficiency of decoder-only models with the inference efficiency of encoder-decoder architectures.
AI.method: Propose block-based double decoders, a novel transformer architecture using doubly-causal block-based attention masks that enable full loss supervision and static sequence packing during training, while maintaining inference efficiency advantages.
AI.result: In scaling law experiments, block-based double decoders strongly outperform encoder-decoders and closely track decoder-only models across different scales. During inference, they reduce KV-cache memory and per-token compute by at least 2/3 while preserving prefill caching and other decoder-only optimization benefits.
AI.conclusion: Block-based double decoders successfully combine the best of both worlds: decoder-like training efficiency with encoder-decoder inference efficiency, offering a practical solution for scalable language modeling with substantial inference-time savings.

title: POLAR-Bench: A Diagnostic Benchmark for Privacy-Utility Trade-offs in LLM Agents
summary: LLM agents increasingly have access to private user data and act on the user's behalf when interacting with third-party systems. The user defines what may and must not be shared, and the agent must robustly follow that intent even when third-party systems behave adversarially. We introduce POLAR-Bench (Policy-aware adversarial Benchmark), in which a trusted model with a privacy policy and a task converses with a third-party model that adversarially probes for both task-relevant and protected attributes. Across 10 domains and 7,852 samples, we score privacy and utility by deterministic set-membership and vary privacy policy dimension and attack strategy along two orthogonal axes, producing a 5 times 5 diagnostic surface per model. Our results reveal a sharp split: current frontier models withhold over 99% of protected attributes, while smaller open-weight models in the 1--30B range, the class users most commonly run as their own trusted agent on-device or via private inference, score notably worse, with the weakest leaking over half. POLAR-Bench thus localizes where each model's intent-following breaks down, providing a foothold for privacy alignment where it matters most.

AI.tldr: POLAR-Bench is a benchmark for evaluating LLM agents' ability to follow user privacy policies when interacting with adversarial third-party systems, revealing that frontier models protect privacy well but smaller open-weight models leak protected information.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents handle private user data and interact with third-party systems, they must robustly follow user-defined privacy policies even when faced with adversarial probing. There's a need to systematically evaluate models' intent-following capabilities for privacy protection.
AI.method: Created POLAR-Bench (Policy-aware adversarial Benchmark) with 10 domains and 7,852 samples. It involves a trusted model (with privacy policy) conversing with a third-party model that adversarially probes for both task-relevant and protected attributes. Scored privacy and utility via deterministic set-membership, varying privacy policy dimension and attack strategy across orthogonal axes.
AI.result: Frontier models withheld over 99% of protected attributes, while smaller open-weight models (1-30B range) performed notably worse, with the weakest leaking over half of protected attributes. The benchmark produces a 5×5 diagnostic surface per model to localize where intent-following breaks down.
AI.conclusion: POLAR-Bench provides a crucial diagnostic tool for evaluating and improving LLM agents' privacy alignment, particularly important for smaller models that users commonly deploy as trusted agents on-device or via private inference.

title: Compositional Literary Primitives in Instruction-Tuned LLMs: Cross-Architectural SAE Features for Self, Style, and Affect
summary: We characterize a compositional architecture of literary primitives in two instruction-tuned large language models (Llama 3.1 8B-Instruct and Gemma 2 9B-IT) via sparse autoencoders on mid-depth residual streams. Four feature classes emerge: naming-gates that promote lexical tokens of a target affect, an eleven-self cluster of first-person register features, stylistic register modulators (show-don't-tell and defamiliarization), and compositional emotions that arise only from multi-feature steering. Under a forced-choice 5-LLM judge panel applied to a 27-category emotion taxonomy (Cowen-Keltner), Llama reaches full 27/27 coverage by combining naming-gates, multi-feature recipes, and single self-feature steering; Gemma reaches 23/27 with adoration as the single residual strict-fail. Under random judging, the per-cell pass probability is on the order of $10^{-3}$ and the expected number of two-seed false-positive cells across the catalog is negligible, so the observed coverage is not consistent with chance. A cross-architectural asymmetry sits in the strict-versus-soft judge contrast: on the same generations, judges agree more often on Llama outputs than on Gemma outputs because Llama outputs name the target affect more directly while Gemma outputs evoke it through scene and imagery. Both architectures contain self-features that serve simultaneously as register markers and as emotion emitters, including a single most-RLHF-loaded self-feature per architecture that intensifies the institutional Helper-AI persona at one operating regime and produces affect-categorizable output at the same calibrated coefficient. Methodologically, the paper presents a three-stage validation pipeline (logit-lens, LLM-rate, 5-LLM judge) with documented anti-patterns; the total compute is single-GPU and about 15 minutes per emotion-feature discovery cycle.

AI.tldr: This paper discovers compositional literary primitives in instruction-tuned LLMs (Llama 3.1 and Gemma 2) using sparse autoencoders, revealing four feature classes that can be steered to generate specific emotional content with high coverage of a 27-category emotion taxonomy.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand the compositional architecture of literary primitives in instruction-tuned LLMs and develop methods for discovering and validating emotional content generation capabilities through feature steering.
AI.method: Used sparse autoencoders on mid-depth residual streams of two instruction-tuned LLMs (Llama 3.1 8B-Instruct and Gemma 2 9B-IT). Implemented a three-stage validation pipeline: 1) logit-lens, 2) LLM-rate, and 3) 5-LLM judge panel for forced-choice categorization using the Cowen-Keltner 27-category emotion taxonomy.
AI.result: Discovered four feature classes: naming-gates, eleven-self cluster, stylistic register modulators, and compositional emotions. Llama achieved full 27/27 emotion coverage while Gemma reached 23/27. Found cross-architectural asymmetry where judges agreed more on Llama outputs. Identified self-features that serve dual roles as register markers and emotion emitters, including a single most-RLHF-loaded self-feature per architecture.
AI.conclusion: Instruction-tuned LLMs contain discoverable compositional literary primitives that can be steered for emotional content generation. The paper provides an efficient single-GPU methodology for emotion-feature discovery with documented anti-patterns, revealing architectural differences in how LLMs express emotions through naming versus scene-evocation strategies.

title: Learning to Hand Off: Provably Convergent Workflow Learning under Interface Constraints
summary: We study workflow learning in a setting where specialized agents hand off control through a shared artifact, each agent observes only a local function of that artifact and its own private state, and no centralized learner accesses joint trajectories -- the operating regime of multi-agent LLM pipelines that span organizational, vendor, or trust boundaries. We formalize this regime as an interface-constrained semi-Markov decision process (IC-SMDP), whose decision epochs occur at handoff times, and design IC-$Q$, an asynchronous decentralized $Q$-learning algorithm in which cross-agent coordination at every handoff is exactly one scalar. Our main result is a finite-sample bound for neural IC-$Q$ that decomposes into three independently controllable error sources: neural function-approximation error, interface representation gap, and a mixing-time residual, under the random option-duration discount. Establishing this bound requires lifting the approximate information state (AIS) framework from single-agent primitive-step MDPs to multi-agent SMDPs and controlling Markovian noise under random duration, neither of which has been done in prior work. To our knowledge this is the first finite-sample guarantee for neural $Q$-learning under decentralized partial observability. Four experiments: a controlled synthetic IC-SMDP that validates the bound term-by-term, multi-LLM mathematical reasoning, multi-agent routing, and multi-agent CPU programming, show that IC-$Q$ matches a centralized oracle without any agent observing joint trajectories, with each of the three error sources scaling along its corresponding axis as the bound predicts.

AI.tldr: IC-Q is a decentralized Q-learning algorithm for multi-agent LLM pipelines where agents hand off control through shared artifacts, with finite-sample guarantees for neural Q-learning under decentralized partial observability.
AI.motivation: Real-world workflow learning in multi-agent LLM pipelines operates under constraints where specialized agents work through handoffs, each agent only observes local functions and private states, and no centralized learner accesses joint trajectories due to organizational, vendor, or trust boundaries.
AI.method: Formalized as Interface-Constrained Semi-Markov Decision Process (IC-SMDP) with decision epochs at handoff times. Developed IC-Q, an asynchronous decentralized Q-learning algorithm where cross-agent coordination at each handoff uses exactly one scalar. Extended Approximate Information State (AIS) framework from single-agent MDPs to multi-agent SMDPs.
AI.result: Proved finite-sample bound for neural IC-Q that decomposes into three independently controllable error sources: neural function-approximation error, interface representation gap, and mixing-time residual. IC-Q matches centralized oracle performance without requiring agents to observe joint trajectories.
AI.conclusion: IC-Q provides the first finite-sample guarantee for neural Q-learning under decentralized partial observability, enabling effective multi-agent LLM pipelines across organizational boundaries while maintaining theoretical guarantees.

title: Metric-Gradient Projection for Stable Multi-Agent Policy Learning
summary: General-sum multi-agent learning is often governed by a stacked update field in which each agent's policy update changes the optimization landscape faced by the others. This coupling can entangle an integrable component of collective improvement with cyclic interaction dynamics, leading to slow or unstable multi-agent learning. Existing approaches, such as regularization, credit assignment, and consensus methods, stabilize MARL through local or algorithmic modifications; HPML complements them by projecting the joint update field onto a metric-gradient component. We introduce \textbf{HPML} (\textbf{H}odge-\textbf{P}rojected \textbf{M}ulti-agent \textbf{L}earning), which views the joint update field of a multi-agent system as an element of an $L^2$ space of vector fields and computes a Hodge-type projection onto the closest metric-gradient potential flow. HPML follows the projected component as the update direction, yielding the closest metric-gradient field under the chosen metric and sampling measure. The projection is defined variationally, characterized by a Poisson-type equation, and implemented through graph-based and amortized neural realizations that recover projected directions from samples. We show that the projected dynamics admit a Lyapunov potential and yield equilibrium-gap bounds with an explicit additive non-potentiality term. Controlled experiments validate the geometric mechanism, and CTDE benchmarks show improved stability and normalized return when HPML is used as a plug-in projection layer in MARL pipelines.

AI.tldr: HPML is a Hodge-projected multi-agent learning method that stabilizes MARL by projecting joint policy updates onto metric-gradient flows, separating beneficial collective improvement from cyclic interactions.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent learning in general-sum games suffers from entangled dynamics where beneficial collective improvement gets mixed with cyclic interaction patterns, leading to slow or unstable learning. Existing approaches use local modifications, but they don't directly address the geometric structure of the joint update field.
AI.method: HPML treats the joint update field as an element of an L² space of vector fields and computes a Hodge-type projection onto the closest metric-gradient potential flow. This is implemented through graph-based and amortized neural realizations that recover projected directions from samples, with the projection defined variationally via a Poisson-type equation.
AI.result: The projected dynamics admit a Lyapunov potential and yield equilibrium-gap bounds with explicit additive non-potentiality terms. Experiments show HPML improves stability and normalized return when used as a plug-in projection layer in MARL pipelines, validating the geometric stabilization mechanism.
AI.conclusion: HPML provides a geometric approach to stabilize multi-agent learning by disentangling integrable improvement components from cyclic dynamics through Hodge projection, complementing existing MARL stabilization methods with a principled geometric foundation.

title: Progressive Autonomy as Preference Learning: A Formalization of Trust Calibration for Agentic Tool Use
summary: We formalize trust calibration for agentic tool use (deciding when an automated agent's proposed action may execute autonomously versus require human approval) as a preference-learning problem. A policy gateway maintains a Gaussian-process posterior over a latent human risk-tolerance function, observed through a probit likelihood on binary approve/deny feedback, and escalates to the human exactly where the approval outcome is most uncertain. We show this is structurally an instance of Preferential Bayesian Optimization, inheriting its inference machinery (approximate Gaussian-process classification) and its sample-efficiency argument (uncertainty-targeted querying), while differing in objective: classifying an action space into allow/block/ask regions rather than optimizing a design.

AI.tldr: Formalizes trust calibration for agentic tool use as a preference-learning problem using Gaussian processes and Bayesian optimization to decide when agents need human approval.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the problem of calibrating trust in automated agents - determining when an agent's proposed action can execute autonomously versus when it requires human approval. This is a critical challenge for safe human-agent collaboration in real-world applications.
AI.method: Formalizes trust calibration as a preference-learning problem. Uses a policy gateway that maintains a Gaussian-process posterior over a latent human risk-tolerance function, observed through probit likelihood on binary approve/deny feedback. Escalates to humans exactly where approval outcome is most uncertain (uncertainty-targeted querying).
AI.result: The approach is shown to be structurally an instance of Preferential Bayesian Optimization, inheriting its inference machinery (approximate Gaussian-process classification) and sample-efficiency argument (uncertainty-targeted querying). However, it differs in objective: classifying an action space into allow/block/ask regions rather than optimizing a design.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a formal preference-learning framework for trust calibration in agentic tool use, leveraging Bayesian optimization techniques to efficiently learn when automated agents need human approval while minimizing human intervention.

title: D-PACE: Dynamic Position-Aware Cross-Entropy for Parallel Speculative Drafting
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference by having a small drafter propose tokens that a larger target model verifies in parallel. Recent diffusion-based parallel drafters such as DFlash predict the full B-token block in one forward pass, enabling deeper drafters and longer accepted blocks. However, existing multi-token drafter objectives often use fixed position-dependent weighting schedules, such as head-dependent weights or block-position decays, which do not adapt as the positions limiting acceptance change during training. To address this, we derive per-position training weights from a differentiable surrogate of expected accepted draft length, matching the weight of each position to its log-probability gradient contribution. The resulting loss, D-PACE (Dynamic Position-Aware Cross-Entropy), shifts training signal toward positions that currently limit acceptance as the drafter improves. Across six benchmarks, two Qwen3-4B draft depths, two decoding temperatures, and two additional target models, D-PACE consistently improves both wall-clock speedup and average emitted length, with 2.3\% measured training-time overhead and no changes to the drafter architecture or inference procedure.

AI.tldr: D-PACE is a novel training objective for speculative decoding drafters that dynamically adjusts per-position weights based on gradient contributions to expected accepted length, improving inference speed without architectural changes.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-token drafter objectives use fixed position-dependent weighting schedules that don't adapt as the positions limiting acceptance change during training, creating a mismatch between training objectives and actual inference performance.
AI.method: The authors derive per-position training weights from a differentiable surrogate of expected accepted draft length, matching each position's weight to its log-probability gradient contribution. This creates D-PACE (Dynamic Position-Aware Cross-Entropy) loss that shifts training signal toward positions currently limiting acceptance.
AI.result: Across six benchmarks, two Qwen3-4B draft depths, two decoding temperatures, and two additional target models, D-PACE consistently improves both wall-clock speedup and average emitted length, with only 2.3% training-time overhead.
AI.conclusion: D-PACE provides an effective training objective that adapts to the drafter's current performance, focusing training on the bottleneck positions that actually limit acceptance during inference, leading to improved speculative decoding performance without architectural changes.

title: PASC: Pipeline-Aware Conformal Prediction with Joint Coverage Guarantees for Multi-Stage NLP and LLM Pipelines
summary: Modern NLP and LLM systems are pipelines: named entity recognition (NER) -> entity disambiguation (NED) -> entity typing, retrieval-augmented generation (retriever -> reader), and agentic chains of planner -> tool -> critic. Errors compound across stages, but existing uncertainty quantification methods either calibrate each stage independently (no joint coverage) or apply a Bonferroni union bound (joint coverage, but conservative). We present PASC (Pipeline-Aware Split Conformal), which reduces multi-stage joint coverage to a single scalar conformal prediction problem on the joint maximum nonconformity score. PASC provides a finite-sample distribution-free guarantee that all K stages are simultaneously covered with probability at least 1 - alpha, and is nearly tight up to a 1/(n+1) factor. On a three-stage NER -> NED -> entity-typing pipeline over CoNLL-2003, PASC achieves 96.4% end-to-end coverage versus 93.4% for Bonferroni and 86.5% for independent CP, at identical average prediction set size (1.083). Under distribution shift to WNUT-17 Twitter and WikiNEuRal Wikipedia data, PASC empirically maintains the target coverage in the tested shift settings while independent CP collapses to 59%. PASC requires a single quantile computation, runs 1.7x faster than Bonferroni, and scales to K = 6 stages where independent CP drops to 0.53 end-to-end coverage. The same joint-maximum-score reduction applies directly to compound LLM systems and agent pipelines.

AI.tldr: PASC (Pipeline-Aware Split Conformal) is a new uncertainty quantification method that provides finite-sample distribution-free joint coverage guarantees for multi-stage NLP/LLM pipelines by reducing multi-stage coverage to a single scalar conformal prediction problem on the joint maximum nonconformity score.
AI.motivation: Modern NLP and LLM systems are multi-stage pipelines (NER → NED → entity typing, retriever → reader, planner → tool → critic) where errors compound across stages. Existing uncertainty quantification methods either calibrate each stage independently (no joint coverage guarantee) or use conservative Bonferroni union bounds (joint coverage but inefficient). There's a need for a method that provides tight joint coverage guarantees for entire pipelines.
AI.method: PASC reduces the multi-stage joint coverage problem to a single scalar conformal prediction problem by computing the joint maximum nonconformity score across all pipeline stages. It computes a single quantile on this maximum score, providing a distribution-free guarantee that all K stages are simultaneously covered with probability at least 1 - α. The method is nearly tight up to a 1/(n+1) factor.
AI.result: On a three-stage NER → NED → entity-typing pipeline over CoNLL-2003, PASC achieves 96.4% end-to-end coverage vs 93.4% for Bonferroni and 86.5% for independent CP, with identical average prediction set size (1.083). Under distribution shift (WNUT-17 Twitter and WikiNEuRal Wikipedia data), PASC maintains target coverage while independent CP collapses to 59%. PASC runs 1.7x faster than Bonferroni and scales to K = 6 stages where independent CP drops to 0.53 end-to-end coverage.
AI.conclusion: PASC provides a theoretically sound and practically effective solution for joint uncertainty quantification in multi-stage pipelines, with significant improvements over existing methods in coverage, efficiency, and robustness to distribution shift. The joint-maximum-score reduction approach directly applies to compound LLM systems and agent pipelines.

title: Discoverable Agent Knowledge -- A Formal Framework for Agentic KG Affordances (Extended Version)
summary: Two decades ago, the Semantic Web Services community was asked how agents with different ontological commitments could discover, compose, and invoke web services coherently. The response was OWL-S and WSMO: formally grounded capability descriptions specifying what a service could do, what the agent must already know for invocation to be epistemically sound, and how ontological mismatches could be formally bridged. Current Knowledge Graph (KG) metadata standards such as VoID and DCAT describe what a KG contains yet say nothing about what a specific agent can prove from it, what closure assumptions govern empty results, or whether the agent's task vocabulary is grounded in the schema. Furthermore, in deployed KGs the governing schema DL and the operative entailment regime can diverge: an epistemic failure mode invisible to current metadata. We revisit and extend these insights for the KG setting with a four-dimensional formal framework from which we derive the Agentic Affordance Profile (AAP): a semantic layer above VoID and DCAT enabling principled KG selection, composition, and failure diagnosis at agent planning time. A five-point research agenda identifies the formal, computational, and engineering work needed to realise AAP-based affordance matching at scale.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Agentic Affordance Profiles (AAPs) as a semantic layer over current KG metadata standards (VoID/DCAT) to enable agents to reason about what they can actually prove from a knowledge graph, addressing epistemic gaps in current KG selection and composition.
AI.motivation: Current Knowledge Graph metadata standards (VoID, DCAT) only describe what data a KG contains, but fail to specify what an agent can actually prove from it, what closure assumptions govern empty results, or whether the agent's vocabulary is grounded in the schema. Additionally, there's an epistemic failure mode where the governing schema description logic and operative entailment regime can diverge in deployed KGs, which current metadata cannot detect.
AI.method: The paper revisits and extends Semantic Web Service insights (from OWL-S and WSMO) to the KG setting. It introduces a four-dimensional formal framework from which it derives the Agentic Affordance Profile (AAP) - a semantic layer above VoID and DCAT that captures what an agent can prove, what it must know for sound reasoning, and how ontological mismatches can be bridged.
AI.result: The paper presents AAP as a solution enabling principled KG selection, composition, and failure diagnosis at agent planning time. It provides a formal framework that addresses the epistemic gaps in current KG metadata standards and identifies a five-point research agenda for formal, computational, and engineering work needed to realize AAP-based affordance matching at scale.
AI.conclusion: Agentic Affordance Profiles represent a necessary semantic layer for enabling intelligent agents to make sound reasoning decisions about knowledge graphs, addressing fundamental epistemic limitations in current metadata standards and providing a path forward for scalable KG agent interoperability.

title: Composition of Memory Experts for Diffusion World Models
summary: World models aim to predict plausible futures consistent with past observations, a capability central to planning and decision-making in reinforcement learning. Yet, existing architectures face a fundamental memory trade-off: transformers preserve local detail but are bottlenecked by quadratic attention, while recurrent and state-space models scale more efficiently but compress history at the cost of fidelity. To overcome this trade-off, we suggest decoupling future-past consistency from any single architecture and instead leveraging a set of specialized experts. We introduce a diffusion-based framework that integrates heterogeneous memory models through a contrastive product-of-experts formulation. Our approach instantiates three complementary roles: a short-term memory expert that captures fine local dynamics, a long-term memory expert that stores episodic history in external diffusion weights via lightweight test-time finetuning, and a spatial long-term memory expert that enforces geometric and spatial coherence. This compositional design avoids mode collapse and scales to long contexts without incurring a quadratic cost. Across simulated and real-world benchmarks, our method improves temporal consistency, recall of past observations, and navigation performance, establishing a novel paradigm for building and operating memory-augmented diffusion world models.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based world model framework that uses specialized memory experts to overcome architectural trade-offs, achieving better temporal consistency and scalability without quadratic computational costs.
AI.motivation: Existing world model architectures face a fundamental memory trade-off: transformers preserve local detail but suffer from quadratic attention costs, while recurrent/state-space models scale efficiently but compress history at the cost of fidelity. The authors aim to overcome this trade-off.
AI.method: A diffusion-based framework that integrates heterogeneous memory models through a contrastive product-of-experts formulation. It uses three specialized experts: 1) short-term memory for local dynamics, 2) long-term memory storing episodic history in external diffusion weights via lightweight test-time finetuning, and 3) spatial long-term memory for geometric/spatial coherence.
AI.result: The compositional design avoids mode collapse and scales to long contexts without quadratic costs. Across simulated and real-world benchmarks, the method improves temporal consistency, recall of past observations, and navigation performance.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a novel paradigm for building and operating memory-augmented diffusion world models by decoupling future-past consistency from single architectures and leveraging specialized experts through a contrastive product-of-experts formulation.

title: Hallucination as Exploit: Evidence-Carrying Multimodal Agents
summary: Multimodal agents use screenshots, documents, and webpages to choose tool calls. When a false visual claim triggers a click, email, extraction, or transfer, hallucination becomes an authorization failure rather than an answer-quality error. We formalize this failure mode as hallucination-to-action conversion: an unsupported perceptual claim supplies the precondition that makes a privileged action appear permitted. We propose evidence-carrying multimodal agents (ECA), which treat free-form model text as inadmissible evidence. ECA decomposes each tool call into action-critical predicates, obtains typed certificates from constrained DOM/OCR/AX verifiers, and lets a deterministic gate grant only the privileges those certificates support. The architecture does not hide perception error; it converts opaque model belief into named verifier, schema, and implementation residuals. Verifier red-teaming over 1,900 attacks exposes this residual directly: four targeted hardening steps reduce gate bypass from 15% to 1.3%. With content-derived certificates, ECA obtains 0% unsafe-action rate on a 200-task end-to-end pipeline (Wilson 95% upper bound 2.67%) and a 120-task browser proof-of-concept (upper bound 4.3%). A direct HACR audit on 500 stratified task keys shows that unsupported action-critical claims reach unsafe execution for naive agents (100.0%) and prompt-only defense (49.6%), but not for ECA. Oracle-certificate replay on 7,488 GPT-5.4 benchmark traces serves as a gate-correctness sanity check, and neural judge baselines remain bypassable under the same threat model. The resulting principle is simple: model language may propose actions, but external evidence must authorize them.

AI.tldr: ECA (Evidence-Carrying Agents) prevents hallucination-to-action conversion in multimodal agents by requiring external evidence certificates for action-critical predicates before allowing privileged operations, reducing unsafe-action rates to near zero.
AI.motivation: Multimodal agents can trigger dangerous real-world actions (clicks, emails, transfers) based on false visual claims (hallucinations), converting perception errors into authorization failures rather than just answer-quality issues.
AI.method: ECA decomposes tool calls into action-critical predicates, obtains typed certificates from constrained verifiers (DOM/OCR/AX), and uses a deterministic gate that only grants privileges supported by these certificates. Treats free-form model text as inadmissible evidence.
AI.result: Four hardening steps reduced gate bypass from 15% to 1.3%. Achieved 0% unsafe-action rate on 200-task pipeline (95% upper bound 2.67%) and 120-task browser POC (upper bound 4.3%). Reduced unsupported action-critical claims reaching execution from 100% (naive) and 49.6% (prompt-only) to 0% for ECA.
AI.conclusion: Model language may propose actions, but external evidence must authorize them. ECA's evidence-carrying architecture converts opaque model belief into measurable verifier residuals, enabling systematic hardening against hallucination-to-action attacks.

title: How Faithful Is Trajectory-Based Data Attribution? Error Sources, Remedies, and Practical Guidelines
summary: Trajectory-based data attribution methods estimate the influence of training samples on model predictions by unrolling the training trajectory. They are widely used in applications such as data selection, data valuation, and model diagnosis, but there is a lack of comprehensive error analysis of these methods, raising concerns about method faithfulness and hindering reliable deployment. In this work, we provide the first systematic analysis of error sources in trajectory-based data attribution, together with concrete remedies to mitigate them and practical guidelines for downstream use. We organize the total error into three categories, config-level, algorithm-level, and system-level. We make three contributions. First, we identify optimizer mismatch as the dominant config-level error: existing methods derive their attribution under the assumption of SGD, even for models trained with the modern de facto optimizer AdamW. We propose AdamW-influence to fully account for AdamW's optimization dynamics, yielding improvements from 10% to over 300% in Spearman correlation between estimated and ground-truth influence across four settings spanning MLP, CNN, GPT-2, and Llama 3.2-1B. Second, we isolate the remaining algorithm-level error arising from the first-order Taylor approximation, identify the learning rate and trajectory length as factors governing the error magnitude, and derive a closed-form error proxy that can be evaluated along the original trajectory without retraining. Third, we translate these insights into practical guidelines for data selection by unifying offline and online strategies under a K-step look-ahead framework. Under this framework, online selection with a short horizon often matches or exceeds offline, and the optimal horizon can be tuned jointly with the learning rate. Together, these results turn the framework into an actionable selection recipe for practitioners.

AI.tldr: First systematic error analysis of trajectory-based data attribution methods, identifying optimizer mismatch as key issue and proposing AdamW-influence with up to 300% improvement, plus practical guidelines for data selection.
AI.motivation: Trajectory-based data attribution methods are widely used but lack comprehensive error analysis, raising concerns about faithfulness and hindering reliable deployment in applications like data selection, valuation, and model diagnosis.
AI.method: Systematic analysis organizes errors into three categories (config-level, algorithm-level, system-level). Proposes AdamW-influence to address optimizer mismatch, derives closed-form error proxy for Taylor approximation error, and introduces K-step look-ahead framework unifying offline and online data selection strategies.
AI.result: AdamW-influence improves Spearman correlation between estimated and ground-truth influence from 10% to over 300% across four settings (MLP, CNN, GPT-2, Llama 3.2-1B). Identifies learning rate and trajectory length as factors governing Taylor approximation error. Shows online selection with short horizon often matches/exceeds offline selection.
AI.conclusion: Provides first systematic error analysis of trajectory-based data attribution with concrete remedies and practical guidelines. Turns framework into actionable selection recipe for practitioners, enabling more reliable deployment of data attribution methods.

title: Not all uncertainty is alike: volatility, stochasticity, and exploration
summary: Adaptive decision-making in biological and artificial intelligence requires balancing the exploitation of known outcomes with the exploration of uncertain alternatives. Although prior work suggests that uncertainty generally promotes exploration, it has typically treated distinct sources of environmental uncertainty as equivalent. We consider environments with latent reward states that drift over time (volatility) and are observed through noisy outcomes (stochasticity). Both increase posterior uncertainty, yet we show they drive optimal exploration in opposite directions: volatility enhances it, stochasticity suppresses it. We establish this asymmetry formally by extending the Gittins index framework to Gaussian state-space bandits with latent dynamics. We further derive Cause-Aware Uncertainty-Sensitive Exploration (CAUSE), a closed-form exploration bonus obtained via control-as-inference that inherits the same monotonicities. CAUSE outperforms standard exploration strategies in environments with heterogeneous noise structure, and also improves on a Gittins-per-arm policy whose rested-bandit optimality does not transfer to restless settings. Learning and exploration are governed by the same noise-inference asymmetry, and the framework predicts that pathological noise inference produces \emph{reversed} rather than merely impaired exploration, with implications for computational accounts of psychiatric conditions.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that two sources of uncertainty (volatility and stochasticity) have opposite effects on optimal exploration: volatility enhances exploration while stochasticity suppresses it.
AI.motivation: Previous work treats different sources of environmental uncertainty as equivalent, but volatility (latent reward states that drift over time) and stochasticity (noisy outcomes) may have distinct effects on exploration despite both increasing posterior uncertainty.
AI.method: Extends the Gittins index framework to Gaussian state-space bandits with latent dynamics, and derives CAUSE (Cause-Aware Uncertainty-Sensitive Exploration), a closed-form exploration bonus obtained via control-as-inference.
AI.result: CAUSE outperforms standard exploration strategies in environments with heterogeneous noise structure, and improves on Gittins-per-arm policies in restless settings. The framework also explains how pathological noise inference produces reversed rather than just impaired exploration.
AI.conclusion: Volatility and stochasticity drive exploration in opposite directions, and this asymmetry has implications for understanding both artificial and biological decision-making, including computational accounts of psychiatric conditions.

title: DynaTrain: Fast Online Parallelism Switching for Elastic LLM Training
summary: Modern large language model (LLM) training is inherently dynamic: resource fluctuations, RLHF phase shifts, and cluster elasticity continually reshape the optimal parallelism layout, posing a significant challenge to existing training frameworks built around a static execution model. We present DynaTrain, a distributed training system for sub-second, online reconfiguration across arbitrary multi-dimensional parallelism. At its core, we propose a Virtual Parameter Space (VPS) abstraction that unifies all distributed training states under one logical coordinate space, turning any parallelism configuration into a deterministic mapping and collapsing complex transition into manageable geometric intersections. On top of VPS, a state routing-and-transition layer executes rank-local transfers under a memory-aware, deadlock-free schedule, and an Elastic Device Manager overlaps new-world construction with ongoing training to mask topology-change cost. On dense and MoE models up to 235B parameters, DynaTrain reconfigures a 70B dense model in under 2s and a 235B MoE model in 4.36s, outperforming state-of-the-art checkpoint-based and elastic systems by up to three orders of magnitude while preserving correctness.

AI.tldr: DynaTrain: A distributed training system enabling sub-second online reconfiguration across arbitrary multi-dimensional parallelism for dynamic LLM training environments.
AI.motivation: Modern LLM training is dynamic with resource fluctuations, RLHF phase shifts, and cluster elasticity that constantly change optimal parallelism layouts. Existing frameworks use static execution models that can't adapt to these dynamic requirements.
AI.method: DynaTrain introduces a Virtual Parameter Space (VPS) abstraction that unifies distributed training states in one logical coordinate space. It includes: 1) VPS for deterministic mapping of parallelism configurations, 2) State routing-and-transition layer for rank-local transfers with memory-aware, deadlock-free scheduling, 3) Elastic Device Manager that overlaps new-world construction with ongoing training.
AI.result: DynaTrain achieves sub-second reconfiguration times: under 2s for a 70B dense model and 4.36s for a 235B MoE model. It outperforms state-of-the-art checkpoint-based and elastic systems by up to three orders of magnitude while maintaining correctness.
AI.conclusion: DynaTrain successfully addresses the challenge of dynamic LLM training by enabling fast, online reconfiguration across arbitrary multi-dimensional parallelism, making distributed training adaptable to changing resource requirements and cluster conditions.

title: SimGym: A Framework for A/B Test Simulation in E-Commerce with Traffic-Grounded VLM Agents
summary: A/B testing remains the gold standard for evaluating modifications to e-commerce storefronts, yet it diverts traffic, requires weeks to reach statistical significance, and risks degrading user experience. We present SimGym, a framework for simulating A/B tests on e-commerce storefronts using vision-language model (VLM) agents operating in a live browser. The framework comprises three key components: (a) a traffic-grounded persona generation pipeline that derives per-shop buyer archetypes and intents from production clickstream data; (b) a live-browser agent architecture that combines multimodal perception over visual and browser-structured observations with episodic memory and guardrails to conduct coherent shopping sessions across control and treatment storefronts; and (c) an evaluation protocol that compares simulated outcome shifts with observed shifts in real buyer behavior. We validate SimGym on A/B tests of visually driven UI theme changes from a major e-commerce platform across diverse storefronts and product categories. Empirical results show that SimGym agents achieve strong agreement with observed outcome shifts, attaining 77% directional alignment with add-to-cart shifts observed across interface variants in real-buyer traffic. It reduces experimental cycles from weeks to under an hour, enabling rapid experimentation without exposing real buyers to candidate variants.

AI.tldr: SimGym is a framework that uses vision-language model agents in live browsers to simulate A/B tests for e-commerce storefronts, reducing testing time from weeks to under an hour while achieving 77% directional alignment with real buyer behavior.
AI.motivation: Traditional A/B testing for e-commerce storefronts has significant drawbacks: it diverts traffic, takes weeks to reach statistical significance, and risks degrading user experience by exposing real customers to potentially harmful variants.
AI.method: The framework consists of three components: 1) traffic-grounded persona generation from production clickstream data, 2) live-browser VLM agents with multimodal perception and episodic memory to simulate shopping sessions, and 3) an evaluation protocol comparing simulated outcomes with real buyer behavior shifts.
AI.result: Empirical validation on visually-driven UI theme changes from a major e-commerce platform shows SimGym achieves 77% directional alignment with add-to-cart shifts observed in real-buyer traffic, while reducing experimental cycles from weeks to under an hour.
AI.conclusion: SimGym provides an effective alternative to traditional A/B testing by enabling rapid experimentation without exposing real buyers to candidate variants, with strong agreement to observed outcome shifts across diverse storefronts and product categories.

title: Symmetry in the Wild: The Role of Equivariance in Neural Fluid Surrogates
summary: Neural surrogates enable orders-of-magnitude acceleration of computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations, with the potential to transform engineering and healthcare workflows. Neural surrogate use in real-world applications requires addressing scalability to large, high-resolution surface and volume meshes, as well as to bespoke architectures, and accounting for limited training data through the use of inductive biases. Group-equivariant architectures are a principled way to introduce such bias, yet they can be detrimental when the learning problem itself breaks symmetry, for example, due to strong distributional alignment in the dataset. In this work, we investigate under which conditions equivariance improves generalization in neural CFD surrogates across tasks with increasing levels of distributional alignment and realism, covering automotive aerodynamics and blood flow (hemodynamics). To systematically assess the added value of equivariance at the limit of problem scaling, we introduce the Anchored-Branched Geometric Algebra Transformer (AB-GATr), a neural surrogate that integrates scalability and symmetry preservation to efficiently model coupled surface and volume quantities in an $E(3)$-equivariant manner. We find that on strongly aligned aerodynamics datasets, i.e., those that break symmetry, enforcing equivariance can degrade in-distribution performance. In contrast, across hemodynamic benchmarks with diverse geometries and varying alignment, equivariance is consistently beneficial. Moreover, across all benchmarks, the explicit equivariance of AB-GATr reliably outperforms implicit symmetry learning through data augmentation. Our findings showcase that equivariance is not universally beneficial across domains, yet it brings tangible advantages in problems lacking strong data regularities.

AI.tldr: Neural CFD surrogates benefit from equivariant architectures in hemodynamics but not in strongly aligned aerodynamics, where symmetry breaking degrades performance.
AI.motivation: Neural surrogates accelerate CFD simulations but need scalability, handling of limited data via inductive biases, and understanding when equivariance helps vs. hurts generalization across different application domains with varying symmetry characteristics.
AI.method: Introduces AB-GATr (Anchored-Branched Geometric Algebra Transformer), an E(3)-equivariant neural surrogate for coupled surface/volume quantities. Systematically tests equivariance across automotive aerodynamics (strong alignment) vs. hemodynamics (diverse geometries, varying alignment) benchmarks.
AI.result: Equivariance degrades performance on strongly aligned aerodynamics datasets (which break symmetry) but consistently benefits hemodynamics with diverse geometries. Explicit equivariance in AB-GATr reliably outperforms implicit symmetry learning via data augmentation across all benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Equivariance is not universally beneficial across domains - it helps in problems lacking strong data regularities (like hemodynamics) but can hurt in problems with strong distributional alignment that breaks symmetry (like certain aerodynamics datasets).

title: Can Large Language Models Revolutionize Survey Research? Experiments with Disaster Preparedness Responses
summary: Survey research faces mounting structural challenges: declining response rates, sample bias, block-wise missingness among at-risk respondents, and AI-assisted fraudulent completions in online panels. Large language models (LLMs) have been proposed as a remedy, yet rigorous evaluations across the full survey workflow remain scarce, particularly in disaster contexts where data quality matters most. We present and evaluate a five-stage framework for LLM integration covering questionnaire design, sample selection, pilot testing, missing-data imputation, and post-collection analysis, using the 2024 Hurricane Milton preparedness survey of Florida residents (n=946) as a shared empirical testbed. We introduce a Protection Motivation Theory (PMT)-constrained co-occurrence knowledge graph and develop seven LLM configurations spanning zero-shot inference, retrieval-augmented baselines, and novel theory-informed variants. Our proposed Anchored Marginal Theory-Informed LLM (A-TLM) outperforms all three classical imputation baselines (IPW/MI, MICE+PMM, missForest) on RMSE under disaster-relevant block-wise MNAR conditions (S4 RMSE 1.439 vs. 1.496 for the next-best), while achieving near-zero signed bias (-0.121) where the random-forest imputer produces the largest absolute bias (-0.631). Organizing retrieval around PMT causal structure and integrating all evidence in a single model call outperforms unstructured retrieval and staged sequential inference (MAE 0.993 vs. 1.097 for standard RAG). We document that near-zero aggregate bias can mask opposing subgroup errors and propose subgroup-stratified bias auditing as a reporting standard. A retrieval-constrained knowledge-graph chatbot demonstrates that hallucination is architecturally manageable through grounded refusal.

AI.tldr: A framework for integrating LLMs across the full survey workflow, demonstrating improved performance for missing-data imputation in disaster contexts compared to classical methods.
AI.motivation: Survey research faces structural challenges: declining response rates, sample bias, block-wise missingness, and AI-assisted fraud. LLMs have been proposed as remedy but lack rigorous evaluation across the full survey workflow, especially in disaster contexts where data quality is critical.
AI.method: Five-stage LLM integration framework covering questionnaire design, sample selection, pilot testing, missing-data imputation, and post-collection analysis. Introduced PMT-constrained co-occurrence knowledge graph and developed seven LLM configurations. Used 2024 Hurricane Milton preparedness survey (n=946) as empirical testbed.
AI.result: Proposed Anchored Marginal Theory-Informed LLM (A-TLM) outperformed classical imputation methods on RMSE under disaster-relevant block-wise MNAR conditions. Achieved near-zero signed bias. PMT-structured retrieval outperformed unstructured retrieval. Demonstrated that near-zero aggregate bias can mask opposing subgroup errors. Showed hallucination is architecturally manageable through grounded refusal.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can effectively address structural challenges in survey research when properly integrated across the full workflow, particularly using theory-informed approaches in disaster contexts. Subgroup-stratified bias auditing should be a reporting standard to reveal masked errors.

title: Multi-Token Residual Prediction
summary: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) generate text by iteratively denoising masked token sequences, offering a tradeoff between parallelism and quality compared to autoregressive models. In current practice, the number of tokens decoded per step is controlled by a confidence threshold, and quality degrades monotonically as more tokens are denoised per step. We introduce Multi-token Residual Prediction (MRP), a lightweight module that enables dependency-aware multi-token denoising within a single backbone forward pass. MRP exploits a key property of the denoising process: the logit distributions at adjacent denoising steps are remarkably similar. Rather than running the backbone a second time to obtain the next-step logits, MRP predicts the residual between steps from the backbone's hidden states, effectively denoising more tokens per backbone forward at a fraction of the cost. We deploy MRP in two inference modes: direct decoding, which uses the corrected logits without verification for a tunable quality--speed tradeoff; and speculative decoding, which verifies MRP's proposals against the backbone for lossless acceleration. Experiments on SDAR models at the 1.7B, 4B, and 8B scales across reasoning and code generation benchmarks demonstrate up to $1.42\times$ lossless speedup in SGLang.

AI.tldr: Multi-token Residual Prediction (MRP) enables dependency-aware multi-token denoising in Diffusion Language Models by predicting residual logits between steps, achieving up to 1.42× speedup without quality loss.
AI.motivation: Current DLMs decode tokens using confidence thresholds, but quality degrades as more tokens are denoised per step. There's a need to accelerate DLMs without sacrificing quality, as running the backbone model multiple times for multi-token generation is expensive.
AI.method: MRP introduces a lightweight module that predicts the residual between adjacent denoising step logits from backbone hidden states. It exploits the similarity between logit distributions at adjacent steps, avoiding the need for additional backbone forward passes. Two inference modes are deployed: direct decoding (tunable quality-speed tradeoff) and speculative decoding (lossless verification).
AI.result: Experiments on SDAR models at 1.7B, 4B, and 8B scales show up to 1.42× lossless speedup in SGLang across reasoning and code generation benchmarks. The method effectively denoises more tokens per backbone forward pass at minimal computational cost.
AI.conclusion: MRP provides an efficient solution for accelerating DLMs by enabling multi-token denoising without requiring additional backbone computations, offering both tunable quality-speed tradeoffs and lossless acceleration through verification.

title: Causal Evidence for Attention Head Imbalance in Modality Conflict Hallucination
summary: Modality-conflict hallucination occurs when multimodal large language models (MLLMs) prioritize erroneous textual premises over contradictory visual evidence. To understand why visual evidence fails to prevail during generation, we take a mechanistic perspective and examine which internal components drive or resist this failure. We perform head-level causal analysis using path patching across five open-source MLLMs and identify two groups of attention heads with opposing causal roles: hallucination-driving heads and hallucination-resisting heads. We find a consistent asymmetry: driving effects are more broadly distributed and carry greater aggregate weight, whereas resisting effects concentrate in a small number of high-importance heads. Ablation experiments further confirm that these groups exert opposing effects during generation: distributed driving influence and localized resistance together form an imbalanced routing structure that biases generation toward the erroneous premise. Motivated by this finding, we propose MACI (Modality-conflict-Aware Causal Intervention), a conditional intervention that suppresses causally identified hallucination-driving heads only when conflict is detected. Across five MLLMs, MACI achieves the largest hallucination reduction among compared inference-time baselines on the MMMC benchmark with a favorable hallucination-accuracy trade-off, and transfers zero-shot to the SCI-SemanticConflict test.

AI.tldr: MLLMs suffer from modality-conflict hallucination where they prioritize erroneous text over contradictory visual evidence. The paper identifies two groups of attention heads (driving vs. resisting) with imbalanced influence, and proposes MACI intervention to suppress driving heads only during conflicts, achieving state-of-the-art hallucination reduction.
AI.motivation: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) exhibit "modality-conflict hallucination" - they prioritize incorrect textual information over contradictory visual evidence, leading to erroneous outputs. The authors aim to understand why visual evidence fails to prevail during generation and develop interventions to mitigate this problem.
AI.method: 1) Performed head-level causal analysis using path patching across five open-source MLLMs to identify attention heads with opposing roles. 2) Identified two groups: hallucination-driving heads and hallucination-resisting heads. 3) Proposed MACI (Modality-conflict-Aware Causal Intervention) - a conditional intervention that suppresses identified hallucination-driving heads only when modality conflict is detected.
AI.result: 1) Found consistent asymmetry: driving effects are broadly distributed with greater aggregate weight, while resisting effects concentrate in few high-importance heads. 2) MACI achieved largest hallucination reduction among inference-time baselines on MMMC benchmark with favorable hallucination-accuracy trade-off. 3) Successfully transferred zero-shot to SCI-SemanticConflict test across five MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: The imbalanced routing structure (distributed driving influence vs. localized resistance) biases MLLM generation toward erroneous premises. MACI's conditional intervention effectively reduces modality-conflict hallucinations by targeting the identified causal mechanisms, demonstrating the value of mechanistic understanding for improving MLLM reliability.

title: Efficient Conditioning Why Pseudo Observation Batch Bayesian Optimization Works When It Does not
summary: Constant Liar (CL), Kriging Believer (KB), and fantasy models are widely used for batch selection in parallel Bayesian Optimization, yet a unified theory explaining their effectiveness and conditions under which they fail has been lacking. We identify efficient conditioning as the key surrogate property the ability to update predictions in closed form when data is augmented. We prove that Gaussian Processes satisfy this requirement, producing provably distinct batch points with separation of order l, and that this holds for any acquisition function monotonically non decreasing in posterior uncertainty (EI, UCB, PI), with qualitatively similar behavior for Thompson Sampling. We unify CL, KB, and fantasy models as instances of a single conditioning mechanism differing only in the lie value distribution, and draw quantitative connections to Local Penalization (LP) and qualitative connections to Determinantal Point Processes (DPPs). To disentangle model structure from optimizer randomness, we introduce the Structural Diversity Diagnostic (SDD), a reusable methodology for testing surrogate compatibility. Experiments on Hartmann6D, Ackley 8D, Levy10D, and SVM hyperparameter tuning validate all theoretical predictions: CL or KBs implicit penalty matches or outperforms explicit LP greedy conditioning achieves convergence on par with joint qEI efficient conditioning extends to Multiquadric RBF networks; and parametric surrogates produce degenerate batches even when fully retrained (random forests), while neural networks regain diversity only at 15x the wall clock cost of GP conditioning. Robustness is confirmed across multiple initial datasets and under observation noise.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a unified theory showing that Gaussian Processes enable efficient batch selection in Bayesian Optimization via closed-form updates, explaining why methods like Constant Liar, Kriging Believer, and fantasy models work, while parametric models often fail.
AI.motivation: Batch selection methods like Constant Liar, Kriging Believer, and fantasy models are widely used in parallel Bayesian Optimization but lack a unified theoretical explanation for their effectiveness and failure conditions.
AI.method: The paper identifies "efficient conditioning" as the key surrogate property (closed-form predictions with augmented data), proves Gaussian Processes satisfy this with provable batch point separation, and unifies various methods as instances of a single conditioning mechanism. Introduces Structural Diversity Diagnostic (SDD) to test surrogate compatibility.
AI.result: Theoretical predictions validated on benchmark functions and SVM hyperparameter tuning: CL/KB's implicit penalty matches/exceeds explicit Local Penalization; greedy conditioning matches joint qEI convergence; efficient conditioning extends to Multiquadric RBF networks; parametric surrogates produce degenerate batches; neural networks require 15x more cost than GP conditioning.
AI.conclusion: Efficient conditioning through Gaussian Processes provides a unified theoretical framework for parallel Bayesian Optimization batch selection methods, explaining their success and identifying when alternative surrogates fail, with robustness confirmed across various conditions.

title: Emergence of Frontier Superposition: Möbius attractor and Cascade Supervision
summary:
Superposition allows Transformers to reason in depth, carrying an entire reasoning frontier in parallel through a bounded-depth forward pass instead of unrolling serial chain-of-thought tokens. While Zhu et al. (2025) hand-crafted an equal-weight breadth-first frontier in a single residual stream for graph reachability, it remained open whether gradient descent could ever find this target amidst permutation-symmetric saddles.
  We close this gap on Reachability-by-Superposition over Erdős-Rényi graphs by isolating architectural and supervisional contributions. Architecturally, we identify a Möbius attractor: under $S_n$-symmetry in the tree regime, layerwise dynamics reduce to a 1D Möbius map whose zero set is a codimension-one manifold of global optima containing the equal-weight superposition state.
  On the supervision side, we identify Cascade Supervision: a loss class whose backward pass simultaneously delivers (A) selectivity bootstrap, (B) gradient persistence across depth, and (C) per-step discrimination (e.g., \mathcal{L}_{sup} and \mathcal{L}_{node}). End-to-end supervision fails condition (B) and is provably insufficient: internal gradients at layer c decay as (np)^{-(D-c-2)/2} in the graph fan-out and stall before the manifold is reached.
  Our thesis: Möbius attractor + Cascade Supervision = emergence of superposition reasoning. The parameter-free decay law predicts a final-step cosine of 0.35 vs. 0.71 (end-to-end vs. cascade) at depth D=3; experiments confirm 0.37 vs. 0.69, matching within 0.02 at every step.

AI.tldr: Transformers can learn superposition reasoning for graph reachability through architectural Möbius attractors and cascade supervision, overcoming gradient decay that prevents end-to-end training from finding optimal superposition states.
AI.motivation: Previous work (Zhu et al., 2025) hand-crafted equal-weight superposition for graph reachability but left open whether gradient descent could discover this structure naturally due to permutation-symmetric saddles. The paper aims to close this gap by identifying why and how superposition reasoning emerges during training.
AI.method: 1) Architecturally identifies a Möbius attractor - under permutation symmetry in tree regimes, layerwise dynamics reduce to a 1D Möbius map whose zero set contains global optima including the equal-weight superposition state. 2) Introduces Cascade Supervision, a loss class providing selectivity bootstrap, gradient persistence across depth, and per-step discrimination. This overcomes gradient decay that occurs in end-to-end supervision.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows end-to-end supervision fails due to gradient decay (np)^{-(D-c-2)/2}, while cascade supervision enables learning superposition reasoning. Experimental results match theoretical predictions: final-step cosine similarity of 0.35 vs. 0.71 (end-to-end vs. cascade) at depth D=3, with experiments showing 0.37 vs. 0.69 - matching within 0.02 at every step.
AI.conclusion: Superposition reasoning emerges from the combination of Möbius attractors in the architecture and cascade supervision. This provides both theoretical understanding and practical training methods for enabling Transformers to perform parallel reasoning through superposition rather than serial chain-of-thought.

title: Quantum Adversarial Machine Learning: From Classical Adaptations to Quantum-Native Methods
summary: Machine learning has revolutionized numerous industrial domains. Despite recent advances, machine learning models remain vulnerable to adversarial threats. Adversarial machine learning is a field that studies these vulnerabilities to build robust machine learning models. Quantum machine learning is an interdisciplinary field that bridges quantum computing and classical machine learning. While quantum machine learning shows potentials to outperform classical machine learning in complex tasks such as regression, classification, and generative modeling, it remains vulnerable to adversarial attacks. Given the recent advancements in quantum computing and machine learning, the quantum adversarial machine learning field has emerged to study the vulnerabilities of quantum machine learning, possible attacks, and novel quantum-enhanced defense strategies. In this survey, we provide a detailed overview on quantum adversarial machine learning and explore the existing attacks and countermeasures. We also review the theoretical underpinnings of this area, emerging trends, and critical challenges.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey on quantum adversarial machine learning, covering vulnerabilities of quantum ML models, existing attacks, defense strategies, and emerging trends in this interdisciplinary field.
AI.motivation: While quantum machine learning shows potential to outperform classical ML in various tasks, it remains vulnerable to adversarial attacks. Recent advancements in both quantum computing and machine learning necessitate a systematic study of quantum adversarial machine learning to understand vulnerabilities, develop attacks, and create robust defense strategies.
AI.method: This is a survey paper that provides a detailed overview of the quantum adversarial machine learning field. The authors systematically explore and review existing attacks, countermeasures, theoretical foundations, and current research trends through comprehensive literature analysis.
AI.result: The survey organizes and presents the current state of quantum adversarial machine learning, including: 1) analysis of vulnerabilities in quantum ML models, 2) categorization of existing attack methods, 3) review of quantum-enhanced defense strategies, and 4) identification of theoretical underpinnings in this emerging field.
AI.conclusion: Quantum adversarial machine learning is an important emerging field at the intersection of quantum computing and classical machine learning. The survey serves as a comprehensive resource for understanding the vulnerabilities of quantum ML, existing attack and defense mechanisms, and provides insights into future research directions and critical challenges in building robust quantum machine learning systems.

title: MOCHA: Multi-Objective Chebyshev Annealing for Agent Skill Optimization
summary: LLM agents organize behavior through skills - structured natural-language specifications governing how an agent reasons, retrieves, and responds. Unlike monolithic prompts, skills are multi-field artifacts subject to hard platform constraints: description fields are truncated for routing, instruction bodies are compacted via progressive disclosure, and co-resident skills compete for limited context windows. These constraints make skill optimization inherently multi-objective: a skill must simultaneously maximize task performance and satisfy platform limits. Yet existing prompt optimizers either ignore these trade-offs or collapse them into a weighted sum, missing Pareto-optimal variants in non-convex objective regions. We introduce MOCHA (Multi-Objective Chebyshev Annealing), which replaces single-objective selection with Chebyshev scalarization - covering the full Pareto front, including non-convex regions - combined with exponential annealing that transitions from exploration to exploitation.
