In our experiments across six diverse agent skills - where all methods share the same multi-objective mutation operator and baselines receive identical per-objective textual feedback - existing optimizers fail to improve the seed skill on 4 of 6 tasks: 1000 rollouts yield zero progress. MOCHA breaks through on every task, achieving 7.5% relative improvement in mean correctness over the strongest baseline (up to 14.9% on FEVER and 10.4% on TheoremQA) while discovering twice as many more Pareto-optimal skill variants.

AI.tldr: MOCHA introduces multi-objective optimization for LLM agent skills using Chebyshev scalarization and exponential annealing to handle platform constraints while maximizing task performance.
AI.motivation: LLM agent skills face hard platform constraints (truncated descriptions, compacted instructions, limited context windows), requiring simultaneous optimization of both task performance and platform limits. Existing prompt optimizers either ignore these trade-offs or use weighted sums that miss Pareto-optimal solutions in non-convex objective regions.
AI.method: MOCHA (Multi-Objective Chebyshev Annealing) combines Chebyshev scalarization - which covers the full Pareto front including non-convex regions - with exponential annealing that transitions from exploration to exploitation. The method is evaluated across six diverse agent skills with all methods sharing the same multi-objective mutation operator and baselines receiving identical per-objective textual feedback.
AI.result: Existing optimizers failed to improve seed skills on 4 of 6 tasks with 1000 rollouts yielding zero progress. MOCHA succeeded on every task, achieving 7.5% relative improvement in mean correctness over the strongest baseline (up to 14.9% on FEVER and 10.4% on TheoremQA) while discovering twice as many Pareto-optimal skill variants.
AI.conclusion: MOCHA effectively addresses the multi-objective nature of LLM agent skill optimization by covering the full Pareto front with Chebyshev scalarization and exponential annealing, outperforming existing methods that fail to handle platform constraints while maximizing task performance.

title: Hybrid-LoRA: Bridging Full Fine-Tuning and Low-Rank Adaptation for Post-Training
summary: Post-training has become essential for adapting large language models (LLMs) to complex downstream behaviors, including instruction following, preference alignment, and multi-step reasoning. Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has recently emerged as a particularly effective post-training paradigm for improving reasoning capabilities, with critic-free algorithms such as GRPO and GSPO enabling scalable optimization. However, RLVR post-training with full fine-tuning (FFT) requires substantial GPU memory and incurs high training costs. Although parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods, such as Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), effectively reduce computational costs, they often suffer from a noticeable performance gap compared to full fine-tuning in post-training for complex reasoning tasks. In this paper, we propose Hybrid-LoRA, an efficient hybrid post-training framework that selectively applies full fine-tuning to a small subset of modules less suited to low-rank adaptation, while adapting the remaining components with LoRA. We introduce a novel Hybrid-LoRA Score to rank candidate modules according to their sensitivity to low-rank adaptation under a fixed parameter budget. Experiments show that Hybrid-LoRA closely matches full fine-tuning performance under a 10% full fine-tuning module budget, with the remaining candidate modules adapted by LoRA, consistently outperforming four state-of-the-art PEFT post-training baselines, achieving improvements of up to 5.65% and on average 4.36% over the best baseline.

AI.tldr: Hybrid-LoRA combines full fine-tuning and LoRA adapters selectively to achieve near-FFT performance with 10x efficiency gain for RLVR post-training of LLMs.
AI.motivation: RLVR post-training improves reasoning but FFT is expensive, while PEFT methods like LoRA have performance gaps for complex reasoning tasks.
AI.method: Proposes Hybrid-LoRA framework that selectively applies FFT to modules less suited for LoRA and uses LoRA for others, with a novel Hybrid-LoRA Score to rank modules.
AI.result: Matches FFT performance with 10% FFT budget, outperforms SOTA PEFT baselines by up to 5.65% (avg 4.36%).
AI.conclusion: Hybrid-LoRA provides efficient high-performance post-training by balancing FFT and LoRA, bridging PEFT-FFT performance gap for complex reasoning.

title: Agentic Trading: When LLM Agents Meet Financial Markets
summary: A growing body of work explores how Large Language Models (LLMs) can be embedded in trading systems as agents that perceive market information, retrieve context, reason about decisions, emit tradable actions, and adapt under market feedback. This paper reframes LLM-based trading agents as expert-system decision pipelines and presents an audit-oriented evidence map of 77 included studies in a protocol-coded snapshot screened through 2026-03-09. A primary empirical subset (n=19) satisfies the minimum boundary of Action Output plus Closed-Loop Evaluation; the remaining 58 included studies are retained as background and design context. The central empirical finding is protocol incomparability: within the primary subset, only 2/19 studies report extractable time-consistent split protocols, 1/19 reports an explicit transaction-cost model, 1/19 documents universe or survivorship handling, 11/19 report execution timing or semantics, 15/19 are coded as R0, and no study reaches R3 reproducibility. We therefore use Architecture-Capability-Adaptation as a working analytical lens rather than a validated taxonomy, and we foreground the evidence ledger, reproducibility audit, and reporting checklist as the main contributions. The resulting survey shows that architectural experimentation is expanding rapidly, while comparable evaluation protocols, execution semantics, and reproducible artifacts remain the field's immediate bottlenecks.

AI.tldr: A systematic survey reveals LLM-based trading agents lack comparable evaluation protocols and reproducibility, with only 2/19 core studies having extractable protocols and none achieving high reproducibility.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly embedded in trading systems as decision-making agents, there's a need to systematically audit and map the evidence landscape to understand current capabilities, limitations, and reproducibility challenges in this emerging field.
AI.method: The paper reframes LLM-based trading agents as expert-system decision pipelines and conducts an audit-oriented evidence map of 77 studies, with 19 studies meeting minimum criteria for inclusion in the primary empirical subset. The analysis uses protocol coding and focuses on Architecture-Capability-Adaptation as an analytical lens.
AI.result: The main finding is severe protocol incomparability: only 2/19 studies report extractable time-consistent split protocols, 1/19 reports explicit transaction-cost models, 1/19 documents universe/survivorship handling, 15/19 are coded as R0 reproducibility, and no study reaches R3 reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: While architectural experimentation is expanding rapidly, comparable evaluation protocols, execution semantics, and reproducible artifacts remain critical bottlenecks for the field. The paper contributes an evidence ledger, reproducibility audit, and reporting checklist to address these issues.

title: Multi-Pedestrian Safety Warning at Urban Intersections Use Case of Digital Twin
summary: Digital twins (DTs) for urban transportation systems have gained increasing attention; however, their systematic evaluation in safety-critical scenarios remains limited. This paper presents a multi-pedestrian safety warning system at urban intersections enabled by a tightly coupled physical-digital twin framework. Built upon the COSMOS city-scale wireless testbed in New York City, the proposed system integrates camera and ultra-wideband (UWB), edge-cloud computing, predictive trajectory modeling, and MQTT-based communication to deliver real-time safety alerts to vulnerable road users (VRUs). The system is evaluated through both field deployment and virtual reality (VR) experiments. Results demonstrate high warning generation accuracy, localization accuracy, efficient end-to-end latency under different model configurations, and significant reductions in user response time when warnings are issued. The proposed DT framework provides a scalable, modular, and generalizable solution for real-time multi-pedestrian safety enhancement at complex urban intersections.

AI.tldr: A digital twin framework for multi-pedestrian safety warnings at urban intersections using real-time sensing, predictive modeling, and edge-cloud computing.
AI.motivation: Digital twins for urban transportation lack systematic evaluation in safety-critical scenarios, particularly for vulnerable road users at complex intersections.
AI.method: Integrated camera and ultra-wideband (UWB) sensing, edge-cloud computing, predictive trajectory modeling, and MQTT communication built on the COSMOS city-scale wireless testbed in NYC. Evaluated through field deployment and VR experiments.
AI.result: High warning accuracy, good localization precision, efficient end-to-end latency, and significant reduction in user response time when warnings are issued.
AI.conclusion: The digital twin framework provides a scalable, modular, and generalizable solution for enhancing real-time multi-pedestrian safety at complex urban intersections.

title: Generative Recursive Reasoning
summary: How should future neural reasoning systems implement extended computation? Recursive Reasoning Models (RRMs) offer a promising alternative to autoregressive sequence extension by performing iterative latent-state refinement with shared transition functions. Yet existing RRMs are largely deterministic, following a single latent trajectory and converging to a single prediction. We introduce \emph{Generative Recursive reAsoning Models (GRAM)}, a framework that turns recursive latent reasoning into probabilistic multi-trajectory computation. GRAM models reasoning as a stochastic latent trajectory, enabling multiple hypotheses, alternative solution strategies, and inference-time scaling through both recursive depth and parallel trajectory sampling. This yields a latent-variable generative model supporting conditional reasoning via $p_θ(y \mid x)$ and, with fixed or absent inputs, unconditional generation via $p_θ(x)$. Trained with amortized variational inference, GRAM improves over deterministic recurrent and recursive baselines on structured reasoning and multi-solution constraint satisfaction tasks, while demonstrating an unconditional generation capability. \href{https://ahn-ml.github.io/gram-website/}{https://ahn-ml.github.io/gram-website}

AI.tldr: GRAM (Generative Recursive reAsoning Models) introduces a probabilistic framework for neural reasoning that extends deterministic recursive models through stochastic latent trajectories, enabling multiple hypotheses, alternative strategies, and improved performance on structured reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing Recursive Reasoning Models (RRMs) are largely deterministic, following single latent trajectories and converging to single predictions. This limitation prevents them from exploring multiple hypotheses or alternative solution strategies, which is crucial for complex reasoning tasks.
AI.method: GRAM transforms recursive latent reasoning into probabilistic multi-trajectory computation by modeling reasoning as stochastic latent trajectories. It's trained with amortized variational inference, enabling conditional reasoning via pθ(y|x) and unconditional generation via pθ(x) with fixed/absent inputs.
AI.result: GRAM outperforms deterministic recurrent and recursive baselines on structured reasoning and multi-solution constraint satisfaction tasks. It demonstrates unconditional generation capability and supports inference-time scaling through both recursive depth and parallel trajectory sampling.
AI.conclusion: GRAM provides a promising framework for future neural reasoning systems that need extended computation capabilities, offering probabilistic multi-trajectory reasoning that captures uncertainty, enables exploration of alternative solutions, and scales effectively through parallel sampling.

title: Fine-Grained Benchmark Generation for Comprehensive Evaluation of Foundation Models
summary: Evaluation of foundation models often rely on aggregate scores from benchmarks that lack comprehensive coverage and metadata for a fine-grained evaluation. We introduce a framework for automated benchmark generation. Our framework generates evaluation problems grounded in reference material, such as textbooks, producing benchmarks with broad coverage, rich metadata, and robustness to contamination. The pipeline employs a multi-agent architecture for problem generation and a solution-graph-driven strategy that significantly improves the reliability of ground truth solutions. Using the framework, we generate three benchmarks in Machine Learning, Corporate Finance, and Personal Finance. Expert review finds a significantly lower ground-truth error rate than previous benchmarks such as MMLU and GSM8K. Evaluation of 12 commercial and open-source models shows that our benchmarks achieve near-uniform competency coverage and surface performance differences across models that existing benchmarks fail to capture. We will open-source the framework and our curated benchmarks soon.

AI.tldr: A framework for automated benchmark generation that creates evaluation problems from reference materials, improving coverage and reliability while reducing ground-truth errors compared to existing benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current foundation model evaluation relies on benchmarks with limited coverage, lack of metadata, and contamination issues, necessitating better evaluation tools with comprehensive coverage and robust ground truth.
AI.method: Multi-agent architecture for problem generation with solution-graph-driven strategy for reliable ground truth solutions, creating benchmarks from reference materials like textbooks across multiple domains.
AI.result: Generated three benchmarks in ML, Corporate Finance, and Personal Finance with significantly lower ground-truth error rates than MMLU and GSM8K, achieving near-uniform competency coverage and revealing model performance differences that existing benchmarks miss.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables automated generation of high-quality benchmarks with broad coverage and reliable ground truth, providing better evaluation of foundation models while being robust to contamination. The framework and benchmarks will be open-sourced.

title: PRISM: A Benchmark for Programmatic Spatial-Temporal Reasoning
summary: Programmatic video generation through code offers geometric precision and temporal coherence beyond pixel-level diffusion models, yet rigorously evaluating whether language models can produce spatially correct animated outputs remains an open problem. We introduce PRISM, a large-scale benchmark of 10,372 human-calibrated instruction-code pairs (20 times larger than prior programmatic video generation benchmarks), grounded in real-world knowledge visualization scenarios across English and Chinese and spanning 437 subject categories. We further propose a funnel-style evaluation framework with four complementary metrics: Code-Level Reliability for executability, Spatial Reasoning for layout correctness over full animation sequences, and Prompt-Aware Dynamic Visual Complexity (PADVC) and Temporal Density (TD) for diagnosing dynamic expression and temporal activity. Systematic evaluation of seven mainstream LLMs reveals a striking Execution-Spatial Gap: the average drop from execution success rate to spatial pass rate is approximately 41%, showing that runnable code does not necessarily yield spatially coherent visual output. These findings show that programmatic video generation evaluation should go beyond executability. PRISM provides a principled benchmark for advancing spatially coherent code generation.

AI.tldr: PRISM is a large-scale benchmark (10,372 instruction-code pairs) for evaluating programmatic video generation capabilities of LLMs, revealing a significant gap between code executability and spatial correctness in animated outputs.
AI.motivation: There's a need to rigorously evaluate whether language models can produce spatially correct animated outputs in programmatic video generation, moving beyond just assessing executability to measure spatial coherence in visual outputs.
AI.method: Created PRISM benchmark with human-calibrated instruction-code pairs grounded in real-world knowledge visualization across English/Chinese (437 subject categories). Developed funnel-style evaluation with four metrics: Code-Level Reliability, Spatial Reasoning, PADVC, and Temporal Density.
AI.result: Evaluation of seven mainstream LLMs revealed an Execution-Spatial Gap: average 41% drop from execution success rate to spatial pass rate, showing runnable code doesn't necessarily yield spatially coherent visual output.
AI.conclusion: Programmatic video generation evaluation should go beyond executability; PRISM provides a principled benchmark for advancing spatially coherent code generation and addressing the execution-spatial gap.

title: Not All Tokens Are Worth Caching: Learning Semantic-Aware Eviction for LLM Prefix Caches
summary: Prefix caching is a key optimization in Large Language Model (LLM) serving, reusing attention Key-Value (KV) states across requests with shared prompt prefixes to reduce expensive prefill computation. However, its benefit depends critically on the eviction policy as GPU memory is scarce, and existing policies such as LRU largely treat cached blocks uniformly. This view ignores a fundamental property of LLM prompts: not all tokens are equally worth caching. We show that different token types within a prompt, including system prompts, user queries, tool outputs, model responses, and chain-of-thought reasoning, exhibit up to 756x variation in reuse rates, yet no existing eviction policy exploits this signal. In this paper, we present SAECache (Semantic-Adaptive Eviction for prefix caches), a semantic-adaptive prefix cache eviction policy that addresses this gap through three innovations: (1) a multi-queue architecture that routes KV blocks to task-specific queues with tailored priority metrics, capturing both session reuse in multi-turn requests and structural reuse in templated single-turn requests; (2) a semantic-aware token weighting mechanism that learns the reuse value of different token types online through eviction feedback; and (3) a fully adaptive online learning schema for all parameter updates, including log-normal timing parameters, position decay power, queue weights, and meta-parameters, which eliminates manual tuning and enables automatic adaptation to deployment-specific workload characteristics. Through extensive evaluation across heterogeneous workloads, we demonstrate that SAECache achieves 1.4x-2.7x TTFT improvement over production-style baselines, while fixed-parameter alternatives can degrade by up to 2.7x under workload mismatch -- a failure mode our adaptive approach avoids entirely.

AI.tldr: SAECache is a semantic-adaptive eviction policy for LLM prefix caches that learns token reuse value online, achieving 1.4x-2.7x TTFT improvement over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing prefix cache eviction policies (like LRU) treat all cached blocks uniformly, ignoring that different token types (system prompts, user queries, tool outputs, etc.) have vastly different reuse rates (up to 756x variation). No current policy exploits this semantic signal for better cache management.
AI.method: SAECache uses three innovations: (1) multi-queue architecture with task-specific queues for session and structural reuse patterns, (2) semantic-aware token weighting that learns reuse value of different token types through eviction feedback, and (3) fully adaptive online learning schema that automatically tunes all parameters (timing, position decay, queue weights, meta-parameters) without manual configuration.
AI.result: SAECache achieves 1.4x-2.7x improvement in Time-To-First-Token (TTFT) compared to production-style baselines. Fixed-parameter alternatives can degrade by up to 2.7x under workload mismatch, which SAECache avoids entirely through adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Semantic awareness in prefix cache eviction policies is crucial for LLM serving optimization. SAECache demonstrates that learning token reuse patterns online and adapting to workload characteristics yields significant performance gains while avoiding degradation from workload mismatch.

title: Conflict-Resilient Multi-Agent Reasoning via Signed Graph Modeling
summary: LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS) have demonstrated strong reasoning and decision-making capabilities that consistently surpass those of single LLM agents. However, their performance often suffers from naive aggregation mechanisms that assume uniformly cooperative interactions. Upon close inspection, we observe that existing graph-based MAS frameworks (1) propagate errors when conflicting signals arise without control, and (2) lack explicit modeling of conflicting inter-agent relations as well as structural awareness, failing to identify reliable interaction patterns. To bridge this gap, we introduce SIGMA, a novel SIgned Graph-informed Multi-Agent reasoning framework that explicitly captures trust, conflict, and neutral relations among agents via a signed relational graph. Specifically, given a query, SIGMA first selects a set of relevant and diverse agents, then constructs a structured signed interaction graph with confidence-weighted edges. Reasoning proceeds through conflict-aware signed message passing, which reinforces information from trustworthy agents while suppressing conflicting signals, and terminates with a structure- and conflict-aware weighted aggregation to yield globally consistent and conflict-resilient predictions. Extensive experiments on six benchmark datasets, across multiple LLM backbones and diverse multi-agent configurations, demonstrate that SIGMA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving notable gains in both accuracy and conflict-resilient performance.

AI.tldr: SIGMA is a signed graph-informed multi-agent reasoning framework that explicitly models trust, conflict, and neutral relations among agents to improve reasoning performance and conflict resilience.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-agent systems suffer from naive aggregation mechanisms that assume uniformly cooperative interactions, leading to error propagation and lack of explicit modeling for conflicting inter-agent relations and structural awareness.
AI.method: SIGMA first selects relevant and diverse agents, then constructs a structured signed interaction graph with confidence-weighted edges. Reasoning proceeds through conflict-aware signed message passing that reinforces trustworthy information while suppressing conflicts, ending with structure- and conflict-aware weighted aggregation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on six benchmark datasets across multiple LLM backbones and diverse multi-agent configurations show SIGMA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines with notable gains in both accuracy and conflict-resilient performance.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling signed relations (trust, conflict, neutral) in multi-agent systems through graph-based frameworks like SIGMA significantly improves reasoning capabilities and conflict resilience compared to approaches that assume uniformly cooperative interactions.

title: The Routing and Filtering Structure of Attention
summary: The attention interaction matrix $QK^{\top}$ contains two entangled computations: a skew-symmetric component that redistributes information between positions (routing) and a symmetric component that scales mutual relevance (filtering). We decompose 1776 heads across five pretrained transformers and find routing operating at low rank, well below the routing capacity allocated by the weight kernel. We introduce $S$-$D$ attention as a diagnostic parameterization that disentangles routing from filtering by construction with guaranteed stability ($\mathrm{Re}(λ) \le 0$) and trains stably without layer normalization. When disentangled and unnormalized, routing self-organizes into a spectral cascade, effective rank $2$ at the first layer, expanding with depth across six scales from 7M to 355M parameters. The cascade predicts where attention can be simplified: linearizing the first seven layers of 125M $S$-$D$ attention costs ${<}5\%$ perplexity, whereas standard attention collapses under the same intervention. The linearizable region widens with depth. Replacing the first four layers with ELU+1 linear attention reaches within $1.4\%$ of baseline at full head dimension. Cascade-allocated architectures trade attention parameters for perplexity ($47\%-65\%$ fewer attention parameters at $+3.9\%$ to $+8.4\%$ PPL). The routing-filtering decomposition makes the spectral budget legible; the cascade makes it actionable.

AI.tldr: The paper shows attention has routing and filtering components, introduces disentangled attention that reveals a spectral cascade in routing across layers, enabling attention simplification and parameter reduction.
AI.motivation: Attention mechanisms in transformers combine routing and filtering operations in an entangled way, making it difficult to understand and optimize their separate functions. The authors want to disentangle these components to better analyze and improve attention efficiency.
AI.method: Propose S-D attention that explicitly separates routing from filtering, analyze routing behavior across transformer layers, discover a spectral cascade pattern where routing operates at low rank, and use this insight to simplify attention mechanisms.
AI.result: Found routing operates at low rank well below allocated capacity, routing self-organizes into spectral cascade across layers, enabling significant attention simplification (linearizing early layers) and parameter reduction (47-65% fewer attention parameters) with minimal perplexity impact.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling routing from filtering reveals a structured spectral cascade that makes attention mechanisms more interpretable and optimizable, enabling substantial efficiency gains through targeted architectural simplifications.

title: What and When to Distill: Selective Hindsight Distillation for Multi-Turn Agents
summary: Reinforcement learning can train LLM agents from sparse task rewards, but long-horizon credit assignment remains challenging: a single success-or-failure signal must be distributed across many actions. Existing methods rely on trajectory-level rewards or proxy signals, without fully leveraging per-step environmental feedback. Multi-turn agent settings are underexplored, where feedback can include error messages, page changes, observations, or reference trajectories. We systematically study five feedback sources and two insertion granularities and introduce SERL, a selective environment-reweighted learning framework. SERL uses the task reward to determine update direction, while environment feedback adjusts placement and magnitude, focusing on critical actions. On ALFWorld and WebShop, SERL achieves 90.0% and 80.1% success, outperforming strong RL and distillation baselines. Analysis shows that grounded, action-relevant feedback at meaningful points consistently outperforms indiscriminate use of longer or richer context.

AI.tldr: SERL framework uses selective environment feedback weighting with task rewards for better credit assignment in LLM-based agents, achieving state-of-the-art performance on ALFWorld and WebShop benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning for LLM agents faces long-horizon credit assignment challenges with sparse rewards. Existing methods don't fully leverage per-step environmental feedback available in multi-turn agent settings like error messages, observations, or reference trajectories.
AI.method: SERL (selective environment-reweighted learning framework) systematically studies five feedback sources and two insertion granularities. It uses task reward to determine update direction while environment feedback adjusts placement and magnitude, focusing on critical actions rather than indiscriminate use of context.
AI.result: SERL achieves 90.0% success on ALFWorld and 80.1% success on WebShop, outperforming strong RL and distillation baselines. Grounded, action-relevant feedback at meaningful points consistently outperforms longer or richer context indiscriminately.
AI.conclusion: Selective use of environment feedback for weighting in reinforcement learning improves credit assignment in multi-turn agent settings, demonstrating that quality of feedback placement is more important than quantity of contextual information.

title: Lossless Anti-Distillation Sampling
summary: Frontier commercial generative models face a growing threat from distillation, whereby a distiller harvests generated responses and trains a competing model of its own at drastically lower cost. Existing defenses either rely on modifying the models outputs, thereby sacrificing response quality for benign users, or on behavioral detection methods, which can be readily circumvented by distributing queries across multiple accounts. In this work, we propose Lossless Anti-Distillation Sampling (LADS), a novel sampling scheme specifically designed to counter multi-account distillation while maintaining a lossless experience for benign users. Concretely, LADS derives the randomness underlying each generation from a private seed determined by the semantic content of the query and the number of times the user has queried the model. By construction, every benign user receives a response independently sampled from the original model at each visit, and thus experiences no distortion. In contrast, for a distiller, different accounts share latent randomness whenever their queries fall in the same semantic bucket. As a result, the harvested data becomes correlated, potentially reducing sample diversity and degrading generalization. Using uniform convergence theory, we show that LADS provably degrades the convergence rate of the distillers generalization gap relative to standard i.i.d. sampling in both unconditional and conditional generation settings. Experiments on image generation, mathematical reasoning, and code generation confirm that LADS substantially degrades the performance of distilled students while preserving exact statistical fidelity for individual users.

AI.tldr: A novel sampling defense called LADS protects frontier generative models from multi-account distillation attacks by making responses dependent on query semantics and user history, preserving benign user experience while degrading distilled model performance.
AI.motivation: Frontier commercial generative models are vulnerable to distillation attacks where attackers harvest generated responses to train competing models at lower cost. Existing defenses either degrade response quality for benign users or can be circumvented by distributing queries across multiple accounts.
AI.method: Lossless Anti-Distillation Sampling (LADS) derives randomness for each generation from a private seed determined by semantic content of the query and the number of times the user has queried the model. This makes responses consistent for individual users but correlated across different accounts when queries share semantic similarity.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis using uniform convergence theory shows LADS provably degrades the convergence rate of distillers' generalization gap. Experimental results on image generation, mathematical reasoning, and code generation confirm LADS substantially degrades distilled student performance while preserving exact statistical fidelity for individual users.
AI.conclusion: LADS provides an effective defense against multi-account distillation attacks without compromising user experience, offering a practical solution to protect commercial generative models from unauthorized distillation while maintaining service quality for legitimate users.

title: Generative Auto-Bidding with Unified Modeling and Exploration
summary:
Automated bidding is central to modern digital advertising. Early rule-based methods lacked adaptability, while subsequent Reinforcement Learning approaches modeled bidding as a Markov Decision Process but struggled with long-term dependencies. Recent generative models show promise, yet they lack explicit mechanisms to balance exploration and safety, relying solely on action perturbations or trajectory guidance without a safety fallback. This results in inefficient exploration and elevated financial risk for advertising platforms.
  To address this gap, we propose GUIDE (Generative Auto-Bidding with Unified Modeling and Exploration), a framework that synergistically integrates directed exploration with a safe fallback mechanism. GUIDE employs a Decision Transformer (DT) to jointly model historical bidding actions and environmental state transitions. A Q-value module guides the DT's exploration via regularization constraints, while an Inverse Dynamics Module (IDM) leverages DT-predicted future states to infer robust, behaviorally consistent actions as a safe policy fallback. The Q-value module then adaptively selects the final action between these two options, balancing exploration and safety. Together, these components form an integrated "explore-safeguard-select" pipeline that unifies efficiency and safety.
  We conduct extensive experiments on public datasets, in simulated auction environments, and through large-scale online deployment on Taobao, a leading Chinese advertising platform. Results show GUIDE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across all scenarios. In real-world deployment, GUIDE achieves notable gains: +4.10% ad GMV, +1.40% ad clicks, +1.66% ad cost, and +3.52% ad ROI, demonstrating its effectiveness and strong industrial applicability.

AI.tldr: GUIDE is a new automated bidding framework that combines generative modeling with safety mechanisms to balance exploration and risk in digital advertising auctions, achieving significant performance gains in real-world deployment.
AI.motivation: Existing automated bidding methods have limitations: early rule-based approaches lack adaptability, reinforcement learning struggles with long-term dependencies, and recent generative models fail to properly balance exploration and safety, leading to inefficient exploration and financial risks for advertising platforms.
AI.method: GUIDE uses a Decision Transformer to jointly model historical bidding actions and state transitions. It combines a Q-value module for exploration guidance with an Inverse Dynamics Module that provides a safe policy fallback based on predicted future states. The system has an "explore-safeguard-select" pipeline that adaptively chooses between exploration and safe actions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on public datasets, simulated environments, and real-world deployment on Taobao show GUIDE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. Real deployment results show +4.10% ad GMV, +1.40% ad clicks, +1.66% ad cost, and +3.52% ad ROI improvements.
AI.conclusion: GUIDE successfully addresses the exploration-safety tradeoff in automated bidding through its unified generative framework with integrated safety mechanisms, demonstrating both strong algorithmic performance and significant industrial applicability in real-world advertising platforms.

title: In-Context Learning Operates as Concept Subspace Learning
summary: Regression and Bayesian accounts of in-context learning (ICL) explain how demonstrations can induce predictors, while mechanistic analyses often identify compact activation directions that steer prompted behavior. However, it remains unclear whether structured demonstrations induce low-dimensional concept inference. We study this question through a concept-subspace view of ICL, in which tasks vary only along intrinsic concept coordinates, although inputs are observed in a high-dimensional ambient space. For ridge and least-squares ICL proxies, prediction decomposes exactly into concept-coordinate regression and off-subspace leakage. Under block-diagonal or near-block-diagonal covariance assumptions, the leading estimation and nuisance-sensitivity terms scale with the dimension of the concept subspace, while residual effects are controlled by cross-subspace coupling. This separation gives a mechanistic prediction: recoverable task information should concentrate in a low-dimensional, task-aligned activation subspace. On CounterFact-derived multi-relation prompts with Llama-3-8B, a 68--73-dimensional subspace of the 4096-dimensional residual stream restores 78.8% of the clean--corrupted accuracy gap, whereas patching the complementary subspace restores 0%. Concept swaps redirect predictions toward injected relations, while random and cross-task matched-rank controls are largely ineffective. Additional experiments on Qwen2.5-7B and a controlled cross-lingual rule task show the same qualitative pattern. These results support concept subspaces as compact, task-aligned mediators of recoverable ICL behavior in structured task families, without implying full-circuit recovery.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a "concept-subspace" view of in-context learning where structured demonstrations induce low-dimensional concept inference, with experimental validation showing compact, task-aligned activation subspaces mediate recoverable ICL behavior.
AI.motivation: While regression/Bayesian accounts explain how demonstrations induce predictors and mechanistic analyses find compact activation directions, it's unclear whether structured demonstrations induce low-dimensional concept inference. The paper aims to bridge this gap by studying concept subspaces in ICL.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis: For ridge/least-squares ICL proxies, prediction decomposes into concept-coordinate regression and off-subspace leakage under block-diagonal covariance assumptions. Experimental validation: On CounterFact-derived multi-relation prompts with Llama-3-8B, identified a 68-73-dimensional subspace within the 4096-dimensional residual stream, with concept swaps and controls. Also tested on Qwen2.5-7B and cross-lingual rule tasks.
AI.result: A low-dimensional (68-73D) subspace of the 4096D residual stream restored 78.8% of the clean-corrupted accuracy gap, while patching the complementary subspace restored 0%. Concept swaps successfully redirected predictions toward injected relations, while random and cross-task controls were ineffective. Same qualitative pattern observed across models and tasks.
AI.conclusion: Concept subspaces serve as compact, task-aligned mediators of recoverable ICL behavior in structured task families, providing a mechanistic prediction that recoverable task information concentrates in low-dimensional, task-aligned activation subspaces, without implying full-circuit recovery.

title: Beyond Mode Collapse: Distribution Matching for Diverse Reasoning
summary: On-policy reinforcement learning methods like GRPO suffer from mode collapse: they exhibit reduced solution diversity, concentrating probability mass on a single solution once discovered and ceasing exploration of alternative strategies. We show this stems from reverse KL minimization's mode-seeking behavior, which reinforces the first high-reward trajectory found rather than maintaining a distribution over multiple diverse solutions. We propose DMPO (Distribution-Matching Policy Optimization), which prevents mode collapse through principled approximation of forward KL minimization. DMPO constructs a group level target distribution over sampled trajectories proportional to their rewards, then aligns the policy distribution to this target. This provides mode-covering behavior without requiring sampling from the intractable global target distribution, enabling sustained exploration throughout training. We validate DMPO on NP-hard combinatorial optimization, where exponentially many feasible solutions exist but only a few approach optimality, an ideal testbed for evaluating exploration. DMPO achieves 43.9% Quality Ratio on text-based NP-Bench (vs. GRPO's 40.1%) and 43.1% on vision-based NP-Bench (vs. 38.4%), demonstrating 9% and 12% relative improvements respectively. These gains generalize to mathematical reasoning (+2.0%) and out-of-domain tasks (+2.3%), showing that diversity-preserving training enhances general reasoning capabilities across modalities. Our work establishes distribution matching as a practical, principled approach to preventing mode collapse in on-policy RL, with consistent quality improvements demonstrating sustained exploration across diverse reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: DMPO (Distribution-Matching Policy Optimization) prevents mode collapse in on-policy RL by approximating forward KL minimization, enabling sustained exploration of diverse solutions and improving performance across NP-hard combinatorial optimization, mathematical reasoning, and out-of-domain tasks.
AI.motivation: On-policy RL methods like GRPO suffer from mode collapse - they concentrate on single high-reward solutions once discovered and stop exploring diverse alternative strategies. This is particularly problematic in domains like combinatorial optimization where many solutions exist but few approach optimality, requiring sustained exploration to find diverse high-quality solutions.
AI.method: DMPO prevents mode collapse through principled approximation of forward KL minimization. It constructs a group-level target distribution over sampled trajectories proportional to their rewards, then aligns the policy distribution to this target. This provides mode-covering behavior without requiring sampling from the intractable global target distribution, enabling sustained exploration throughout training.
AI.result: DMPO achieves 43.9% Quality Ratio on text-based NP-Bench (vs. GRPO's 40.1%) and 43.1% on vision-based NP-Bench (vs. 38.4%), showing 9% and 12% relative improvements. Gains generalize to mathematical reasoning (+2.0%) and out-of-domain tasks (+2.3%), demonstrating enhanced reasoning capabilities across modalities.
AI.conclusion: Distribution matching is a practical, principled approach to preventing mode collapse in on-policy RL. DMPO enables sustained exploration throughout training, leading to consistent quality improvements across diverse reasoning tasks and modalities.

title: Precision Tracked Transformer via Kalman Filtering, Kriging and Process Noise
summary: The Transformer is the foundational building block of modern AI, yet offers no principled handling of \emph{uncertainty}, which is prevalent in real applications: cold-start tokens with sparse histories in sequential recommendation, heterogeneous signal quality in language models, and attention sinks induced by unconstrained softmax. Every token is treated with uniform confidence. We show this uniformity is a degenerate case of our \emph{Bayesian Filtering Transformer} (BFT): attention becomes precision-weighted kriging, the residual connection becomes a Kalman update with adaptive gain, and the FFN becomes a dynamics model propagating precision via a Jacobian--plus--process-noise rule. Observation precision comes from a parameter-free Restricted Maximum Likelihood (REML) estimator with a conjugate Bayesian prior. BFT replaces any Transformer layer with negligible overhead. On sequential recommendation, BFT applied to three major architectures yields significant gains on six benchmarks, with the largest improvements on cold-start users and rare items where uncertainty is highest. On supervised fine-tuning of large language models with noisy data, BFT improves robustness in two regimes: noisy supervision (token-label corruption in question answering) and noisy context (retrieval-augmented QA with real RAG distractors). A single principled modification -- restoring precision -- unlocks substantial headroom across both classical sequence-modeling and modern LLM regimes.

AI.tldr: BFT replaces Transformer's uniform token confidence with precision-weighted Bayesian filtering, improving performance on cold-start items, noisy supervision, and retrieval-augmented QA.
AI.motivation: Standard Transformers treat all tokens with uniform confidence, lacking principled uncertainty handling for real-world scenarios like cold-start tokens in sequential recommendation, heterogeneous signal quality in language models, and attention sinks from unconstrained softmax.
AI.method: BFT (Bayesian Filtering Transformer) reinterprets Transformer components probabilistically: attention becomes precision-weighted kriging, residual connection becomes Kalman update with adaptive gain, and FFN becomes dynamics model with precision propagation via Jacobian-plus-process-noise rule. Uses parameter-free REML estimator with conjugate Bayesian prior for observation precision.
AI.result: Significant gains on six sequential recommendation benchmarks with largest improvements on cold-start users and rare items; improved robustness in noisy supervision (token-label corruption in QA) and noisy context (retrieval-augmented QA with real RAG distractors).
AI.conclusion: A single principled modification—replacing uniform confidence with precision-based uncertainty handling—unlocks substantial performance improvements across classical sequence modeling and modern LLM regimes with negligible overhead.

title: Attention-Guided Reward for Reinforcement Learning-based Jailbreak against Large Reasoning Models
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in solving complex problems by generating structured, step-by-step reasoning content. However, exposing a model's internal reasoning process introduces additional safety risks; for example, recent studies show that LRMs are more vulnerable to jailbreak attacks than standard LLMs. In this paper, we investigate jailbreak attacks on LRMs and reveal that the attack success rate (ASR) is closely correlated with LRMs' attention patterns. Specifically, successful jailbreaks tend to assign lower attention to harmful tokens in the input prompt, while allocating higher attention to those tokens in the reasoning content. Motivated by this finding, we propose a novel jailbreak method for LRMs that leverages reinforcement learning (RL) to enhance attack effectiveness, explicitly incorporating attention signals into the reward function design. In addition, we introduce diverse persuasion strategies to enrich the RL action space, which consistently improves the ASR. Extensive experiments on five open-source and closed-source LRMs across three benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves substantially higher ASR, outperforming existing approaches in terms of effectiveness, efficiency, and transferability.

AI.tldr: Using reinforcement learning with attention signals and diverse persuasion strategies to jailbreak Large Reasoning Models more effectively than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) expose more safety risks than standard LLMs due to their structured reasoning, and successful jailbreaks correlate with specific attention patterns (lower attention to harmful tokens in input, higher attention in reasoning).
AI.method: Proposes a novel jailbreak method using reinforcement learning (RL) with attention signals incorporated into reward function design, plus diverse persuasion strategies to enrich the RL action space.
AI.result: Method achieves substantially higher attack success rate (ASR) on five open/closed-source LRMs across three benchmarks, outperforming existing approaches in effectiveness, efficiency, and transferability.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcement learning with attention-guided rewards and persuasion strategies creates more effective jailbreak attacks on LRMs, revealing security vulnerabilities in reasoning-based models.

title: Automated Big Data Quality Assessment using Knowledge Graph Embeddings
summary:
Automated data quality assessment is crucial for managing big data, but existing solutions face challenges in achieving accurate context-aware assessment. This paper presents a novel knowledge-based approach to enhance automated data quality assessment. Our approach utilizes knowledge graph embeddings to predict missing edges between the input dataset's context representation and the relevant quality rules and dimensions within a knowledge graph representing contextual data characteristics and the required quality assessment operations. We surpass conventional practices by integrating diverse representations within the knowledge graph, drawing insights from contextual information from a thorough literature investigation. This integration allows us to develop a comprehensive and context-specific data quality assessment plan tailored to each context. Leveraging the knowledge graph improves our understanding of the input dataset's context, overcoming the limitations of traditional methods that rely solely on strict matching and overlook contextual characteristics. By injecting numerical edge attributes, we assign corresponding weights to each predicted quality measurement, providing a comprehensive data quality assessment plan for the input dataset.
  To evaluate our approach, we leverage AmpliGraph, a framework developed and benchmarked by AccentureLabs. The evaluation involves employing a real-world radiation sensors dataset provided by the Lebanese Atomic Energy Commission (LAEC-CNRS). The results obtained from this evaluation demonstrate the capability of our solution to generate a comprehensive data quality assessment plan for the given input dataset.

AI.tldr: A knowledge-based approach using knowledge graph embeddings to enhance automated data quality assessment by predicting missing relationships between dataset contexts and quality rules, overcoming limitations of traditional context-insensitive methods.
AI.motivation: Existing automated data quality assessment solutions struggle with achieving accurate context-aware assessment. Traditional methods rely on strict matching and overlook contextual characteristics, limiting their effectiveness in real-world big data scenarios.
AI.method: Utilizes knowledge graph embeddings to predict missing edges between input dataset context representations and relevant quality rules/dimensions within a knowledge graph. Integrates diverse representations from literature insights, injects numerical edge attributes for weighted quality measurements, and develops comprehensive context-specific assessment plans.
AI.result: Evaluation using AmpliGraph framework and real-world radiation sensors dataset from Lebanese Atomic Energy Commission demonstrates the solution's capability to generate comprehensive data quality assessment plans tailored to specific dataset contexts.
AI.conclusion: The knowledge-based approach successfully enhances automated data quality assessment by overcoming limitations of traditional methods through contextual understanding, providing a more comprehensive and context-specific assessment framework.

title: Position: The Turing-Completeness of Real-World Autoregressive Transformers Relies Heavily on Context Management
comment: Accepted to the ICML 2026 Position Paper Track
summary: Many works make the eye-catching claim that Transformers are Turing-complete. However, the literature often conflates two distinct settings: (i) a fixed Transformer system setting, in which a fixed autoregressive Transformer is coupled with a fixed context-management method to process inputs of different lengths step by step, and (ii) a scaling-family setting, in which a family of different models (with increasing context-window length or numerical precision) is used to handle different input lengths. Existing proofs of Transformer Turing-completeness are frequently established in setting (ii), whereas real-world LLM deployment and the standard notion of Turing-completeness correspond more naturally to setting (i). In this paper, we first formalize the fixed-system setting, thereby providing a concrete characterization of how real-world LLMs operate. We then argue that results proved in the scaling-family setting provide theoretically meaningful resource bounds but do not establish Turing-completeness, thereby clarifying a common misinterpretation of existing results. Finally, we show that different context-management methods can yield sharply different computational power, and we advocate the position that context management is a central component that critically determines the computational power of real-world autoregressive Transformers.

AI.tldr: Transformers are often claimed to be Turing-complete, but existing proofs rely on scaling families of models rather than fixed systems as used in practice, highlighting that context management methods crucially determine computational power.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses confusion in literature about Transformer Turing-completeness claims. Many works conflate two distinct settings: (1) fixed Transformer systems (like real LLMs) and (2) scaling families of models. Existing proofs use setting (2) but real-world deployment and proper Turing-completeness require setting (1).
AI.method: First formalizes the fixed-system setting to characterize real-world LLM operation. Then analyzes the scaling-family setting vs. fixed-system distinction. Finally examines how different context-management methods affect computational power in autoregressive Transformers.
AI.result: Shows that results from scaling-family setting provide theoretical resource bounds but don't establish true Turing-completeness. Demonstrates that context-management methods can yield sharply different computational power, making context management a central determinant of computational power in real Transformers.
AI.conclusion: Context management is a critical component that fundamentally determines the computational power of real-world autoregressive Transformers. Existing Turing-completeness claims are often misinterpreted because they don't properly address the fixed-system setting used in practice.

title: StampFormer: A Physics-Guided Material-Geometry-Coupled Multimodal Model for Rapid Prediction of Physical Fields in Sheet Metal Stamping
summary: Traditional sheet metal forming relies on time-consuming and expensive Finite Element Analysis (FEA) for design validation, a process that significantly prolongs design cycles. While surrogate models offer faster iteration, current approaches have limitations: scalar-based methods cannot capture comprehensive field-based FEA results, while existing image-based models often ignore the critical role of material properties by focusing solely on geometry. To address this gap, we develop a physics-guided deep learning framework, namely StampFormer, which simultaneously uses component geometry and material stress-strain responses to predict FEA outcomes. The StampFormer framework uses three core components to process data. A Material-Augmented Geometric Network (MAGN) first fuses geometric and material data. This information is then integrated at various levels by a Hierarchical Material Embedding Injection Unit (HMEIU) before being processed by the primary network backbone, an adapted Swin-UNet. We evaluated our model on the stamping of a crossmember panel with two simulation datasets for steel and aluminium panels, and results demonstrate that StampFormer provides high-fidelity predictions of critical physical fields - including thinning, major strain, minor strain, plastic strain, and displacement - in under a second. Compared with ground truth FEA, our model achieved an average relative error of less than 8.5% on the four 2D fields and a mean squared error of less than 1.2 mm2 for the 3D displacement field. In summary, we introduce a practical and efficient framework that integrates multimodal information, namely geometry and material properties, to provide fast and accurate predictions, enabling designers to perform real-time manufacturability assessments.

AI.tldr: StampFormer is a physics-guided deep learning framework that predicts sheet metal forming simulation results by simultaneously processing component geometry and material stress-strain responses, achieving high-fidelity predictions in under a second.
AI.motivation: Traditional FEA for sheet metal forming is time-consuming and expensive, prolonging design cycles. Existing surrogate models have limitations: scalar-based methods can't capture comprehensive field-based results, while image-based models ignore material properties by focusing only on geometry.
AI.method: StampFormer integrates geometry and material properties using three core components: Material-Augmented Geometric Network (MAGN) for fusing geometric and material data, Hierarchical Material Embedding Injection Unit (HMEIU) for multi-level integration, and adapted Swin-UNet as the primary network backbone.
AI.result: The model achieved high-fidelity predictions of critical physical fields (thinning, major/minor strain, plastic strain, displacement) in under a second. Average relative error was <8.5% for 2D fields and MSE <1.2 mm² for 3D displacement field compared to ground truth FEA on steel and aluminum stamping datasets.
AI.conclusion: StampFormer provides a practical, efficient framework for real-time manufacturability assessments by integrating multimodal geometry and material property information for fast, accurate predictions, overcoming limitations of traditional FEA and existing surrogate models.

title: BLINKG: A Benchmark for LLM-Integrated Knowledge Graph Generation
summary: Generating Knowledge Graphs (KGs) remains one of the most time-consuming and labor-intensive tasks for knowledge engineers, as they need to identify semantic equivalences between input data sources and ontology terms. While declarative solutions (e.g., RML, SPARQL-Anything) have helped to generalize this process, aligning input schema elements with ontology terms still involves intricate transformations and requires considerable manual effort. With the advent of Large Language Models (LLMs), there is growing interest in leveraging their capabilities to assist KG engineers. Although some studies have explored using LLMs to automate KG construction, there is still no standardized framework for assessing how effectively they establish correspondences between data schemes and ontology concepts. Therefore, in this paper, we propose BLINKG, a benchmark designed to evaluate the mapping capabilities of LLMs in constructing KGs from heterogeneous data sources. The benchmark includes a set of scenarios with increasing complexity, based on real-world use cases. We conduct an extensive experimental evaluation of several stateof-the-art LLMs using BLINK and observe that they already offer promising solutions. However, their performance remains limited in complex scenarios. Thanks to this benchmark, we can already assess the current capabilities of LLMs for KG construction. Additionally, we define a set of requirements for achieving (semi)automated (LLM-driven) KG construction, opening new research lines in this area.

AI.tldr: BLINKG is a benchmark for evaluating LLMs' ability to map data sources to ontology terms for Knowledge Graph construction, showing promising results but limitations in complex scenarios.
AI.motivation: Knowledge Graph construction is labor-intensive despite declarative solutions like RML and SPARQL-Anything. While LLMs show potential for automating this process, there's no standardized framework to assess their mapping capabilities between data schemes and ontology concepts.
AI.method: Proposed BLINKG benchmark with increasing complexity scenarios based on real-world use cases. Conducted extensive experimental evaluation of state-of-the-art LLMs using this benchmark.
AI.result: LLMs show promising solutions for KG construction, but performance remains limited in complex scenarios. The benchmark enables assessment of current LLM capabilities and identifies gaps.
AI.conclusion: BLINKG provides a framework to evaluate LLMs for KG construction and defines requirements for achieving (semi)automated LLM-driven KG construction, opening new research directions.

title: Spectral Gradient Surgery for Domain-Generalizable Dataset Distillation
summary: Dataset Distillation (DD) synthesizes a compact synthetic dataset that preserves the training utility of a full dataset. However, its standard formulation assumes that test data follow the same distribution as training data, an assumption that rarely holds in practice. A straightforward extension-applying post-hoc Domain Generalization (DG) techniques to distilled data-is ill-suited because existing DG methods rely on the natural diversity of real datasets, which compact synthetic sets inherently lack, while also incurring substantial augmentation overhead that conflicts with the efficiency objective of dataset distillation. To address this limitation, we introduce Domain Generalizable Dataset Distillation (DGDD), a new problem setting that explicitly targets out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization of distilled datasets. We study this problem through a widely adopted DD baseline of Distribution Matching (DM). We attribute the OOD vulnerability of DM to the entanglement of class-discriminative and domain-specific information within the compressed synthetic set, and propose Spectral Gradient Surgery (SGS) to disentangle the two. The key insight of SGS is that cross-domain agreement among domain-wise gradients in the spectral domain reveals which gradient components are shared across source domains-and are therefore class-discriminative-and which are domain-specific. Based on this observation, SGS augments the standard DM update with two complementary gradients: one that reinforces cross-domain shared components and another that explicitly promotes diversity within the distilled dataset. Extensive experiments on diverse-scale benchmarks demonstrate that SGS substantially improves OOD generalization while remaining plug-and-play compatible with existing DM methods.

AI.tldr: DGDD introduces domain generalizable dataset distillation using Spectral Gradient Surgery to improve out-of-distribution generalization while maintaining dataset compression efficiency.
AI.motivation: Standard dataset distillation assumes same distribution for training and test data, which rarely holds in practice. Existing domain generalization methods require natural data diversity that compressed synthetic datasets lack, and their augmentation overhead conflicts with dataset distillation's efficiency goals.
AI.method: Proposes Spectral Gradient Surgery (SGS) that analyzes cross-domain gradient agreement in spectral domain to disentangle class-discriminative vs domain-specific information. SGS adds two complementary gradient updates: reinforcing cross-domain shared components and promoting diversity within distilled dataset.
AI.result: SGS substantially improves out-of-distribution generalization across diverse-scale benchmarks while remaining plug-and-play compatible with existing distribution matching methods.
AI.conclusion: DGDD framework successfully addresses OOD generalization in dataset distillation through principled disentanglement of class-discriminative and domain-specific information using spectral gradient analysis.

title: Efficient Elicitation of Collective Disagreements
summary: We analyze the structure of the disagreement among a population of voters over a set of alternatives. Surveys typically ask either for pairwise comparisons, simple and intuitive for participants, or full rankings over alternatives, eliciting the entire voters' preferences. Building on the observation that pairwise comparisons cannot distinguish structural disagreement from noise, we propose a stratified framework to identify the minimal aggregated preference information needed to compute a number of disagreement measures from the literature. Specifically, we introduce the plurality matrix, a generalization of pairwise comparisons that records, for every subset $S$ of alternatives, the probability that each $a \in S$ ranks first in $S$. We define the level of a disagreement measure as the smallest subset size needed to express it, showing that many existing notions, including rank-variance and divisiveness, sit at level $3$, proving that pairwise comparisons are not enough. In addition, we demonstrate the interest of going beyond level $3$ both theoretically and experimentally. To make these results actionable, we design two elicitation protocols to estimate the plurality matrix, exploring the trade-off between the number of required participants and the cognitive load requested to each of them.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a stratified framework for measuring disagreement in voting populations, showing that pairwise comparisons are insufficient and introducing the plurality matrix that records ranking probabilities to capture structural disagreement.
AI.motivation: Current voting survey methods have limitations: pairwise comparisons are simple but cannot distinguish structural disagreement from noise, while full rankings are cognitively demanding. There's a need for a framework that identifies the minimal preference information needed to accurately measure disagreement.
AI.method: The authors introduce the plurality matrix - a generalization of pairwise comparisons that records, for every subset S of alternatives, the probability that each alternative ranks first in S. They define the "level" of a disagreement measure as the smallest subset size needed to express it, and design two elicitation protocols to estimate the plurality matrix while balancing participant load and sample size.
AI.result: The paper shows that many existing disagreement measures (including rank-variance and divisiveness) require level 3, proving pairwise comparisons (level 2) are insufficient. Both theoretical analysis and experiments demonstrate the value of going beyond level 3 for accurate disagreement measurement.
AI.conclusion: The stratified framework and plurality matrix provide a principled approach to measuring disagreement that bridges the gap between simple but insufficient pairwise comparisons and cognitively demanding full rankings, with practical elicitation protocols that optimize the trade-off between participant burden and measurement accuracy.

title: VCR: Learning Valid Contextual Representation for Incomplete Wearable Signals
summary: Wearable devices enable continuous health monitoring from multimodal signals, but real-world deployment is hindered by limited labeled data and pervasive sensor incompleteness. While large-scale self-supervised pretraining reduces label dependence, most existing methods assume full modality availability. Current approaches for handling modality missingness often reconstruct entire absent signals, which can encourage hallucinating modality-specific details that are not inferable from the observed sensor signals and degrade robustness. We propose VCR, a self-supervised framework that learns to extract valid representations robust to modality missingness. VCR employs an orthogonal tokenizer to enforce strict orthogonal disentanglement by rectifying latent manifolds and applying a geometric projection, separating each modality into shared semantics and modality-specific residuals. This design preserves complete information integrity while serving as a structural foundation for robust learning under modality missingness. The resulting tokens are processed by a missing-aware mixture-of-experts backbone that adapts to varying patterns of modality availability. By constraining the objective to reconstruct only the shared components of missing modalities, VCR effectively mitigates hallucinations of non-inferable modality-specific details. Across multiple health monitoring tasks, VCR consistently improves performance and robustness under full, single-missing, and multiple-missing modality settings compared with strong supervised and self-supervised baselines.

AI.tldr: VCR is a self-supervised framework for robust multimodal health monitoring that handles missing modalities by disentangling shared semantics from modality-specific details, preventing hallucination while maintaining information integrity.
AI.motivation: Real-world wearable health monitoring faces two key challenges: limited labeled data and pervasive sensor incompleteness/missing modalities. While self-supervised pretraining helps with label scarcity, most methods assume full modality availability, and current approaches for handling missing modalities often reconstruct entire absent signals, leading to hallucination of non-inferable details and reduced robustness.
AI.method: VCR uses an orthogonal tokenizer to enforce strict orthogonal disentanglement through latent manifold rectification and geometric projection, separating each modality into shared semantics and modality-specific residuals. This preserves complete information while providing structural robustness. The tokens are processed by a missing-aware mixture-of-experts backbone that adapts to varying modality availability patterns. The framework constrains reconstruction to only shared components of missing modalities, preventing hallucination.
AI.result: Across multiple health monitoring tasks, VCR consistently improves performance and robustness under full modality, single-missing modality, and multiple-missing modality settings compared to strong supervised and self-supervised baselines.
AI.conclusion: VCR provides an effective self-supervised framework for robust multimodal health monitoring that handles modality missingness by learning valid representations through orthogonal disentanglement and constrained reconstruction, avoiding hallucination while maintaining performance across varying modality availability scenarios.

title: Generative-Evaluative Agreement: A Necessary Validity Criterion for LLM-Enabled Adaptive Assessment
summary: When the same LLM generates assessment items, simulates student responses, and scores them, the validation loop is self-referential. We introduce Generative-Evaluative Agreement (GEA), a validity criterion measuring whether an LLM's scoring function recovers the skill levels its generative function was instructed to produce. In the first direct measurement of GEA on a two-stage adaptive assessment, the model recovers roughly half the intended variance r = 0.698 with systematic positive bias. GEA is strong r > 0.7 for syntactically verifiable skills but near zero for design-level skills, and low-skill overestimation inflates scores near the routing threshold. We argue that granular, skill-decomposed rubrics are the principal proposed mechanism for strengthening GEA and outline complementary mitigations.

AI.tldr: LLMs create assessment items, simulate student responses, and score them - creating a self-referential validation loop. The paper introduces Generative-Evaluative Agreement (GEA) to measure whether LLM scoring recovers intended skill levels, finding only partial recovery with systematic bias.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the fundamental validity problem when LLMs are used in educational assessment loops where the same model both generates assessment items/simulates responses and scores them, creating a self-referential validation loop that lacks external verification.
AI.method: Introduces Generative-Evaluative Agreement (GEA) as a validity criterion measuring whether an LLM's scoring function recovers the skill levels its generative function was instructed to produce. Conducts the first direct measurement of GEA on a two-stage adaptive assessment.
AI.result: The model recovers roughly half the intended variance (r = 0.698) with systematic positive bias. GEA is strong (r > 0.7) for syntactically verifiable skills but near zero for design-level skills. Low-skill overestimation inflates scores near routing thresholds.
AI.conclusion: Granular, skill-decomposed rubrics are the principal proposed mechanism for strengthening GEA. The paper argues for this approach and outlines complementary mitigations to address the validity issues in LLM-based educational assessment systems.

title: Lying Is Just a Phase: The Hidden Alignment Transition in Language Model Scaling
comment: 15 pages, 8 figures, 2 tables. Companion paper: "The Growing Pains of Frontier Models: When Leaderboards Stop Separating and What to Measure Next." Code: https://github.com/adilamin89/cape-scaling. Dashboard: https://zehenlabs.com/cape/
summary: Scaling laws predict loss from compute but not how capabilities interact. We measure the coupling between reasoning and truthfulness across 63 base models from 16 families and find a regime change invisible to loss curves: below a family-dependent critical scale $N_c$, capabilities anticorrelate; above it, they cooperate. $N_c \approx 3.5$B parameters [2.9B, 13.4B] (bootstrap 95% CI), but model size is not the only variable that determines phase. Architecture, data curation, and training recipe each shift $N_c$ independently: curated training eliminated the coupling dip between Qwen generations ($0.025 \to 0.830$ at matched scale), Gemma-4 at 4B achieves coupling 0.871, characteristic of 13B+ standard-trained models, through distillation and architectural innovation, and Phi at 1B matches web-trained coupling at 10B through data curation alone. Width normalization eliminates the anticorrelation across all tested families, supporting an output-projection bottleneck. Internally, 38 of 40 models show zero competing attention heads. A sparse-regression ODE cross-predicts held-out Llama-2 at 5.6% error. The diagnostic requires no model internals -- only public benchmark scores across a model family. The cooperative regime extends to the frontier ($r = +0.72$, 34 models, 10 labs). Code, data, and an open-source activation-steering tool for any open-weight model are released alongside an interactive dashboard that diagnoses any model's coupling phase, suggests concrete interventions (data curation, width, benchmark rotation), and provides ODE scaling predictions, frontier diagnostics, and eigenstructure analysis: https://zehenlabs.com/cape/.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a critical scaling threshold in AI models where reasoning and truthfulness capabilities transition from anticorrelating to cooperating, with this phase transition detectable through public benchmark scores.
AI.motivation: Current scaling laws predict loss from compute but fail to capture how different capabilities interact. The authors aim to understand the coupling between reasoning and truthfulness across model scales and identify invisible regime changes not evident from loss curves alone.
AI.method: Analyzed 63 base models from 16 families, measuring reasoning-truthfulness coupling. Used bootstrap confidence intervals for critical scale N_c. Investigated effects of architecture, data curation, and training recipes. Applied width normalization and sparse-regression ODE to predict held-out models. Required only public benchmark scores, not model internals.
AI.result: Found a family-dependent critical scale N_c ≈ 3.5B parameters (95% CI [2.9B, 13.4B]) where capabilities transition from anticorrelating to cooperating. Factors like data curation, distillation, and architectural innovations can shift N_c independently. Width normalization eliminates anticorrelation across families. The cooperative regime extends to frontier models (r = +0.72 across 34 models from 10 labs).
AI.conclusion: Capabilities undergo phase transitions invisible to standard loss metrics, with cooperative regimes achievable through various interventions. The paper provides a diagnostic tool using only public benchmarks and offers actionable interventions (data curation, width adjustment, benchmark rotation) to improve capability coupling.

title: Library Drift: Diagnosing and Fixing a Silent Failure Mode in Self-Evolving LLM Skill Libraries
summary: Self-evolving skill libraries face a silent failure mode we term \emph{library drift}: unbounded skill accumulation without outcome-driven lifecycle management causes retrieval degradation, false-positive injections, and performance stagnation. Recent evaluation confirms the symptom--LLM-authored skills deliver +0.0pp gain while human-curated ones deliver +16.2pp (SkillsBench)--yet the underlying mechanism has not been isolated. We provide (1) a reproducible trigger: ablations that isolate drift--one disables skill injection (flat floor, +0.002), one imposes premature retirement (active harm, $-$0.019); (2) trace-level diagnostics: an append-only evidence log with per-skill contribution scores, attribution verdicts, and router engagement metrics that make the failure visible before it reaches end-task scores; and (3) a verified fix: a minimal governance recipe (outcome-driven retirement + bounded active-cap + meta-skill authoring prior) that lifts held-out pass@1 from a 0.258 baseline to a late-window mean of 0.584 (rolling gain $+$0.328) on MBPP+ hard-100 over 100 rounds. Eight ablations decompose which governance mechanisms are load-bearing and which are subsumed, providing a concrete playbook for diagnosing library drift in any self-evolving agent.

AI.tldr: Paper identifies "library drift" in self-evolving skill libraries where unbounded skill accumulation causes performance degradation, provides diagnostics, and proposes governance mechanisms that improve performance significantly.
AI.motivation: Self-evolving skill libraries face "library drift" - a silent failure mode where continuous skill accumulation without lifecycle management leads to retrieval degradation, false-positive injections, and performance stagnation. Recent evaluations show LLM-authored skills provide zero gain while human-curated ones deliver significant improvement, but the underlying mechanism hasn't been isolated.
AI.method: 1) Created reproducible triggers for drift through ablation studies, 2) Developed trace-level diagnostics with append-only evidence logs featuring per-skill contribution scores, attribution verdicts, and router engagement metrics, 3) Designed a minimal governance recipe including outcome-driven retirement, bounded active capacity, and meta-skill authoring priors, tested through 100 rounds on MBPP+ hard-100.
AI.result: Governance mechanism lifted held-out pass@1 from 0.258 baseline to 0.584 late-window mean (rolling gain +0.328) on MBPP+ hard-100. Ablation studies showed: disabling skill injection creates flat floor (+0.002), premature retirement causes active harm (-0.019), and isolated the specific governance mechanisms that are load-bearing.
AI.conclusion: Library drift is a critical failure mode in self-evolving skill libraries that can be diagnosed through specific metrics and mitigated with minimal governance mechanisms. The paper provides concrete tools for isolating drift effects and practical governance recipes that substantially improve performance, offering a playbook for diagnosing and fixing library drift in any self-evolving agent system.

title: An Integrated Forecasting Prototype for Emergency Department Boarding Time to Support Proactive Operational Decision Making
