AI.result: No condensation strategy significantly altered hypothesis quality across tasks. LLM-based condensers increased token costs by 24-94%, while masking tool-call outputs achieved 8.6% net savings. The optimal condenser varied by scientific domain and task length.
AI.conclusion: Memory condensation strategies don't substantially impact hypothesis quality in scientific discovery tasks, but they significantly affect costs. The optimal strategy depends on the scientific domain and task characteristics, with masking tool-call outputs offering cost savings while LLM-based methods substantially increase expenses.

title: Minimax Optimal Variance-Aware Regret Bounds for Multinomial Logistic MDPs
summary: We study reinforcement learning for episodic Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) whose transitions are modelled by a multinomial logistic (MNL) model. Existing algorithms for MNL mixture MDPs yield a regret of $\smash{\tilde{O}(dH^2\sqrt{T})}$ (Li et al., 2024), where $d$ is the feature dimension, $H$ the episode length, and $T$ the number of episodes. Inspired by the logistic bandit literature (Abeille et al., 2021; Faury et al., 2022; Boudart et al., 2026), we introduce a problem-dependent constant $\barσ\_T \leq 1/2$, measuring the normalised average variance of the optimal downstream value function along the learner's trajectory. We propose an algorithm achieving a regret of $\smash{\tilde{O}(dH^2\barσ\_T\sqrt{T})}$, which recovers the existing bound in the worst case and improves upon it for structured MDPs. For instance, for KL-constrained robust MDPs, $\barσ\_T = O(H^{-1})$, reducing the horizon dependence by a factor $H$. We further establish a matching $\smash{Ω(dH^2\barσ\_T\sqrt{T})}$ lower bound, proving minimax optimality (up to logarithmic factors) and fully characterising the regret complexity of MNL mixture MDPs for the first time.

AI.tldr: Improved regret bound for episodic MNL mixture MDPs using problem-dependent variance constant, achieving minimax optimality with better horizon dependence for structured MDPs.
AI.motivation: Existing algorithms for MNL mixture MDPs achieve O(dH²√T) regret, but this worst-case bound may be loose for structured MDPs where transitions have specific properties.
AI.method: Introduce problem-dependent constant σ̄_T measuring normalized average variance of optimal downstream value function. Design algorithm leveraging logistic bandit techniques to achieve regret O(dH²σ̄_T√T).
AI.result: Achieves regret O(dH²σ̄_T√T) which improves over existing bound for structured MDPs (e.g., σ̄_T = O(H⁻¹) for KL-constrained robust MDPs). Proves matching Ω(dH²σ̄_T√T) lower bound, establishing minimax optimality.
AI.conclusion: First complete characterization of regret complexity for MNL mixture MDPs with minimax optimal algorithm that adapts to problem structure through variance constant.

title: Delta Attention Residuals
summary: Attention Residuals replace standard additive residual connections with learned softmax attention over previous layer outputs, enabling selective cross-layer routing. However, standard Attention Residuals still attend over cumulative hidden states in previous layers, which are highly redundant. We show that this redundancy leads to routing collapse in deeper layers: attention weights become low-contrast and closer to uniform (max weight ${\approx}$0.2), limiting the model's ability to select informative states in previous layers. This raises a key but underexplored design question: what layer-wise representations should be routed in Attention Residuals? To answer this question, we propose Delta Attention Residuals, which attend over deltas -- the change introduced by each sublayer ($\mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{h}_{i+1} - \mathbf{h}_i$) -- instead of cumulative states. Delta representations are structurally diverse and yield higher-contrast attention distributions (max weight ${\approx}$0.6), enabling more selective and effective routing across layers. This principle applies at both per-sublayer and block granularity. Across all tested scales (220M--7.6B), Delta Attention Residuals consistently outperform both standard residuals and Attention Residuals, with 1.7--8.2\% validation perplexity gains. Delta Attention Residuals also enables converting pretrained checkpoints into Delta Attention Residuals via standard fine-tuning. Code is available at https://github.com/wdlctc/delta-attention-residuals-code.

AI.tldr: Delta Attention Residuals replace standard Attention Residuals by attending to layer deltas (changes) instead of cumulative states, improving routing selectivity and performance.
AI.motivation: Standard Attention Residuals attend over redundant cumulative hidden states, leading to routing collapse in deeper layers where attention weights become low-contrast and nearly uniform (max weight ≈0.2), limiting selective routing capabilities.
AI.method: Proposes Delta Attention Residuals that attend over deltas (the change introduced by each sublayer: v_i = h_{i+1} - h_i) instead of cumulative states. These delta representations are structurally diverse and yield higher-contrast attention distributions (max weight ≈0.6). The approach works at both per-sublayer and block granularity.
AI.result: Across all tested scales (220M--7.6B), Delta Attention Residuals consistently outperform both standard residuals and Attention Residuals, achieving 1.7--8.2% validation perplexity gains. The method also enables converting pretrained checkpoints into Delta Attention Residuals via standard fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Delta representations provide more diverse and informative states for cross-layer routing in Attention Residuals, solving the routing collapse problem and enabling more selective and effective routing across layers for improved model performance.

title: OpenComputer: Verifiable Software Worlds for Computer-Use Agents
summary: We present OpenComputer, a verifier-grounded framework for constructing verifiable software worlds for computer-use agents. OpenComputer integrates four components: (1) app-specific state verifiers that expose structured inspection endpoints over real applications, (2) a self-evolving verification layer that improves verifier reliability using execution-grounded feedback, (3) a task-generation pipeline that synthesizes realistic and machine-checkable desktop tasks, and (4) an evaluation harness that records full trajectories and computes auditable partial-credit rewards. In its current form, OpenComputer covers 33 desktop applications and 1,000 finalized tasks spanning browsers, office tools, creative software, development environments, file managers, and communication applications. Experiments show that OpenComputer's hard-coded verifiers align more closely with human adjudication than LLM-as-judge evaluation, especially when success depends on fine-grained application state. Frontier agents struggle with end-to-end completion despite partial progress, and open-source models exhibit sharp drops from their OSWorld-Verified scores, exposing a persistent gap in robust computer automation.

AI.tldr: OpenComputer is a verifier-grounded framework for creating verifiable software environments for computer-use agents, featuring application state verifiers, self-evolving verification, task synthesis, and evaluation tools to assess agent performance on real desktop applications.
AI.motivation: There's a need for robust frameworks to evaluate computer-use agents on real desktop applications with verifiable outcomes, addressing the gap in reliable assessment methods for automated computer tasks beyond simple benchmarks.
AI.method: The framework integrates four components: (1) app-specific state verifiers with structured inspection endpoints, (2) self-evolving verification layer using execution feedback, (3) task-generation pipeline for realistic machine-checkable desktop tasks, and (4) evaluation harness recording full trajectories with auditable partial-credit rewards.
AI.result: OpenComputer covers 33 desktop applications and 1,000 finalized tasks across multiple categories. Hard-coded verifiers align better with human adjudication than LLM-as-judge, especially for fine-grained state assessment. Frontier agents struggle with end-to-end completion despite partial progress, and open-source models show significant performance drops from their OSWorld-Verified scores.
AI.conclusion: OpenComputer provides a verifiable framework for assessing computer-use agents, revealing persistent gaps in robust computer automation and demonstrating the importance of application-specific verifiers over general LLM-based evaluation methods for accurate performance assessment.

title: SPHERICAL KV: Angle-Domain Attention and Rate-Distortion Retention for Efficient Long-Context Inference
summary:
Long-context inference is increasingly constrained by the KV cache: resident memory grows with context length, and decoding becomes limited by repeated High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) streaming rather than arithmetic. Existing methods such as eviction, windowing, quantization, and offloading reduce footprint, but often leave the critical-path bottleneck only partially addressed, especially when compressed states must still be reconstructed into dense vectors during decoding.
  We present Spherical KV, a long-context inference method that treats KV allocation as a rate-distortion problem grounded in attention geometry for efficient decoding. The method is built on two ideas: (i) represent directional information cheaply in the decode hot loop, and (ii) allocate retention and precision according to estimated future utility. Its first component, Angle-Domain Attention (ADA), stores keys in a spherical parameterization consisting of a scalar radius and compact angle codes, and computes attention logits directly from these codes without reconstructing dense keys. This preserves a paged, block-local, fusion-friendly decode path and directly targets HBM traffic in realistic serving settings. Its second component, Rate-Distortion Retention (RDR), jointly chooses keep/drop decisions and precision tiers per token and head under a fixed budget, producing tier-homogeneous pages with lightweight metadata and coalesced reads. Together, ADA and RDR provide a deployment-oriented mechanism for reducing KV residency while preserving decode efficiency.

AI.tldr: Spherical KV: A long-context inference method that treats KV cache as a rate-distortion problem, using compact angular representations and utility-based retention/precision allocation to reduce memory footprint while maintaining decode efficiency.
AI.motivation: Long-context inference is bottlenecked by KV cache memory growth and HBM bandwidth limitations. Existing methods (eviction, windowing, quantization, offloading) reduce footprint but don't fully solve the critical-path bottleneck during decoding, especially when compressed states must be reconstructed into dense vectors.
AI.method: Two main components: 1) Angle-Domain Attention (ADA): Stores keys in spherical parameterization (scalar radius + compact angle codes), computes attention logits directly from angle codes without reconstructing dense keys, preserving paged/block-local decode path. 2) Rate-Distortion Retention (RDR): Jointly chooses keep/drop decisions and precision tiers per token/head under fixed budget, producing tier-homogeneous pages with lightweight metadata and coalesced reads.
AI.result: The method provides a deployment-oriented mechanism for reducing KV residency while preserving decode efficiency, directly targeting HBM traffic reduction in realistic serving settings.
AI.conclusion: Spherical KV addresses long-context inference bottlenecks by treating KV allocation as a rate-distortion problem, using geometric attention principles to efficiently allocate resources while maintaining performance, providing a practical solution for memory-constrained long-context decoding.

title: Distribution-Free Uncertainty Quantification for Continuous AI Agent Evaluation
comment: 6 pages, 7 figures, 2 tables. Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Agentic Uncertainty Quantification (AgenticUQ) - Poster
summary: We adapt split conformal prediction and adaptive conformal inference (ACI) to continuous AI agent evaluation, providing distribution-free coverage guarantees for forecasted quality scores. Conformal intervals achieve calibration error below 0.02 across all nominal levels at the 24h horizon, while ACI correctly widens intervals by 35% following agent releases then reconverges. We further develop compositional uncertainty bounds for multi-agent pipelines (validated via simulation across inter-stage correlations rho in [-0.5, 0.9]), a conformal abstention rule for pairwise rankings with controlled false-ranking rate, and FDR-corrected abstention for leaderboard-scale multiple testing. Evaluating 50 agents via 18 real-time signals collected hourly, we show that per-agent conditional coverage is well-concentrated around the nominal level (mean 80.4%, 90% of agents within [72%, 90%]), and that cross-source sentiment divergence predicts ranking instability (r=0.64, p<0.01). A circularity-controlled validation confirms the framework captures signal beyond benchmarks (rho_s=0.52, p<0.01, n=35). Code and data are released under CC BY 4.0.

AI.tldr: This paper applies conformal prediction methods to provide statistical guarantees for continuous evaluation of AI agents, achieving calibrated uncertainty intervals and developing compositional bounds for multi-agent systems.
AI.motivation: There's a need for statistically rigorous, continuous evaluation of AI agents that provides distribution-free coverage guarantees for forecasted quality scores, particularly for real-time monitoring of agent performance with uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: The authors adapt split conformal prediction and adaptive conformal inference (ACI) to continuous AI agent evaluation, develop compositional uncertainty bounds for multi-agent pipelines, create conformal abstention rules for pairwise rankings, and apply FDR-corrected abstention for multiple testing.
AI.result: Conformal intervals achieve calibration error below 0.02 at 24h horizon; ACI correctly widens intervals by 35% after agent releases; compositional bounds validated across inter-stage correlations; per-agent conditional coverage well-concentrated; cross-source sentiment divergence predicts ranking instability; framework captures signal beyond benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The conformal prediction framework provides reliable statistical guarantees for continuous AI agent evaluation with distribution-free coverage, enabling practical uncertainty quantification, multi-agent pipeline analysis, and controlled error rates in real-world deployment scenarios.

title: When Individually Calibrated Models Become Collectively Miscalibrated
comment: 42 pages, 1 main figure, multiple tables. Accepted at ProbML 2026
summary:
Probabilistic prediction systems often aggregate probability estimates from multiple models into a single decision. A common assumption is that if each model is individually calibrated, the aggregate prediction will also be well calibrated. We show that this assumption fails in multi-agent settings: individually calibrated predictors can become collectively miscalibrated when their predictions interact strategically, in the game-theoretic sense of Brier-optimal local response, even without deliberate coordination.
  This phenomenon arises naturally when agents are independently trained on overlapping data. We prove that under Brier-score-based aggregation with positively correlated beliefs, each agent's individually optimal report systematically underestimates the positive-class probability, yielding a Price of Anarchy greater than one whenever Cov(b_i, b_j) > 0.
  In a canonical setting (n = 5 agents, pairwise correlation = 0.5, base rate = 0.3), the empirically measured PoA in false-negative rate reaches 7.25x. In contrast, VCG-based aggregation aligns incentives by rewarding marginal contribution, achieving dominant-strategy incentive compatibility and near-optimal performance.
  Experiments on three real-world datasets (NSL-KDD, UNSW-NB15, Credit Card Fraud) show that VCG provides strong robustness while maintaining comparable accuracy. It performs particularly well in data-sparse and adversarial settings, and adaptive weighting further improves performance under distribution shift.

AI.tldr: Individually calibrated predictors can become collectively miscalibrated when aggregated in multi-agent settings due to strategic interactions, with Brier-score aggregation causing systematic underestimation of positive probabilities; VCG-based aggregation solves this incentive misalignment problem.
AI.motivation: The paper challenges the common assumption that aggregating individually calibrated probability estimates from multiple models results in a well-calibrated aggregate prediction. It identifies that in multi-agent settings with strategic interactions, individually calibrated predictors can become collectively miscalibrated even without deliberate coordination, which is a significant problem for probabilistic prediction systems.
AI.method: The paper uses game-theoretic analysis (Brier-optimal local response) to model strategic interactions between predictors. It proves theoretical results about systematic underestimation under Brier-score aggregation with positively correlated beliefs. It compares Brier-score aggregation with VCG-based aggregation, which rewards marginal contributions. Experiments are conducted on synthetic settings (n=5 agents, pairwise correlation=0.5, base rate=0.3) and three real-world datasets (NSL-KDD, UNSW-NB15, Credit Card Fraud).
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows Price of Anarchy > 1 when Cov(b_i, b_j) > 0, with empirical measurements showing PoA in false-negative rate reaching 7.25x in canonical settings. VCG-based aggregation achieves dominant-strategy incentive compatibility and near-optimal performance. Real-world experiments demonstrate VCG's strong robustness, particularly in data-sparse and adversarial settings, while maintaining comparable accuracy. Adaptive weighting further improves performance under distribution shift.
AI.conclusion: The aggregation method matters crucially in multi-agent prediction systems. Brier-score aggregation fails to maintain calibration due to incentive misalignment in strategic settings, while VCG-based aggregation solves this problem by aligning incentives through marginal contribution rewards. VCG provides robust performance especially in challenging scenarios like data scarcity and distribution shift.

title: From SGD to Muon: Adaptive Optimization via Schatten-p Norms
summary: Modern optimizers, like Muon, impose matrix-wise geometry constraints on their updates. These matrix-wise constraints can be unified under Linear Minimization Oracle (LMO) theory. However, all current methods impose fixed LMO geometries for the update rules, chosen by-design or empirically, which are not necessarily optimal according to the problem's geometry. We introduce a novel efficient datadriven criterion for dynamically choosing proxy-optimal update LMO geometries on individual Deep Neural Network layers. Derived in closed form from gradient and activation statistics using a single-step random feature regression surrogate model, our criterion navigates a design space interpolating from SGD to Muon updates. Moreover, integrating parameter-wise preconditioning allows our framework to recover SGD, Muon, Adam, and MuAdam as specific extrema. To make this adaptive approach scalable, we pair it with efficient computational strategies, achieving only a $\sim$ 3% runtime overhead on highly optimized baselines. As a proof of concept, we show that this data-driven optimizer beats or remains competitive with the performance of the best performing optimizer between Muon and AdamW across three different training scenarios. Ultimately, this work provides evidence that LMO geometry can be successfully and efficiently adapted from runtime data, opening a new pathway for optimizer design beyond static geometries.

AI.tldr: A data-driven adaptive optimizer that dynamically chooses optimal update geometries for deep neural networks, interpolating between SGD, Muon, Adam, and MuAdam with minimal runtime overhead.
AI.motivation: Current optimizers like Muon use fixed matrix-wise geometry constraints (Linear Minimization Oracles) that are chosen by design or empirically, which may not be optimal for the actual problem geometry. There's a need for adaptive geometry selection based on runtime data.
AI.method: Developed a novel data-driven criterion using single-step random feature regression to dynamically select proxy-optimal LMO geometries per layer. Uses gradient and activation statistics to derive closed-form solutions that navigate design space from SGD to Muon updates, with parameter-wise preconditioning to recover various optimizer types as special cases.
AI.result: Achieves only ~3% runtime overhead on optimized baselines while beating or remaining competitive with best performing optimizer (Muon vs AdamW) across three different training scenarios. Demonstrates successful adaptation of LMO geometry from runtime data.
AI.conclusion: This work provides evidence that LMO geometry can be successfully and efficiently adapted from runtime data, opening new pathways for optimizer design beyond static geometries, moving toward more adaptive, data-driven optimization approaches.

title: TwinRouterBench: Fast Static and Live Dynamic Evaluation for Realistic Agentic LLM Routing
summary: LLM routing matters most in long-horizon applications such as coding agents, deep research systems, and computer-use agents, where a single user request triggers many model calls. Routing each call to the cheapest sufficient model can cut costs without sacrificing quality, yet existing router benchmarks evaluate routers only on one-shot prompts. They never expose the router-visible prefix at an intermediate agent step, never test whether a cheaper replacement preserves downstream task success, and often rely on online LLM judges at evaluation time. We introduce TwinRouterBench, a step-level routing benchmark with two tracks. The static track provides 970 router-visible prefixes from 520 instances across SWE-bench, BFCL, mtRAG, QMSum, and PinchBench, each paired with an execution-verified target tier estimated under a released downgrade-and-cascade protocol; scoring is deterministic arithmetic over tier labels, trajectory membership, and token costs, with no online evaluator-side LLM judge. The dynamic track supplies a harness that runs routers on the full 500-case SWE-bench Verified suite; in this paper we report a 100-case held-out evaluation disjoint from the static SWE supervision split. At each LLM call the router selects a concrete model from a locked pool, and success is measured by official task resolution and realized API spend. The two tracks support fast offline iteration followed by end-to-end validation under live agent execution. Code and data are available at https://github.com/CommonstackAI/TwinRouterBench.

AI.tldr: TwinRouterBench is a new benchmark for evaluating LLM routers in multi-step agent applications, featuring static and dynamic tracks for comprehensive step-level routing evaluation without relying on online LLM judges.
AI.motivation: Current LLM router benchmarks only evaluate one-shot prompts, failing to assess step-level routing decisions in long-horizon applications where routing each call to the cheapest sufficient model can significantly reduce costs without quality loss.
AI.method: The benchmark has two tracks: 1) Static track with 970 router-visible prefixes from 520 instances across 5 datasets, using execution-verified target tiers and deterministic scoring; 2) Dynamic track with a harness running routers on full SWE-bench Verified suite for end-to-end validation under live agent execution.
AI.result: Provides comprehensive evaluation framework that avoids online LLM judges, enables deterministic scoring based on tier labels and token costs, and supports both offline iteration and live validation with real API spend measurement.
AI.conclusion: TwinRouterBench addresses critical gaps in LLM routing evaluation for agent applications, offering reproducible, execution-based assessment that can guide development of cost-effective routing strategies without sacrificing task success.

title: Prior Knowledge or Search? A Study of LLM Agents in Hardware-Aware Code Optimization
summary: LLM discovery and optimization systems are increasingly applied across domains, implementing a common propose-evaluate-revise loop. Such optimization or discovery progresses via context conditioning on received feedback from an environment. However, as modern LLM agents are increasingly complex in their structure, it is difficult to evaluate which components contribute the most, and when and how this exploration may fail. We answer these questions through three controlled experiments. Our findings: (1) In pure black-box optimization, LLMs act as greedy optimizers. (2) In zero-shot kernel generation, providing explicit input-size information has no measurable effect, models converge to the same kernel parameters regardless of size or temperature, as though the size instruction were invisible. Moreover, when tasked to perform kernel optimization for uncommon kernel sizes, performance sharply degrades regardless of the language used. (3) In feedback-loop kernel optimization, CUDA improves monotonically under iterative feedback, while TVM IR actively degrades, which demonstrates that kernel optimization degrades when models operate with low-density language. Our results conclude that LLMs in code optimization tasks highly depend on pretrained priors rather than provided feedback or agentic structure.

AI.tldr: LLMs act as greedy optimizers in black-box scenarios and heavily rely on pretrained priors rather than feedback or agent structure in code optimization tasks.
AI.motivation: As LLM-based optimization systems become more complex, it's difficult to determine which components contribute most and when exploration fails. The paper aims to understand LLM behavior in optimization tasks through controlled experiments.
AI.method: Three controlled experiments: 1) pure black-box optimization, 2) zero-shot kernel generation with varying parameters, 3) feedback-loop kernel optimization comparing different languages/representations (CUDA vs TVM IR).
AI.result: 1) LLMs act as greedy optimizers. 2) Input-size information has no effect; models converge to same kernel parameters regardless of size/temperature. Performance degrades sharply for uncommon kernel sizes. 3) CUDA improves monotonically with feedback while TVM IR degrades, showing optimization fails with low-density languages.
AI.conclusion: LLMs in code optimization tasks depend heavily on pretrained priors rather than provided feedback or agentic structure. Their optimization capabilities are constrained by their training data patterns.

title: From Llama to Cria: Scaling Down Neural Networks via Neuron-Level Spectral Structural Importance Evaluation
summary: This paper proposes a neuron pruning framework based on neuron-level spectral structural importance evaluation. Given a trained neural network, we record the hidden states of each hidden layer during inference and model neurons as graph nodes, with hidden states treated as graph signals. Using ideas from graph signal processing, we infer layer-wise input and output graphs that characterize the structural relationships among neurons before and after each layer transformation. We then evaluate the spectral structural importance of neurons by analyzing the transformation between these graphs based on spectral graph theory. Neurons with high spectral structural importance are regarded as strongly involved in the internal representation transformation and are therefore preserved, while neurons with low importance scores are selected as pruning candidates. The pruning process is conducted iteratively until a predefined effective parameter reduction target is reached. Instead of fine-tuning after every pruning step, the proposed strategy first removes low-importance neurons to obtain a compact architecture and then applies a final recovery fine-tuning stage to restore task performance. By connecting neuron pruning with graph signal processing and spectral structural analysis, the proposed framework offers a principled way to reduce neural network size while maintaining solution quality. Experimental results on CIFAR-10 image classification and SST-2 sentiment classification show that our method can effectively remove low-importance neurons and achieve compact networks with competitive performance after recovery fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: A neuron pruning framework using spectral graph theory to identify structurally important neurons for removal, achieving compact networks with minimal performance loss after recovery fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Need for principled neuron pruning methods that go beyond simple weight magnitude-based approaches to better understand and preserve the structural importance of neurons in network transformations.
AI.method: Treats neurons as graph nodes and hidden states as graph signals, infers layer-wise input/output graphs using graph signal processing, evaluates neuron importance via spectral graph theory analysis of transformation between graphs, iteratively prunes low-importance neurons, and applies final recovery fine-tuning.
AI.result: The method effectively removes low-importance neurons and achieves compact networks with competitive performance on CIFAR-10 image classification and SST-2 sentiment classification tasks after recovery fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a principled approach to neuron pruning by connecting it with graph signal processing and spectral structural analysis, enabling effective neural network compression while maintaining solution quality.

title: From Prompts to Pavement Through Time: Temporal Grounding in Agentic Scene-to-Plan Reasoning
summary: Recent attempts to support high-level scene interpretation and planning in Autonomous Vehicles (AVs) using ensembles of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) continue to treat time as a secondary property. This lack of temporal grounding leads to inconsistencies in reasoning about continuous actions, undermining both safety and interpretability. This work explores whether temporal conditioning within inter-agent communication can preserve or enhance coherence without introducing degradation in semantic or logical consistency. To investigate this, we introduce three planner architectures with progressively increasing temporal integration and evaluate them on curated subsets of the BDD-X dataset using semantic, syntactic, and logical metrics. Results show that while temporal conditioning reshapes reasoning style, it yields no statistically significant improvements in standard NLP-based correctness metrics. However, qualitative analysis reveals predictive hazard reasoning, stable corrective behavior, and strategic divergence in the Sentinel. These findings clarify the limits of prompt-based temporal grounding and establish the first empirical benchmark for temporal scene-to-plan reasoning.

AI.tldr: Adding time-based conditioning to LLM ensemble planners for autonomous vehicles doesn't significantly improve standard NLP metrics but enables better hazard prediction and stable corrective behavior.
AI.motivation: Current LLM/LMM ensembles for AV scene interpretation treat time as secondary, causing inconsistencies in reasoning about continuous actions and undermining safety/interpretability. The paper explores whether temporal conditioning in inter-agent communication can improve coherence without degrading semantic/logical consistency.
AI.method: Introduced three planner architectures with progressively increasing temporal integration. Evaluated them on curated subsets of the BDD-X dataset using semantic, syntactic, and logical metrics.
AI.result: Temporal conditioning reshapes reasoning style but yields no statistically significant improvements in standard NLP-based correctness metrics. However, qualitative analysis reveals predictive hazard reasoning, stable corrective behavior, and strategic divergence in the Sentinel.
AI.conclusion: The findings clarify the limits of prompt-based temporal grounding and establish the first empirical benchmark for temporal scene-to-plan reasoning in AV systems.

title: Towards Family-Grouped Hierarchical Federated Learning on Sub-5KB Models: A Feasibility Study of Privacy-Preserving ECG Monitoring for Ultra-Resource-Constrained Wearables
comment: Supported by Shenzhen Coddie Technology Co., Ltd. This is a preprint and has not been peer-reviewed
summary: Cardiovascular disease remains the leading cause of death worldwide, and early detection of arrhythmias through continuous ECG monitoring on wearable devices can prevent life-threatening events. Federated Learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training by keeping raw ECG data on device, yet standard FL incurs prohibitive communication overhead and standard deep learning models cannot fit on ultra-low-power microcontrollers. We propose Family-Grouped Hierarchical Federated Learning (Family-FL), a three-tier architecture that uses the family as a natural privacy boundary for intra-family aggregation before global synchronization. We further design a hardware-constrained Tiny CNN-LSTM architecture with only 669 parameters, INT8-quantized to occupy merely 4.65KB Flash and 2.95KB RAM, meeting the constraints of STC32G12K128-class microcontrollers. Experiments on the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database (mean of 5 independent runs with different seeds) demonstrate that Family-FL reduces communication volume by 76.7% compared to FedAvg while maintaining comparable accuracy. Family-FL-Tiny achieves 91.9 +/- 1.2% accuracy with macro-F1 of 0.483 +/- 0.031, reducing total communication to 0.31% of FedAvg. The model achieves reliable ventricular arrhythmia detection (per-class F1 = 0.80), the most clinically critical abnormality for home-based preliminary screening. These results demonstrate the technical feasibility of privacy-preserving federated learning on ultra-resource-constrained microcontrollers through simulation-based evaluation. We honestly discuss limitations: no hardware deployment, single-dataset validation (MIT-BIH, 47 subjects), reduced rare-class sensitivity, and absence of formal differential privacy guarantees.

AI.tldr: A privacy-preserving federated learning system called Family-FL enables arrhythmia detection on ultra-low-power microcontrollers with minimal communication overhead while maintaining patient privacy.
AI.motivation: Cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of death worldwide, and early detection of arrhythmias through continuous ECG monitoring on wearable devices can prevent life-threatening events. However, standard federated learning incurs prohibitive communication overhead and cannot fit on ultra-low-power microcontrollers while preserving privacy.
AI.method: The paper proposes Family-Grouped Hierarchical Federated Learning (Family-FL), a three-tier architecture that uses the family as a natural privacy boundary for intra-family aggregation before global synchronization. Additionally, they design a hardware-constrained Tiny CNN-LSTM architecture with only 669 parameters, INT8-quantized to occupy merely 4.65KB Flash and 2.95KB RAM for STC32G12K128-class microcontrollers.
AI.result: Family-FL reduces communication volume by 76.7% compared to FedAvg while maintaining comparable accuracy. Family-FL-Tiny achieves 91.9 +/- 1.2% accuracy with macro-F1 of 0.483 +/- 0.031, reducing total communication to 0.31% of FedAvg. The model achieves reliable ventricular arrhythmia detection (per-class F1 = 0.80), which is the most clinically critical abnormality for home-based screening.
AI.conclusion: The results demonstrate the technical feasibility of privacy-preserving federated learning on ultra-resource-constrained microcontrollers through simulation-based evaluation. The authors acknowledge limitations including no hardware deployment, single-dataset validation (MIT-BIH, 47 subjects), reduced rare-class sensitivity, and absence of formal differential privacy guarantees.

title: Explainable Wastewater Digital Twins: Adaptive Context-Conditioned Structured Simulators with Self-Falsifying Decision Support
comment: 17 pages, 7 figures, 6 tables, 2 algorithms. Supplementary material (7 pages) included as ancillary file
summary: Operators of safety-critical industrial processes increasingly rely on digital twins to screen control interventions, but such simulators rarely carry certified safety guarantees. Wastewater treatment plants exemplify the gap: operators face a daily safety-efficiency trade-off where aerating too little risks effluent violations and nitrous-oxide (N2O) spikes, and aerating too much wastes energy. We develop an explainable digital twin for aeration and dosing setpoints. CCSS-IX, the simulator, is a bank of interpretable locally linear state-space "experts" adaptively mixed by a context-aware gating network, building on a continuous-time regime-switching scaffold. A runtime decision layer applies conformal risk control to abstain, reopen, or return a falsifying temporal witness for any operator-proposed action that cannot be statistically certified. The artificial-intelligence contribution is twofold: an identifiable, context-conditioned structured surrogate that retains operator-readable dynamics, and a self-falsifying decision rule with finite-sample coverage guarantees. The engineering contribution is a validated, end-to-end decision-support pipeline, tested on a 1000-step slice of the Avedøre full-scale plant (42.6% sensor missingness, 2-minute sampling), the Agtrup/BlueKolding full-scale plant in Denmark, and the Benchmark Simulation Model No. 2 (BSM2) international benchmark, under a matched ten-seed protocol. The static structured ensemble lies within 0.78% root-mean-square error of an unconstrained black-box reference, and the adaptive variant within 1.08%. The calibrated reopen rule cuts aggregate two-plant regret by 43.6% at an unsafe-action cost weight of 4 and eliminates unsafe chosen actions on the BSM2 main slice. Event-aligned temporal witnesses prevent 93 of 187 false-safe N2O approvals, about 4.65x the dyadic baseline (paired McNemar p < 1e-21).

AI.tldr: Researchers developed an explainable digital twin for wastewater treatment plants that uses interpretable linear models with conformal risk control to certify safety of control actions, reducing unsafe decisions by 43.6%.
AI.motivation: Current digital twins for safety-critical industrial processes like wastewater treatment lack certified safety guarantees. Operators face a daily trade-off between safety (preventing effluent violations and N2O spikes) and efficiency (avoiding excessive aeration energy waste), needing better decision support with statistical safety assurances.
AI.method: CCSS-IX: an explainable digital twin using a bank of interpretable locally linear state-space "experts" adaptively mixed by context-aware gating network, built on continuous-time regime-switching framework. Runtime decision layer applies conformal risk control to abstain, reopen, or return temporal witnesses for operator-proposed actions that can't be statistically certified.
AI.result: Achieved 0.78% RMSE for static ensemble and 1.08% for adaptive variant compared to black-box reference. Reopen rule cut aggregate two-plant regret by 43.6% at unsafe-action weight of 4, eliminated unsafe actions on BSM2. Temporal witnesses prevented 93 of 187 false-safe N2O approvals (4.65x baseline, p < 1e-21).
AI.conclusion: The approach provides an explainable, certified-safe digital twin for industrial process control with finite-sample coverage guarantees, effectively balancing safety and efficiency while maintaining interpretability for operators.

title: SAGE: Shaping Anchors for Guided Exploration in RLVR of LLMs
summary: Recent studies observe that reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) reliably improves pass@1 on reasoning tasks, yet often fails to yield comparable gains in pass@k, raising the question of whether RLVR genuinely enables large language models to acquire novel reasoning abilities or merely enhances the efficiency of sampling reasoning modes already present in the base model. Prior analyses largely support the latter view, attributing this limitation to structural properties of standard RLVR objectives that result in insufficient exploration pressure. In this work, we argue that a central structural constraint arises from reverse-KL regularization, which stabilizes training but inherently anchors the policy to the reference distribution, thereby suppressing the emergence of alternative reasoning modes. However, we show that neither removing the KL term nor replacing it with forward-KL provides a satisfactory solution, as both disrupt the efficiency-coverage trade-off by either inducing reward hacking or allocating probability mass to off-target regions. To resolve this tension, we propose SAGE, a principled framework that enables controllable empirical support expansion by reshaping the reverse-KL anchor distribution itself through a guide function q(x,y), achieving consistent improvements in both pass@1 and pass@k across challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks. Our code is available at https://github.com/tally0818/SAGE.

AI.tldr: RLVR (reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards) improves pass@1 but not pass@k on reasoning tasks. The paper identifies reverse-KL regularization as the structural constraint limiting exploration and proposes SAGE, a framework that reshapes the anchor distribution to enable support expansion, achieving gains in both pass@1 and pass@k.
AI.motivation: RLVR reliably improves pass@1 scores on reasoning tasks but often fails to yield comparable gains in pass@k. This raises a fundamental question: does RLVR actually teach LLMs new reasoning abilities, or does it merely make them more efficient at sampling reasoning modes already present in the base model? Prior research suggests it's the latter, attributed to structural limitations in standard RLVR objectives that provide insufficient exploration pressure.
AI.method: The paper identifies reverse-KL regularization as the key structural constraint that stabilizes training but anchors the policy to the reference distribution, suppressing alternative reasoning modes. They show that simply removing the KL term or replacing it with forward-KL doesn't work well - the former causes reward hacking, while the latter allocates probability mass to off-target regions. To address this, they propose SAGE (Support-Aware Gradient Expansion), a principled framework that enables controllable empirical support expansion by reshaping the reverse-KL anchor distribution through a guide function q(x,y).
AI.result: SAGE achieves consistent improvements in both pass@1 and pass@k across challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks, resolving the efficiency-coverage trade-off that plagued previous RLVR approaches.
AI.conclusion: The reverse-KL regularization in standard RLVR objectives limits exploration and prevents the emergence of diverse reasoning modes. SAGE provides a principled solution by reshaping the anchor distribution itself, enabling both improved efficiency (pass@1) and coverage (pass@k), demonstrating that with proper structural adjustments, RLVR can indeed help LLMs acquire novel reasoning abilities rather than just optimizing existing ones.

title: Streamlined Constraint Reasoning via CNN Pattern Recognition on Enumerated Solutions
summary: Constraint programming practitioners accelerate hard problems through a layered set of techniques applied in order of risk. Standard hardening (symmetry-breaking and implied constraints) is applied first and preserves satisfiability. Streamliner constraints, which restrict search to a structural sub-family of solutions, do not preserve satisfiability and are reserved as a final lever. Existing automated streamliner-synthesis approaches either search a constraint grammar or prompt a Large Language Model directly on the problem model. We propose a different approach: enumerate feasible solutions, train a Convolutional Neural Network contrastively against perturbed non-solutions to detect structural patterns, and translate the CNN's discriminative signal into candidate MiniZinc streamliners through LLM-driven synthesis. The CNN grounds the LLM's constraint generation in observed solution structure rather than model text alone. We evaluate on hardened benchmark models where streamliner discovery is the residual performance lever. Our pipeline achieves 98.8% portfolio time reduction on hardened Vessel Loading, 98.6% on hardened Social Golfers, and 89.4% on Black Hole, with best-single streamliners reaching geometric-mean speedups of 932x, 356x, and 1103x respectively. Discovered streamliners include class-based packing constraints on Vessel Loading, beyond-hardening canonicalisations on Social Golfers, and layout-coordinate bounds on Black Hole.

AI.tldr: A novel pipeline for automated streamliner synthesis in constraint programming that uses CNN-based solution structure analysis to ground LLM-driven constraint generation, achieving significant speedups on hardened benchmark problems.
AI.motivation: Existing automated streamliner-synthesis approaches either search constraint grammars or prompt LLMs directly on problem models, which may not effectively capture structural patterns in feasible solutions. There's a need for methods that can detect and leverage solution structure to generate effective streamliner constraints.
AI.method: Proposed approach: 1) enumerate feasible solutions, 2) train a Convolutional Neural Network contrastively against perturbed non-solutions to detect structural patterns, 3) translate CNN's discriminative signal into candidate MiniZinc streamliners through LLM-driven synthesis. The CNN grounds LLM's constraint generation in observed solution structure rather than model text alone.
AI.result: Achieved significant performance improvements: 98.8% portfolio time reduction on hardened Vessel Loading, 98.6% on hardened Social Golfers, and 89.4% on Black Hole. Best single streamliners achieved geometric-mean speedups of 932x, 356x, and 1103x respectively. Discovered novel streamliner types including class-based packing constraints, beyond-hardening canonicalisations, and layout-coordinate bounds.
AI.conclusion: The CNN-based approach successfully grounds LLM streamliner synthesis in observed solution structure, enabling effective discovery of streamliner constraints that provide substantial performance improvements on hardened constraint programming problems where streamliner discovery is the residual performance lever.

title: From Sparsity to Simplicity: Enabling Simpler Sequential Replacements via Sparse Attention Distillation
summary: Self-attention serves as the core foundation of large-scale transformer pretraining, but its quadratic token interaction cost makes inference expensive. Replacing attention with simpler sequential modules is appealing, yet naive substitution is often lossy, especially at larger scales. This paper revisits attention replacement through the lens of sparsity. Based on the observation of diverse sparsity patterns across transformer layers, we posit that pretrained transformers decompose the complex token dependency across tokens into various sequence-to-sequence mappings of diverse complexities, where some layer functionalities can be approximated and replaced with much simpler sequential modules without loss. We evaluate this premise using a plug-and-play layer-wise distillation framework to approximate and replace attention functionalities in pretrained vision transformer models. Controlled group-wise replacements under a fixed training budget reveal a clear pattern: substituting layers with sparser attention incurs substantially smaller accuracy drops than replacing denser ones. We further impose explicit attention sparsity on the pretrained ViT via AViT-style token retention and perform sparsity-guided distillation for sequential replacing models, where we see increasing teacher sparsity consistently reduces the student-teacher gap. The proposed method achieves efficient attention replacement for reduced parameter size and latency through the guidance of attention sparsity.

AI.tldr: TL;DR: Attention sparsity patterns guide efficient replacement of transformer attention layers with simpler sequential modules, reducing parameters and latency while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Self-attention in transformers has quadratic computational cost making inference expensive. Simple sequential modules could replace attention but naive substitution causes accuracy loss, especially at larger scales.
AI.method: 1. Observes diverse attention sparsity patterns across transformer layers. 2. Uses layer-wise distillation framework to approximate and replace attention in vision transformers. 3. Controlled group-wise replacements reveal sparser attention layers are easier to replace. 4. Applies AViT-style token retention to impose explicit sparsity and performs sparsity-guided distillation for sequential replacement models.
AI.result: Substituting sparser attention layers incurs substantially smaller accuracy drops than replacing denser ones. Increasing teacher sparsity consistently reduces student-teacher gap. The method achieves efficient attention replacement with reduced parameter size and latency.
AI.conclusion: Attention sparsity serves as effective guidance for replacing attention with simpler sequential modules, enabling efficient transformer inference without significant accuracy loss. Different layers have varying complexity needs, with sparser attention patterns being more replaceable.

title: PEEK: Context Map as an Orientation Cache for Long-Context LLM Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly operate over long and recurring external contexts, like document corpora and code repositories. Across invocations, existing approaches preserve either the agent's trajectory, passive access to raw material, or task-level strategies. None of them preserves what we argue is most needed for repeated same-context workloads: reusable orientation knowledge (e.g., what the context contains, how it is organized, and which entities, constants, and schemas have historically been useful) about the recurring context itself. We introduce PEEK, a system that caches and maintains this orientation knowledge as a context map: a small, constant-sized artifact in the agent's prompt that gives it a persistent peek into the external context. The map is maintained by a programmable cache policy with three modules: a Distiller that extracts transferable knowledge from inference-time signals, a Cartographer that translates it into structured edits, and a priority-based Evictor that enforces a fixed token budget. On long-context reasoning and information aggregation, PEEK improves over strong baselines by 6.3-34.0% while using 93-145 fewer iterations and incurring 1.7-5.8x lower cost than the state-of-the-art prompt-learning framework, ACE. On context learning, PEEK improves solving rate and rubric accuracy by 6.0-14.0% and 7.8-12.1%, respectively, at 1.4x lower cost than ACE. These gains generalize across LMs and agent architectures, including OpenAI Codex, a production-grade coding agent. Together, these results show that a context map helps long-context LLM agents interact with recurring external contexts more accurately and efficiently.

AI.tldr: PEEK is a system that caches reusable orientation knowledge about recurring external contexts in a constant-sized context map, enabling LLM agents to work with long contexts more efficiently and accurately.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches for LLM agents operating over long and recurring external contexts preserve either agent trajectory, passive access to raw material, or task-level strategies, but fail to preserve reusable orientation knowledge about the context itself - what it contains, how it's organized, and which elements have been historically useful.
AI.method: PEEK introduces a context map system with three programmable cache policy modules: (1) Distiller extracts transferable knowledge from inference-time signals, (2) Cartographer translates this knowledge into structured edits, and (3) priority-based Evictor enforces a fixed token budget to maintain a small, constant-sized artifact in the agent's prompt.
AI.result: On long-context reasoning and information aggregation, PEEK improves over strong baselines by 6.3-34.0% while using 93-145 fewer iterations and incurring 1.7-5.8x lower cost than ACE. On context learning, it improves solving rate and rubric accuracy by 6.0-14.0% and 7.8-12.1% respectively at 1.4x lower cost.
AI.conclusion: A context map helps long-context LLM agents interact with recurring external contexts more accurately and efficiently, with gains generalizing across different language models and agent architectures including production-grade coding systems like OpenAI Codex.

title: FLUIDSPLAT: Reconstructing Physical Fields from Sparse Sensors via Gaussian Primitives
summary: Reconstructing continuous flow fields from sparse surface-mounted sensors is central to aerodynamic design, flow control, and digital-twin instrumentation. Existing neural methods for this task typically encode sensor readings into implicit latent codes with little spatial interpretability and limited formal guidance on how representational capacity should scale with observation count. Inspired by 3D Gaussian Splatting, we introduce FLUIDSPLAT, a sensor-conditioned model that predicts K anisotropic Gaussian primitives forming a partition-of-unity scaffold, a spatially explicit and interpretable intermediate representation of the flow. For an idealized Gaussian primitive estimator, we prove an $O(K^{-s/d})$ approximation rate for fields with Sobolev smoothness $s$; incorporating $N$ noisy observations yields a squared-risk decomposition with bias $O(K^{-2s/d})$ and variance $O(σ^{2}K/N)$.Balancing the two yields $K^{*}\!\sim\!(N/σ^{2})^{d/(2s+d)}$: primitive count cannot grow freely under sparse sensing, revealing a variance bottleneck that motivates complementing the scaffold with a state-conditioned residual decoder. On a standard cylinder-flow benchmark, FLUIDSPLAT achieves the best mean error across all surface-sensor layouts; on AirfRANS with 8 surface-pressure sensors, it reduces error by 11-23% over the strongest baseline across three standard splits.

AI.tldr: FLUIDSPLAT is a sensor-conditioned model using anisotropic Gaussian primitives for reconstructing flow fields from sparse surface sensors, achieving state-of-the-art performance with interpretable spatial representations.
AI.motivation: Existing neural methods for reconstructing flow fields from sparse sensors use implicit latent codes with poor spatial interpretability and lack formal guidance on how representational capacity should scale with observation count.
AI.method: Inspired by 3D Gaussian Splatting, the method predicts K anisotropic Gaussian primitives forming a partition-of-unity scaffold as an interpretable intermediate representation, complemented with a state-conditioned residual decoder.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows O(K^{-s/d}) approximation rate for Sobolev-smooth fields, and variance bottleneck analysis leads to optimal scaling K*∼(N/σ²)^{d/(2s+d)}. On cylinder-flow benchmark, achieves best mean error across all sensor layouts; on AirfRANS with 8 sensors, reduces error by 11-23% over strongest baselines.
AI.conclusion: FLUIDSPLAT provides a spatially interpretable, theoretically grounded approach to flow field reconstruction from sparse sensors, outperforming existing methods while revealing important scaling relationships between primitive count and observation quality.

title: Robotics-Inspired Guardrails for Foundation Models in Socially Sensitive Domains
comment: Under review at Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research (JAIR)
summary: Foundation models are increasingly deployed in socially sensitive domains such as education, mental health, and caregiving, where failures are often cumulative and context-dependent. Existing guardrail approaches -- ranging from training-time alignment to prompting, decoding constraints, and post-hoc moderation -- primarily provide empirical risk reduction rather than enforceable behavioral guarantees, and largely treat safety as a property of individual outputs rather than interaction trajectories. We reframe guardrails as a problem of runtime behavioral control over interaction trajectories, drawing on robotics to introduce formal constructs for constraint enforcement in uncertain, closed-loop systems. We instantiate these ideas in the Grounded Observer framework and apply it across three real-world deployments: small talk, in-home autism therapy, and behavioral de-escalation in schools. Across settings, the framework enables runtime interventions that mitigate drift into undesirable interaction regimes while adapting to diverse social contexts. We discuss extensions to the framework and propose research directions toward stronger guarantees.

AI.tldr: Foundation model guardrails should provide formal behavioral guarantees for interaction trajectories, not just empirical risk reduction for individual outputs, using runtime control inspired by robotics.
AI.motivation: Foundation models are deployed in socially sensitive domains (education, mental health, caregiving) where failures are cumulative and context-dependent. Current guardrail approaches only provide empirical risk reduction rather than enforceable behavioral guarantees and treat safety as a property of individual outputs rather than interaction trajectories.
AI.method: Reframe guardrails as runtime behavioral control over interaction trajectories, drawing on robotics to introduce formal constructs for constraint enforcement in uncertain, closed-loop systems. Instantiate these ideas in the Grounded Observer framework and apply it across three real-world deployments: small talk, in-home autism therapy, and behavioral de-escalation in schools.
AI.result: The framework enables runtime interventions that mitigate drift into undesirable interaction regimes while adapting to diverse social contexts across all deployment settings.
AI.conclusion: Runtime behavioral control frameworks provide stronger guarantees for foundation model safety in socially sensitive domains. Extensions to the framework are discussed with proposed research directions toward even stronger guarantees.

title: EVA-0: Test-Time Model Evolution with Only Two Forward Passes per Sample
summary: Test-time model evolution offers a promising way for deployed models to improve from unlabeled test-time experience, yet most existing methods depend on backpropagation (BP), which incurs substantial memory overhead and makes them difficult to deploy on edge devices, quantized models, specialized accelerators, or black-box models. In this work, we study test-time model evolution under a strict two-forward budget, a setting that pushes adaptation toward highly efficient real-world deployment. We reveal three key obstacles in zeroth-order test-time optimization: susceptibility to shortcut solutions, uncontrolled weight drift, and ineffective update direction estimation. To overcome them, we propose EVA-0, a minimal zeroth-order adaptation framework that: 1) keeps the loss scale-invariant to prevent shortcut solutions; 2) devises an anchor-guided optimization strategy to alleviate weight drift; 3) uses sample-wise symmetric two-sided perturbation for update direction estimation and inference. EVA-0 requires no BP and performs both inference and adaptation within only two forward passes per sample. Results on ImageNet-C & ViT-Base show that EVA-0 outperforms both BP-based DeYO and BP-free FOA, while achieving a 14x speed-up over FOA. Code will be released.

AI.tldr: EVA-0 is a zeroth-order test-time adaptation framework that requires only two forward passes per sample (no backpropagation), achieving efficient deployment on edge devices while outperforming BP-based methods on ImageNet-C.
AI.motivation: Test-time model evolution typically requires backpropagation which has high memory overhead and deployment limitations on edge devices, quantized models, specialized accelerators, or black-box models. There's a need for efficient adaptation methods under strict computational budgets.
AI.method: EVA-0 uses a zeroth-order optimization approach with three key components: 1) loss scale-invariance to prevent shortcut solutions, 2) anchor-guided optimization to control weight drift, 3) sample-wise symmetric two-sided perturbation for update direction estimation. All adaptation and inference done in only two forward passes per sample.
AI.result: EVA-0 outperforms both backpropagation-based DeYO and BP-free FOA on ImageNet-C with ViT-Base, while achieving 14x speed-up over FOA. It demonstrates effective test-time adaptation without backpropagation.
AI.conclusion: EVA-0 provides an efficient zeroth-order adaptation framework that enables test-time model evolution under strict computational budgets, making it suitable for deployment on resource-constrained devices and overcoming key obstacles in zeroth-order optimization.

title: Probabilistic Tiny Recursive Model
summary: Tiny Recursive Models (TRM) solve complex reasoning tasks with a fraction of the parameters of modern large language models (LLMs) by iteratively refining a latent state and final answer. While powerful, their deterministic recursion can lead to convergence at suboptimal solutions, without escape mechanism. A common workaround relies on task-specific input perturbations at test time combined with answer aggregation via voting. We introduce Probabilistic TRM (PTRM), a task-agnostic framework for test-time compute scaling that addresses this limitation through stochastic exploration. PTRM injects Gaussian noise at each deep recursion step, enabling parallel trajectories to explore diverse solution basins, and selects among them using the model's existing Q head (used for early stopping in the original TRM). Without requiring retraining or task-specific augmentations, PTRM enables substantial accuracy gains across benchmarks, including Sudoku-Extreme (87.4% to 98.75%) and on various puzzles from Pencil Puzzle Bench (62.6% to 91.2%). On the latter, PTRM achieves nearly double the accuracy of frontier LLMs (91.2% vs. 55.1%) at less than 0.0001x the cost, using only 7M parameters.

AI.tldr: PTRM introduces stochastic exploration to deterministic recursive models, enabling test-time compute scaling without retraining and achieving dramatic accuracy improvements on complex reasoning tasks at minimal parameter cost.
AI.motivation: Tiny Recursive Models (TRM) are efficient for complex reasoning but suffer from deterministic recursion that can converge to suboptimal solutions without escape mechanisms. Current workarounds require task-specific input perturbations and answer aggregation.
AI.method: Introduces Probabilistic TRM (PTRM) that injects Gaussian noise at each deep recursion step, enabling parallel trajectories to explore diverse solution basins. It uses the model's existing Q head for selection among trajectories (originally used for early stopping in TRM).
AI.result: PTRM achieves substantial accuracy gains without retraining: Sudoku-Extreme (87.4% to 98.75%), various puzzles from Pencil Puzzle Bench (62.6% to 91.2%). On Pencil Puzzle Bench, achieves nearly double accuracy of frontier LLMs (91.2% vs. 55.1%) at <0.0001x cost with only 7M parameters.
AI.conclusion: PTRM provides a task-agnostic framework for test-time compute scaling that addresses limitations of deterministic recursive models through stochastic exploration, enabling dramatic performance improvements on complex reasoning tasks while maintaining extreme parameter efficiency.

title: MO-CAPO: Multi-Objective Cost-Aware Prompt Optimization
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve strong performance across a wide range of tasks but are highly sensitive to prompt design, motivating the need for automatic prompt optimization. Existing methods predominantly focus on performance alone, ignoring competing objectives such as inference cost or latency. At the same time, existing work on multi-objective prompt optimization relies on off-the-shelf NSGA-II, ignoring optimization efficiency. As a remedy, we introduce MO-CAPO, a novel multi-objective prompt optimization algorithm that jointly optimizes performance and inference cost while leveraging budget allocation for cost-efficient optimization. We further propose a deployment-oriented cost objective that captures the full computational profile of LLM inference. We evaluate our approach across four tasks and three LLMs and compare it to an NSGA-II-based multi-objective method and state-of-the-art single-objective prompt optimizers. Results show that MO-CAPO consistently identifies strong, robust, and diverse Pareto front approximations while maintaining cost-efficiency. It outperforms the NSGA-II baseline on 8 out of 12 cases in terms of the noisy R2 metric and achieves competitive performances often already at a considerably lower budget. The discovered solution sets span diverse performance-cost trade-offs that are omitted by single-objective optimizers, yet the top-performance candidates remain competitive with single-objective solutions. Additionally, we conduct the first evaluation of multi-objective machine learning experiments that considers generalization and robustness through noisy R2 and approximation gap, enabling a more realistic assessment of solution quality. MO-CAPO enables practitioners to select from an efficiently discovered set of multiple prompts offering different trade-offs between performance and cost.

AI.tldr: MO-CAPO is a novel multi-objective prompt optimization algorithm that jointly optimizes LLM performance and inference cost, outperforming existing methods while maintaining cost-efficiency and discovering diverse performance-cost trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Existing prompt optimization methods focus only on performance, ignoring important practical objectives like inference cost and latency. Current multi-objective approaches use inefficient off-the-shelf algorithms like NSGA-II without considering optimization efficiency.
AI.method: MO-CAPO jointly optimizes performance and inference cost using budget allocation for cost-efficient optimization. It introduces a deployment-oriented cost objective that captures the full computational profile of LLM inference, including factors beyond just token counts.
AI.result: MO-CAPO consistently identifies strong, robust, and diverse Pareto front approximations across four tasks and three LLMs. It outperforms NSGA-II baseline on 8 out of 12 cases using noisy R2 metric, achieves competitive performance at lower budgets, and discovers solution sets with diverse trade-offs omitted by single-objective optimizers.
AI.conclusion: MO-CAPO enables practitioners to efficiently discover multiple prompts offering different performance-cost trade-offs, with top-performance candidates remaining competitive with single-objective solutions while providing broader optimization options.

title: GeoX: Mastering Geospatial Reasoning Through Self-Play and Verifiable Rewards
summary: Geospatial reasoning requires solving image-grounded problems over the complex spatial structure of a scene. However, developing this capability is hindered by the cost of annotating a vast and combinatorial question space. We propose GeoX, a self-play framework that acquires spatial logic through executable programs that yield verifiable rewards, without relying on large-scale human-curated data Given a satellite or aerial image, our framework employs a single multimodal policy that proposes spatial problems as executable programs and solves them under three reasoning modes-abduction, deduction, and induction-over spatial primitives and an image understanding tool. A verifier executes each program to covert a reward signal that jointly optimizes the two roles via reinforcement learning. GeoX consistently improves its base VLMs by up to 5.5 points on average, matching or exceeding conventional baselines trained on millions of curated data. Along-side the proposed method, we release a benchmark for geospatial understanding accumulated through self-play.

AI.tldr: GeoX is a self-play framework that uses executable programs to learn geospatial reasoning without human annotations, improving vision-language models by up to 5.5 points on average.
AI.motivation: Geospatial reasoning requires solving complex spatial problems, but development is hindered by the high cost of annotating vast combinatorial question spaces with human-curated data.
AI.method: A self-play framework using a single multimodal policy that proposes spatial problems as executable programs and solves them through three reasoning modes (abduction, deduction, induction) over spatial primitives and an image understanding tool, with verifier-executed programs providing reward signals for reinforcement learning.
AI.result: GeoX consistently improves base vision-language models by up to 5.5 points on average, matching or exceeding conventional baselines trained on millions of curated data points.
AI.conclusion: The self-play framework effectively learns spatial logic without large-scale human annotations and is accompanied by a released benchmark for geospatial understanding accumulated through the self-play process.

title: Multi-Headed Transformer Architectures as Time-dependent Wasserstein Gradient Flows
summary: In recent years, transformer architectures have revolutionized the field of language processing, opening the door to previously unforeseen possibilities. However, from a theoretical point of view, the mathematical models proposed in the literature often lack direct contact with the actual architectures and depend on strong simplifying assumptions. In this paper, we reduce this gap by modelling the data flow in multi-headed transformer architectures as time-dependent gradient flows for a suitable interaction energy capturing the design of the attention mechanism. The explicit dependence on time allows us to consider different weights for each head and for each layer, without imposing constraints on the initialization method. Moreover, we prove that, under a suitable integrability assumption on the evolution of the weights, each element of the $ω$-limit set of the gradient flows is a stationary point of the interaction energy at a limiting weight distribution. Finally, we analyse the stability of the gradient flows considering perturbations of both the initial data and the weights. Specifically, on the one hand, we study the robustness of the proposed models with respect to noisy inputs, establishing a continuous dependence of the gradient flows on the initial data and uniqueness of the flows. On the other hand, we prove the $Γ$-convergence of the perturbed interaction energy to the unperturbed one, leading to the convergence of the corresponding gradient flows. We complement these theoretical results with numerical experiments that confirm the predicted energy-dissipation identity and clarify the asymptotic behavior of the dynamics in both the autonomous-like (Ornstein--Uhlenbeck) and the genuinely non-autonomous (oscillating-weights) regimes.

AI.tldr: This paper models multi-headed transformer architectures as time-dependent gradient flows for an interaction energy that captures attention mechanisms, analyzing their mathematical properties including stability, convergence, and asymptotic behavior.
AI.motivation: Existing theoretical models for transformers lack direct contact with actual architectures and depend on strong simplifying assumptions. The authors aim to bridge this gap by providing a more realistic mathematical framework that captures the attention mechanism's design.
AI.method: Model data flow in multi-headed transformer architectures as time-dependent gradient flows for a suitable interaction energy. Allow different weights for each head and layer without initialization constraints. Use mathematical analysis including ω-limit sets, Γ-convergence, and stability analysis with perturbations of initial data and weights. Complement theory with numerical experiments.
AI.result: Proved that under integrability assumptions on weight evolution, elements of ω-limit sets are stationary points of interaction energy at limiting weight distributions. Established robustness to noisy inputs via continuous dependence on initial data and uniqueness. Proved Γ-convergence of perturbed interaction energy to unperturbed one, leading to gradient flow convergence. Numerical experiments confirmed energy-dissipation identity and clarified asymptotic behavior in autonomous (Ornstein-Uhlenbeck) and non-autonomous (oscillating-weights) regimes.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a mathematically rigorous framework connecting transformer architectures to gradient flow theory, addressing the theoretical-practical gap while establishing important stability and convergence properties that enhance understanding of transformer dynamics.

title: Distributional Energy-Based Models for Uncertainty-Aware Structured LLM Reasoning
summary: When Large Language Models produce structured outputs such as travel plans, code solutions, or multi-step proofs, individual reasoning steps may appear correct while the output as a whole violates budgets, fails test cases, or contradicts earlier deductions. We propose a decomposed energy function that combines a learned quality scorer with deterministic analytical constraint penalties for verifying structured LLM outputs. The quality scorer is a heterogeneous ensemble of low-rank adapters on a single frozen encoder (3% trainable parameters); the ensemble mean ranks candidates while the standard deviation quantifies epistemic uncertainty, driving a two-pass inference loop that triggers targeted regeneration or abstention. Across five benchmarks (GSM8K, MuSR, TravelPlanner, TACO, Knights & Knaves), our 149M-parameter verifier orchestrating a pool of 7-26B open generators outperforms single-shot Qwen-72B on every benchmark, matches Claude Sonnet 4.6 on MuSR (67.7% vs. 68.0%), and reduces constraint violations by 53% relative to Opus 4.6 on TravelPlanner (oracle 0.028, random 0.231). The two routes are complementary: structural verification wins when constraints are checkable (the verifier captures signal frontier models cannot self-detect), while pretraining-scale priors win where they are not (narrative inference, code semantics). A cross-dataset confounding analysis confirms genuine quality discrimination on four reasoning tasks and identifies a model-identity shortcut on code, mitigated via last-layer retraining. Scorers trained on difficult data transfer zero-shot: a MuSR-trained scorer achieves 93.9% on GSM8K without seeing a math problem.

AI.tldr: A decomposed energy function combining learned quality scoring with constraint penalties verifies structured LLM outputs, using a small 149M-parameter verifier to orchestrate large generators, outperforming much larger models while reducing constraint violations by 53%.
AI.motivation: LLMs often produce structured outputs where individual steps seem correct but the overall output violates constraints, budgets, or test cases, requiring better verification methods for reliable structured generation.
AI.method: A decomposed energy function with learned quality scorer (heterogeneous ensemble of low-rank adapters on frozen encoder) and deterministic constraint penalties. Uses two-pass inference with ensemble mean for ranking and standard deviation for uncertainty quantification to trigger regeneration or abstention.
AI.result: The 149M-parameter verifier orchestrating 7-26B open generators outperforms Qwen-72B on every benchmark, matches Claude Sonnet on MuSR, reduces constraint violations by 53% vs Opus 4.6, and shows complementary benefits: structural verification works for checkable constraints while pretraining-scale priors work for narrative/code tasks.
AI.conclusion: Decomposed verification with learned quality scoring and constraint penalties provides effective structured output validation, with small verifiers effectively orchestrating large generators, demonstrating complementary strengths and showing strong transfer learning capabilities across reasoning tasks.

title: AutoResearchClaw: Self-Reinforcing Autonomous Research with Human-AI Collaboration
summary: Automating scientific discovery requires more than generating papers from ideas. Real research is iterative: hypotheses are challenged from multiple perspectives, experiments fail and inform the next attempt, and lessons accumulate across cycles. Existing autonomous research systems often model this process as a linear pipeline: they rely on single-agent reasoning, stop when execution fails, and do not carry experience across runs. We present AutoResearchClaw, a multi-agent autonomous research pipeline built on five mechanisms: structured multi-agent debate for hypothesis generation and result analysis, a self-healing executor with a \textsc{Pivot}/\textsc{Refine} decision loop that transforms failures into information, verifiable result reporting that prevents fabricated numbers and hallucinated citations, human-in-the-loop collaboration with seven intervention modes spanning full autonomy to step-by-step oversight, and cross-run evolution that converts past mistakes into future safeguards. On ARC-Bench, a 25-topic experiment-stage benchmark, AutoResearchClaw outperforms AI Scientist v2 by 54.7%. A human-in-the-loop ablation across seven intervention modes reveals that precise, targeted collaboration at high-leverage decision points consistently outperforms both full autonomy and exhaustive step-by-step oversight. We position AutoResearchClaw as a research amplifier that augments rather than replaces human scientific judgment. Code is available at https://github.com/aiming-lab/AutoResearchClaw.

AI.tldr: AutoResearchClaw is a multi-agent autonomous research pipeline that uses structured debate, self-healing execution, verifiable reporting, human collaboration, and cross-run evolution to outperform existing systems by 54.7% on scientific research benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing autonomous research systems have limitations: they model research as linear pipelines, use single-agent reasoning, stop when execution fails, and don't carry experience across runs. Real scientific discovery requires iterative processes where hypotheses are challenged, experiments fail and inform next attempts, and lessons accumulate.
AI.method: Built on five mechanisms: 1) Structured multi-agent debate for hypothesis generation and result analysis, 2) Self-healing executor with Pivot/Refine decision loop that transforms failures into information, 3) Verifiable result reporting preventing fabricated numbers and hallucinated citations, 4) Human-in-the-loop collaboration with seven intervention modes, 5) Cross-run evolution converting past mistakes into future safeguards.
