AI.conclusion: AMLE provides significantly better planner-admissible value extensions for sparse goal-conditioned planning compared to harmonic extension. The local action-gap certificate offers a principled theoretical guarantee for rollout success, and AMLE's superior empirical performance demonstrates its practical utility for planning problems with sparse labels.

title: Sequential Consensus for Multi-Agent LLM Debates: A Wald-SPRT compute governor with calibration-based failure detection
summary: Multi-agent LLM debate improves factuality and reasoning, but most recipes pick a fixed round count, over-spending on easy items and under-spending on hard ones. We adapt Wald's Sequential Probability Ratio Test (SPRT) as a plug-in compute governor for LLM debates. After each round, an LLM judge emits a [0,1] consensus score on the latest agent positions; a Wald monitor accumulates the log-likelihood ratio of "useful convergence" vs "not yet useful" under a Beta likelihood family, and stops when either boundary is crossed or returns a capped best-effort outcome at R_max. Under i.i.d. assumptions the rule inherits SPRT type-I/type-II error guarantees; in deployment the calibration itself is the more important object, since it estimates whether the judge score actually separates useful from unhelpful convergence in a given domain. We evaluate two tracks: (i) a Monte-Carlo study under calibrated Beta models characterising working curves, error rates, capping behaviour, and sensitivity; and (ii) a real-LLM evaluation on 200 attempted MMLU and 200 attempted GSM8K items with three heterogeneous agents (gpt-5, claude-opus-4-6, gemini-2.5-pro) and a claude-opus-4-6 judge, using disjoint 40-item calibration subsets. On GSM8K the rule stops in 1.01 average rounds (4.06 LLM calls) at 97.0% accuracy vs 99.0% for fixed-5 debate at 15 calls: a 3.7x call reduction at -2pp accuracy. On MMLU the calibrated KL collapses to about 0 and the rule caps on 99.5% of items at 2.1x cost. The takeaway is not that SPRT makes debate more accurate, but that a classical sequential test serves as a cheap compute-control and failure-detection layer for multi-agent LLM systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using Wald's Sequential Probability Ratio Test (SPRT) as an adaptive stopping rule for multi-agent LLM debates, reducing unnecessary computation on easy problems while allocating more to hard ones, achieving significant compute savings with minimal accuracy trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Traditional multi-agent LLM debates use fixed round counts, which inefficiently allocate computational resources - over-spending on easy problems and under-spending on hard ones. There's a need for adaptive mechanisms that can dynamically decide when a debate has reached useful convergence.
AI.method: The authors adapt Wald's SPRT as a compute governor for LLM debates. After each round, an LLM judge provides consensus scores on agent positions. A Wald monitor accumulates log-likelihood ratios between "useful convergence" vs "not yet useful" under a Beta likelihood family, stopping when either boundary is crossed or returning best-effort outcome at maximum rounds.
AI.result: On GSM8K: Achieves 97.0% accuracy with only 1.01 average rounds (4.06 LLM calls) vs 99.0% for fixed-5 debate at 15 calls - 3.7x call reduction with only -2pp accuracy drop. On MMLU: Calibration showed near-zero KL divergence, causing the rule to cap on 99.5% of items at 2.1x cost. The method doesn't improve accuracy but provides efficient compute control.
AI.conclusion: SPRT serves as an effective compute-control and failure-detection layer for multi-agent LLM systems, enabling significant computational savings with minimal accuracy trade-offs. The key insight is that classical sequential tests can be adapted to optimize resource allocation in modern LLM debate systems.

title: On-Device Continual Learning with Dual-Stage Buffer and Dynamic Loss for Point-of-Care Pneumonia Diagnosis
comment: Presented at 32nd Samsung Humantech Paper Awards
summary: Deep learning models detect pneumonia from chest X-rays with high accuracy, but the performance declines under domain shifts caused by differences in devices, patients, or institutions. We present PneumoNet, a domain-incremental learning method for point-of-care pneumonia diagnosis in resource-limited settings. PneumoNet combines a lightweight CNN for on-device prediction, a dual-stage balanced buffer for class-balanced replay, and a dynamic class-weighted loss to correct training-batch imbalances. Evaluated on a domain-shifted PneumoniaMNIST dataset simulating five realistic domain change scenarios, PneumoNet achieves 86.6% accuracy with 1.4% forgetting while being smaller and faster than existing baselines. These results highlight PneumoNet's potential to enable adaptive, privacy-preserving diagnostic AI directly on point-of-care medical devices in real-world and pandemic-ready healthcare.

AI.tldr: PneumoNet is a domain-incremental learning method that enables adaptive pneumonia diagnosis on point-of-care devices while maintaining performance under domain shifts with minimal forgetting.
AI.motivation: Deep learning models for pneumonia detection from chest X-rays suffer performance declines under domain shifts caused by differences in devices, patients, or institutions, especially in resource-limited settings. There's a need for adaptive, privacy-preserving diagnostic AI that can work directly on point-of-care medical devices.
AI.method: PneumoNet combines: 1) a lightweight CNN for on-device prediction, 2) a dual-stage balanced buffer for class-balanced replay to mitigate catastrophic forgetting, and 3) a dynamic class-weighted loss to correct training-batch imbalances during incremental learning.
AI.result: Evaluated on a domain-shifted PneumoniaMNIST dataset simulating five realistic domain change scenarios, PneumoNet achieves 86.6% accuracy with only 1.4% forgetting, while being smaller and faster than existing baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: PneumoNet demonstrates potential to enable adaptive, privacy-preserving diagnostic AI directly on point-of-care medical devices, making it suitable for real-world healthcare applications and pandemic-ready healthcare systems.

title: Worst-Group Equalized Odds Regularization for Multi-Attribute Fair Medical Image Classification
summary: Diagnostic performance in medical AI varies systematically across demographic groups, yet subgroup AUC can mask clinically important disparities. At a fixed inference-time operating point, some groups may exhibit over-diagnostic behaviour, characterized by elevated true and false positive rates, while others show under-diagnostic patterns with reduced true and false positive rates. These opposing tendencies can cancel in aggregate AUCs while producing meaningful inequities in clinical decision-making. Motivated by the need to assess and mitigate such disparities at the operating point and across multiple demographic attributes simultaneously, we propose a worst-group equalized-odds margin regularizer. The proposed regularizer explicitly targets subgroup-level deviations on both the true positive and false positive sides at inference. At each update, the method identifies subgroups defined by explicit demographic attributes (e.g., age, sex, and race) that exhibit the most extreme margin deviations and applies a unified penalty, enabling fairness optimization across multiple demographic axes without requiring explicit intersectional constraints. Across two medical imaging datasets in realistic multi-label settings, our method consistently reduces disparities in Equalized Odds and Equalized Opportunity with minimal impact on AUC, preserving diagnostic performance while improving fairness.

AI.tldr: A fairness regularizer that targets worst-group equalized odds deviations across multiple demographic attributes simultaneously, reducing disparities while preserving diagnostic performance.
AI.motivation: Subgroup AUC can mask clinically important disparities where groups exhibit over-diagnostic (elevated TP/FP rates) or under-diagnostic (reduced TP/FP rates) patterns that cancel in aggregate metrics, creating meaningful inequities in clinical decision-making at fixed operating points.
AI.method: Proposes a worst-group equalized-odds margin regularizer that identifies subgroups with the most extreme margin deviations on both TP and FP sides at each update, applies unified penalty across multiple demographic attributes without requiring explicit intersectional constraints.
AI.result: Method consistently reduces disparities in Equalized Odds and Equalized Opportunity metrics across two medical imaging datasets in realistic multi-label settings, with minimal impact on AUC (preserving diagnostic performance while improving fairness).
AI.conclusion: The proposed regularizer effectively addresses clinical fairness concerns by targeting worst-group deviations across multiple demographic attributes simultaneously, enabling practical fairness optimization in medical AI applications without sacrificing diagnostic accuracy.

title: DeRegiME: Deep Regime Mixtures for Probabilistic Forecasting under Distribution Shift
summary: We introduce DeRegiME -- Deep Regime Mixture of Experts -- a direct multi-horizon probabilistic forecaster that separates latent uncertainty regimes from the underlying signal and softly assigns each forecast location to learned recurring regimes using a sparse variational Gaussian process (GP) whose nonstationary regime-mixing kernel and Student-t likelihood combine per-regime sub-kernels and noise processes via a shared gate. This yields a single sparse-GP posterior, not a mixture of GP experts. DeRegiME addresses a key limitation of neural forecasters: point forecasts discard residual uncertainty, and probabilistic heads -- whether single marginals, uninterpreted mixtures, quantile sets, or diffusion samples -- rarely expose the regime structure of the residual. Yet distribution shift in noisy heteroskedastic time series may be abrupt, gradual, or horizon-dependent and often appears in residual uncertainty rather than the conditional mean. DeRegiME yields an interpretable mean-residual-noise decomposition with a direct-sum feature-space representation that anchors regimes as clusters of residual similarity whose transitions surface as implicit changepoints. The effective number of regimes is pruned by the stick-breaking gate. We prove kernel validity and predictive-density propriety, and across ten benchmarks and three encoder grids DeRegiME improves negative log predictive density (NLPD) by 20.3% over the strongest encoder-matched baseline, a DeepAR/GluonTS-style dynamic Student-t head, with parallel gains on CRPS (3.0%) and MSE (4.7%). Improvements are consistent across all datasets, which span abrupt, gradual, and seasonal shifts.

AI.tldr: DeRegiME is a probabilistic time series forecasting model that identifies latent uncertainty regimes using sparse variational Gaussian processes with a gate mechanism, improving forecasting accuracy across various distribution shift scenarios.
AI.motivation: Current neural forecasters have limitations: point forecasts discard residual uncertainty, and probabilistic methods rarely expose the underlying regime structure of residuals. Distribution shifts in noisy heteroskedastic time series often appear in residual uncertainty rather than just the conditional mean, and can be abrupt, gradual, or horizon-dependent.
AI.method: Uses a sparse variational Gaussian process with nonstationary regime-mixing kernel and Student-t likelihood that combines per-regime sub-kernels via a shared gate. It employs a stick-breaking gate mechanism that prunes the effective number of regimes and yields a single sparse-GP posterior rather than a mixture of GP experts.
AI.result: Across ten benchmarks and three encoder grids, DeRegiME improves negative log predictive density (NLPD) by 20.3% over the strongest encoder-matched baseline (DeepAR/GluonTS-style dynamic Student-t head), with gains of 3.0% on CRPS and 4.7% on MSE. Improvements are consistent across all datasets including abrupt, gradual, and seasonal shifts.
AI.conclusion: DeRegiME successfully addresses the limitation of neural forecasters by providing interpretable regime-aware probabilistic forecasting that can handle various types of distribution shifts, making residual uncertainty regimes explicit and improving forecasting performance across multiple metrics.

title: GAE Falls Short in Imperfect-Information Self-Play Reinforcement Learning
summary: Competitive multi-agent reinforcement learning in imperfect-information games requires agents to act under partial observability and against adversarial opponents, necessitating stochastic policies. While self-play reinforcement learning with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) has achieved strong empirical success, its standard advantage estimator, generalized advantage estimation, suffers from additional variance due to the sampling of stochastic future actions. This variance is amplified in equilibrium self-play because of the stochastic nature of the equilibrium policy and persists even when the critic is exact. We address this bottleneck by introducing $Q$-boosting, a variance-reduced advantage estimator based on a centralized action-value critic, and propose Variance-Reduced Policy Optimization (VRPO), incorporating this new estimator. The algorithm replaces sampled multi-step backups with a multi-step Expected SARSA$(λ)$ trace, computing policy expectations at each step to average out action-sampling noise, while retaining PPO's clipped objective and on-policy actor updates. Empirically, VRPO consistently achieves strong performance from mid-sized to large-scale games including Dou Dizhu and Heads-Up No-Limit Texas Hold'em.

AI.tldr: VRPO (Variance-Reduced Policy Optimization) introduces Q-boosting, a variance-reduced advantage estimator that uses Expected SARSA(λ) traces to reduce action-sampling noise in imperfect-information multi-agent games, achieving strong performance in games like Dou Dizhu and Texas Hold'em.
AI.motivation: Standard PPO with generalized advantage estimation suffers from additional variance in multi-agent imperfect-information games due to stochastic future action sampling. This variance is amplified in equilibrium self-play even with exact critics, creating a performance bottleneck.
AI.method: Proposes Q-boosting, a variance-reduced advantage estimator based on a centralized action-value critic, and VRPO algorithm that replaces sampled multi-step backups with multi-step Expected SARSA(λ) trace. Computes policy expectations at each step to average out action-sampling noise while retaining PPO's clipped objective and on-policy actor updates.
AI.result: VRPO consistently achieves strong performance across various scales of imperfect-information games including Dou Dizhu and Heads-Up No-Limit Texas Hold'em, demonstrating effectiveness of the variance reduction approach.
AI.conclusion: The Q-boosting advantage estimator and VRPO algorithm effectively reduce variance caused by stochastic action sampling in multi-agent imperfect-information games, enabling more stable and effective learning in competitive self-play settings.

title: Euclidean Embedding of Data Using Local Distances
summary: We study the problem of recovering a globally consistent Euclidean embedding of data, given only a local distance graph and propose a method that optimally represents these distances. The method operates solely on a neighborhood graph weighted by pairwise distances, without requiring any prior vector representation of the data. The embedding is obtained by solving a variational problem that matches local, on-graph distances to the Euclidean metric, induced by the differentials of the embedding functions. The resulting Euler-Lagrange equations are derived in a coordinate-free form, enabling direct evaluation of all operators from the distance graph alone. Though non-linear and missing an explicit expression for their non-linearity, these equations are shown to be resolved as an iteratively updated sparse linear problem. The main contributions of the proposed approach are (a) the derivation of the functional equations governing the optimal Euclidean embedding in the continuum, (b) a representation-free formulation that requires only a neighborhood distance graph and no feature vectors and (c) an estimation procedure based exclusively on local graph operations. We experimentally evaluate the resulting non-parametric algorithm on synthetic manifolds and real datasets, demonstrating consistent preservation of local metric structure and neighboring relations, while approximating the global isometric embedding.

AI.tldr: A graph-based method for Euclidean embedding that optimizes global consistency using only local distance graphs, solving variational problems with iterative sparse linear solutions.
AI.motivation: To recover globally consistent Euclidean embeddings from data when only local distance graphs are available, without requiring prior vector representations or feature vectors.
AI.method: Solves variational problems matching local on-graph distances to Euclidean metrics via differentials of embedding functions. Uses Euler-Lagrange equations in coordinate-free form, resolved as iteratively updated sparse linear problems. Operates solely on neighborhood graphs weighted by pairwise distances.
AI.result: Non-parametric algorithm that preserves local metric structure and neighboring relations while approximating global isometric embeddings. Demonstrated effective performance on synthetic manifolds and real datasets.
AI.conclusion: The method successfully achieves globally consistent Euclidean embeddings using only local distance graphs, providing a representation-free approach that relies exclusively on graph operations without needing vector representations.

title: Beyond Extrapolation: Knowledge Utilization Paradigm with Bidirectional Inspiration for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Time-series forecasting is critical in various scenarios, such as energy, transportation, and public health. However, most existing forecasters rely primarily on one-way inference, \textit{i.e.}, mapping \textbf{history} to \textbf{target}, and overlook the structural information provided by a revised natural chain (``\textbf{history} (model input) -- \textbf{target} (ground-truth output) -- \textbf{post-target continuation}''). The post-target continuation records how trajectories evolve after the target, which can help stabilize forecasting, but it is not observable at inference time. In this work, we aim to obtain an approximate proxy of the post-target continuation for the current input, providing structural knowledge for bidirectional forecasting. This idea is instantiated as KUP-BI (Knowledge Utilization Paradigm with Bidirectional Inspiration), a new time-series modeling paradigm that distills continuation-style knowledge (as an approximate post-target continuation proxy) from a \emph{train-only} historical library and integrates it into standard forecasting backbones. The input stream and the continuation-proxy stream are fused via a lightweight feature-level gating module. This design does not introduce information beyond what is already contained in the training trajectories; instead, it provides a structured inductive bias that helps backbones exploit typical continuation patterns rather than relying solely on parametric extrapolation. Experimental results on six public datasets show that KUP-BI consistently improves the forecasting performance of state-of-the-art models, with small additional overhead.

AI.tldr: KUP-BI: A new time-series forecasting paradigm that uses a historical library to generate approximate post-target continuation proxies as structural knowledge, enabling bidirectional forecasting and improving existing models with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Current time-series forecasters rely on one-way inference (history to target) and overlook the structural information in the natural chain of "history-target-post-target continuation." The post-target continuation provides valuable structural knowledge but isn't observable at inference time, creating a gap in available information for forecasting.
AI.method: Proposes KUP-BI (Knowledge Utilization Paradigm with Bidirectional Inspiration), which: 1) Distills continuation-style knowledge from a train-only historical library to create approximate post-target continuation proxies, 2) Integrates this knowledge into standard forecasting backbones via a lightweight feature-level gating module that fuses input and continuation-proxy streams, providing structured inductive bias without introducing new external information.
AI.result: Experimental results on six public datasets show KUP-BI consistently improves forecasting performance of state-of-the-art models with small additional computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: By leveraging historical continuation patterns as structural knowledge through approximate proxies, KUP-BI enables bidirectional forecasting that helps models exploit typical continuation patterns rather than relying solely on parametric extrapolation, leading to improved and stabilized forecasting performance across diverse datasets.

title: ExECG: An Explainable AI Framework for ECG models
summary: Deep learning has enabled ECG diagnostic models with strong performance in tasks such as arrhythmia classification and abnormality detection. However, accuracy alone is insufficient for clinical deployment because it does not explain why a specific output was produced, limiting justification, error analysis, and trust. Although ECG XAI has been extensively investigated and steadily improved, practical pipelines and reporting conventions vary across studies, hindering reuse and reproducibility. To address these issues, we present Explainable AI framework for ECG models (ExECG), a Python framework that provides a three-stage pipeline: Wrapper standardizes access across heterogeneous ECG formats and intermediate representations, Explainer unifies diverse XAI methods under a shared execution protocol, and Visualizer supports consistent cross-method comparison within a unified interface. We demonstrate end-to-end usage with concise examples and two case studies, highlighting interoperable and reproducible ECG explainability.

AI.tldr: ExECG is a Python framework that provides a standardized three-stage pipeline for explainable AI in ECG analysis, addressing reproducibility issues in current ECG XAI research.
AI.motivation: Current ECG diagnostic models using deep learning lack explainability, which limits clinical deployment. While ECG XAI research has progressed, practical pipelines and reporting conventions vary across studies, hindering reuse and reproducibility.
AI.method: A three-stage Python framework: 1) Wrapper for standardizing access across heterogeneous ECG formats, 2) Explainer for unifying diverse XAI methods under shared execution protocols, and 3) Visualizer for consistent cross-method comparison within unified interface.
AI.result: Demonstrated end-to-end usage with concise examples and two case studies, highlighting interoperable and reproducible ECG explainability.
AI.conclusion: ExECG addresses reproducibility and standardization issues in ECG XAI research by providing a unified framework that enables consistent implementation, comparison, and deployment of explainable AI methods for ECG analysis.

title: Backdooring Masked Diffusion Language Models
summary: Masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) are emerging as a compelling new paradigm for text generation, but their training-time security remains largely unexplored. Existing backdoor attacks on Gaussian diffusion models or autoregressive language models do not directly apply to MDLMs because MDLMs rely on discrete state corruption and iterative denoising rather than continuous noising or left-to-right prediction. In this work, we present the first systematic study of training-time backdoor attacks on MDLMs. We propose SHADOWMASK, a backdoor attack that modifies the MDLM forward corruption process by replacing the standard all-mask terminal distribution with a trigger-mask mixture prior. This creates a dedicated denoising pathway from trigger-corrupted states to attacker-specified targets while preserving clean denoising behavior. We further provide a principled mathematical formulation by defining the backdoored forward process, deriving the reverse-time posterior, and obtaining the continuous-time training objective. Evaluations on DiT-based MDLM and LLaDA-8B-Instruct across WikiText-103, OpenWebText, and Alpaca show that SHADOWMASK achieves near-100% attack success, substantially outperforms standard data poisoning, largely preserves clean utility, remains effective under full-model and parameter-efficient fine-tuning, and is robust against representative defenses.

AI.tldr: SHADOWMASK is the first systematic training-time backdoor attack on Masked Diffusion Language Models that modifies the forward corruption process with a trigger-mask mixture prior to create dedicated malicious denoising pathways while preserving clean behavior.
AI.motivation: Masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) are emerging as important text generation models, but their training-time security remains unexplored. Existing backdoor attacks on Gaussian diffusion or autoregressive language models don't apply to MDLMs due to their discrete state corruption and iterative denoising mechanisms.
AI.method: Propose SHADOWMASK, which modifies the MDLM forward corruption process by replacing the standard all-mask terminal distribution with a trigger-mask mixture prior. This creates dedicated denoising pathways from trigger-corrupted states to attacker-specified targets while preserving clean denoising. Provides mathematical formulation including backdoored forward process, reverse-time posterior derivation, and continuous-time training objective.
AI.result: Evaluation on DiT-based MDLM and LLaDA-8B-Instruct across WikiText-103, OpenWebText, and Alpaca shows: near-100% attack success, substantially outperforms standard data poisoning, largely preserves clean utility, remains effective under full-model and parameter-efficient fine-tuning, and is robust against representative defenses.
AI.conclusion: SHADOWMASK demonstrates the vulnerability of masked diffusion language models to training-time backdoor attacks, highlighting the need for security research in this emerging paradigm. The attack is highly effective, stealthy, and robust against existing defenses.

title: From Simple to Complex: Curriculum-Guided Physics-Informed Neural Networks via Gaussian Mixture Models
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) offer a mesh-free framework for solving partial differential equations (PDEs), yet training often suffers from gradient pathologies, spectral bias, and poor convergence, especially for problems with strong nonlinearity, sharp gradients, or multiscale features. We propose the Curriculum-Guided Gaussian Mixture Physics-Informed Neural Network (CGMPINN), which integrates Gaussian mixture modeling with dynamic curriculum learning. Specifically, a GMM is periodically fitted to the PDE residual distribution to quantify spatially varying learning difficulty. A smooth curriculum schedule progressively shifts training focus from easy to harder regions, while precision-based variance modulation suppresses unreliable clusters during early optimization. This dual curriculum is governed by a shared curriculum parameter and can be combined with self-adaptive loss balancing. We further establish theoretical guarantees, including sublinear convergence of the gradient norm for the induced time-varying loss, uniform equivalence between the curriculum-weighted and standard PDE losses, and a generalization bound with an explicit weighting-induced bias characterization. Experiments on six benchmark PDEs spanning elliptic, parabolic, hyperbolic, advection-dominated, and nonlinear reaction-diffusion types show that CGMPINN consistently achieves the lowest relative $L_2$ and maximum absolute errors among all compared methods, reducing relative $L_2$ error by up to 97.8\% over the standard PINN at comparable cost. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/Mathematics-Yang/CGMPINN.

AI.tldr: CGMPINN integrates Gaussian mixture modeling with dynamic curriculum learning to overcome PINN training issues like gradient pathologies and spectral bias, achieving significant error reduction across various PDE types.
AI.motivation: Standard PINNs suffer from training difficulties including gradient pathologies, spectral bias, and poor convergence, especially for problems with strong nonlinearity, sharp gradients, or multiscale features.
AI.method: Periodically fits GMM to PDE residual distribution to quantify learning difficulty, uses smooth curriculum schedule to shift focus from easy to hard regions, implements precision-based variance modulation, and can combine with self-adaptive loss balancing.
AI.result: Achieves lowest relative L2 and maximum absolute errors among compared methods on six benchmark PDEs (elliptic, parabolic, hyperbolic, advection-dominated, nonlinear reaction-diffusion), reducing relative L2 error by up to 97.8% over standard PINN at comparable cost.
AI.conclusion: CGMPINN effectively addresses PINN training challenges through curriculum-guided Gaussian mixture modeling, with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical performance across diverse PDE types.

title: CODA: Rewriting Transformer Blocks as GEMM-Epilogue Programs
summary: Transformer training systems are built around dense linear algebra, yet a nontrivial fraction of end-to-end time is spent on surrounding memory-bound operators. Normalization, activations, residual updates, reductions, and related computations repeatedly move large intermediate tensors through global memory while performing little arithmetic, making data movement an increasingly important bottleneck in otherwise highly optimized training stacks. We introduce CODA, a GPU kernel abstraction that expresses these computations as GEMM-plus-epilogue programs. CODA is based on the observation that many Transformer operators exposed as separate framework kernels can be algebraically reparameterized to execute while a GEMM output tile remains on chip, before it is written to memory. The abstraction fixes the GEMM mainloop and exposes a small set of composable epilogue primitives for scaling, reductions, pairwise transformations, and accumulation. This constrained interface preserves the performance structure of expert-written GEMMs while remaining expressive enough to cover nearly all non-attention computation in the forward and backward pass of a standard Transformer block. Across representative Transformer workloads, both human- and LLM-authored CODA kernels achieve high performance, suggesting that GEMM-plus-epilogue programming offers a practical path toward combining framework-level productivity with hardware-level efficiency.

AI.tldr: CODA introduces a GPU kernel abstraction that expresses Transformer memory-bound computations as GEMM-plus-epilogue programs, allowing them to execute while GEMM output tiles remain on-chip, significantly reducing data movement bottlenecks.
AI.motivation: Transformer training systems spend nontrivial time on memory-bound operators (normalization, activations, residual updates, reductions) that move large intermediate tensors through global memory with little arithmetic. Data movement has become an important bottleneck in otherwise optimized training stacks.
AI.method: CODA reparameterizes Transformer operators algebraically to execute as GEMM-plus-epilogue programs, fixing the GEMM mainloop while exposing composable epilogue primitives for scaling, reductions, pairwise transformations, and accumulation. This allows computations to execute while GEMM output tiles remain on-chip before being written to memory.
AI.result: CODA kernels achieve high performance across representative Transformer workloads, with both human- and LLM-authored implementations, suggesting the approach combines framework-level productivity with hardware-level efficiency.
AI.conclusion: GEMM-plus-epilogue programming offers a practical path to address data movement bottlenecks in Transformer training by enabling memory-bound computations to execute on-chip alongside GEMM operations, achieving both performance and productivity.

title: Rethinking Muon Beyond Pretraining: Spectral Failures and High-Pass Remedies for VLA and RLVR
summary: Muon is a matrix-aware optimizer that leverages Newton-Schulz (NS) iterations to enforce spectral gradient orthogonalization by driving all singular values of the momentum matrix toward 1. While this uniform spectral whitening enhances exploration and outperforms AdamW in LLM pretraining, we show it could lead to fundamental limitations beyond pretraining in two regimes: (i) cross-modality vision-language-action (VLA) training, where inherently low-rank action-module gradients cause amplification of noisy tail directions, and (ii) reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), where low-SNR gradients and the need to preserve per-head specialization from prior training make whitening unstable. To address these challenges, we propose Pion, a drop-in replacement for Muon that preserves its computational efficiency while replacing uniform spectral whitening with a two-stage Promotion+Suppression mechanism, which we call the high-pass NS iteration. This design induces a sharp spectral high-pass effect, anchoring dominant singular values at 1 while suppressing noisy tail components toward 0, with controllable filter strength. To preserve pretrained per-head heterogeneity, Pion also supports a per-head mode that applies updates independently across attention heads via a simple reshape, at no extra cost. In VLA training on LIBERO and LIBERO-Plus, Pion consistently outperforms both baselines across l_1-regression (VLA-Adapter) and flow-matching (VLANeXt) architectures, e.g., reaching 100% success rate on LIBERO Object after 1,500 training steps with VLA-Adapter, vs. 97.0% for Muon and only 32.2% for AdamW. The advantage of Pion further extends to a real Franka Research 3 robot with a pi_0.5 backbone under the DROID setup on three grasp-and-place tasks. In RLVR post-training on Qwen3-1.7B/4B with GRPO and GMPO, Pion also outperforms AdamW on MATH and GSM8K while Muon collapses to zero.

AI.tldr: Pion is a matrix-aware optimizer that improves upon Muon by replacing uniform spectral whitening with a high-pass filtering mechanism, enabling better performance in cross-modality training and reinforcement learning where Muon fails.
AI.motivation: Muon's uniform spectral whitening approach, while effective for LLM pretraining, has fundamental limitations in two regimes: (1) cross-modality vision-language-action training where low-rank gradients cause noise amplification, and (2) reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards where low-SNR gradients and the need to preserve per-head specialization make whitening unstable.
AI.method: Pion replaces Muon's uniform spectral whitening with a two-stage Promotion+Suppression mechanism called high-pass NS iteration. This creates a sharp spectral high-pass effect that anchors dominant singular values at 1 while suppressing noisy tail components toward 0, with controllable filter strength. Pion also supports a per-head mode that applies updates independently across attention heads via simple reshape at no extra cost.
AI.result: In VLA training on LIBERO and LIBERO-Plus, Pion consistently outperforms both Muon and AdamW across different architectures, achieving 100% success rate on LIBERO Object after 1,500 steps vs. 97.0% for Muon and 32.2% for AdamW. The advantage extends to real robot tasks. In RLVR post-training, Pion outperforms AdamW on MATH and GSM8K while Muon collapses to zero.
AI.conclusion: Pion successfully addresses Muon's limitations in cross-modality training and reinforcement learning settings while preserving computational efficiency, making it a versatile drop-in replacement that maintains Muon's pretraining benefits while extending applicability to more challenging optimization scenarios.

title: EviTrack: Selection over Sampling for Delayed Disambiguation
summary:
Sequential prediction is challenging in regimes of delayed disambiguation, where early observations are ambiguous and multiple latent explanations remain plausible until sufficient evidence accumulates. Standard approaches based on marginal inference struggle in this setting, either collapsing uncertainty prematurely or failing to recover once informative evidence arrives.
  We introduce EviTrack, a test-time inference framework that operates over latent trajectories rather than marginal states. EviTrack maintains a set of competing trajectory hypotheses and applies evidence- and likelihood-ratio-based selection to delay commitment until supported by data, drawing inspiration from hypothesis management in multiple hypothesis tracking and track-before-detect.
  To evaluate this setting, we construct a controlled synthetic benchmark with known latent ground truth that explicitly exhibits delayed disambiguation. At matched inference budget, EviTrack substantially outperforms sampling-based baselines, achieving faster post-disambiguation recovery.
  These results show that, in delayed disambiguation regimes, moderate trajectory-level selection is more effective than increasing sampling coverage, highlighting selection over sampling as a key principle for reliable sequential inference.

AI.tldr: EviTrack is a new inference framework that maintains multiple trajectory hypotheses during sequential prediction, using evidence-based selection to delay commitment until sufficient evidence arrives, outperforming sampling methods in delayed disambiguation scenarios.
AI.motivation: Standard marginal inference approaches struggle with delayed disambiguation regimes where early observations are ambiguous and multiple latent explanations remain plausible. These methods either collapse uncertainty too early or fail to recover once informative evidence arrives.
AI.method: EviTrack operates over latent trajectories rather than marginal states, maintaining a set of competing trajectory hypotheses. It applies evidence- and likelihood-ratio-based selection to delay commitment until supported by data, inspired by hypothesis management in multiple hypothesis tracking and track-before-detect.
AI.result: At matched inference budget, EviTrack substantially outperforms sampling-based baselines on a controlled synthetic benchmark with known latent ground truth, achieving faster post-disambiguation recovery.
AI.conclusion: In delayed disambiguation regimes, moderate trajectory-level selection is more effective than increasing sampling coverage, highlighting selection over sampling as a key principle for reliable sequential inference.

title: Cross-Paradigm Knowledge Distillation: A Comprehensive Study of Bidirectional Transfer Between Random Forests and Deep Neural Networks for Big Data Applications
summary: The exponential growth of big data has intensified the need for efficient and interpretable machine learning models that can handle diverse data characteristics while maintaining computational efficiency. Knowledge distillation has primarily focused on neural network-to-neural network transfer, leaving cross-paradigm knowledge transfer largely unexplored. This paper presents the first comprehensive study of bidirectional knowledge distillation between Random Forests (RF) and Deep Neural Networks (DNN), addressing critical gaps in ensemble learning and model compression for big data applications. We propose novel methodologies including progressive multi-stage distillation, multi-teacher ensemble distillation from diverse tree models, and uncertainty-aware cross-paradigm transfer mechanisms. Through 144 comprehensive experiments across 6 diverse datasets encompassing classification and regression tasks, we demonstrate that bidirectional RF-DL distillation achieves competitive performance while providing complementary benefits: interpretability from tree models and expressiveness from neural networks. Our results show that multi-teacher ensemble distillation consistently outperforms traditional approaches, with NN-COMPACT achieving 98.13% classification accuracy and NN-WIDE reaching 92.6% R^2 score in regression tasks. The proposed framework enables deployment flexibility in big data environments, allowing optimal model selection based on computational constraints and interpretability requirements. This work establishes a new research direction in cross-paradigm knowledge transfer with significant implications for interpretable AI and scalable model deployment in resource-constrained big data systems.

AI.tldr: First comprehensive study of bidirectional knowledge distillation between Random Forests and Deep Neural Networks, enabling cross-paradigm knowledge transfer with interpretability and expressiveness benefits.
AI.motivation: The exponential growth of big data requires efficient and interpretable ML models. Knowledge distillation has focused mainly on neural network-to-neural network transfer, leaving cross-paradigm knowledge transfer (especially between ensemble models like RF and DNNs) largely unexplored, creating gaps in ensemble learning and model compression for big data applications.
AI.method: Novel methodologies including: 1) progressive multi-stage distillation, 2) multi-teacher ensemble distillation from diverse tree models, and 3) uncertainty-aware cross-paradigm transfer mechanisms. Conducted 144 comprehensive experiments across 6 diverse datasets covering both classification and regression tasks.
AI.result: Bidirectional RF-DNN distillation achieves competitive performance with complementary benefits: interpretability from tree models and expressiveness from neural networks. Multi-teacher ensemble distillation consistently outperforms traditional approaches, with NN-COMPACT achieving 98.13% classification accuracy and NN-WIDE reaching 92.6% R² score in regression tasks.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a new research direction in cross-paradigm knowledge transfer with significant implications for interpretable AI and scalable model deployment in resource-constrained big data systems. The framework enables deployment flexibility allowing optimal model selection based on computational constraints and interpretability requirements.

title: A Two-Phase Adaptive Balanced Penalty Method for Controllable Pareto Front Learning under Split Feasibility Conditions
comment: 36 pages, 18 figures, 12 tables. Submitted to Neural Networks (Elsevier)
summary: We address the open problem of training hypernetworks for Controllable Pareto Front Learning (CPFL) under split feasibility conditions with rigorous theoretical guarantees. We reformulate the constrained Pareto problem as a Bi-Level Scalarized Split Problem (BSSP) and propose the Adaptive Balanced Penalty (ABP) algorithm, whose three gradient components -- optimality, set feasibility, and image feasibility -- are blended through an adaptive indicator driven by a computable lower bound. Using a novel convex surrogate technique, we prove full-sequence convergence under standard convexity and Robbins-Monro step-size assumptions. The ABP penalty structure is then translated into a two-phase, feasibility-first training strategy for Hyper-MLP and HyperTrans architectures (ABP-HyperNet). To evaluate constrained CPFL, we introduce the Expected Feasible Hypervolume (EFHV), which jointly captures solution quality and constraint satisfaction. Experiments on five multi-objective benchmarks validate the ABP solver against ground truth, while three multi-task learning datasets demonstrate that ABP-HyperNet achieves up to 2.3x higher EFHV than unconstrained baselines by raising feasibility from 36-49% to 87-100%.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes ABP-HyperNet, a novel method for training hypernetworks for Controllable Pareto Front Learning under split feasibility constraints, with theoretical convergence guarantees and improved constraint satisfaction.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the open problem of training hypernetworks for Controllable Pareto Front Learning under split feasibility conditions, which lacks rigorous theoretical guarantees. Current approaches struggle with constraint satisfaction in constrained Pareto optimization problems.
AI.method: The authors reformulate the constrained Pareto problem as a Bi-Level Scalarized Split Problem and propose the Adaptive Balanced Penalty algorithm. This algorithm blends three gradient components (optimality, set feasibility, image feasibility) using an adaptive indicator. They then translate this penalty structure into a two-phase, feasibility-first training strategy for Hyper-MLP and HyperTrans architectures (ABP-HyperNet).
AI.result: Experiments on five multi-objective benchmarks show ABP solver validates against ground truth. On three multi-task learning datasets, ABP-HyperNet achieves up to 2.3x higher Expected Feasible Hypervolume than unconstrained baselines, raising feasibility from 36-49% to 87-100%.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ABP-HyperNet framework successfully addresses the challenge of constrained Pareto front learning with hypernetworks, providing both theoretical convergence guarantees and practical improvements in constraint satisfaction and solution quality through the novel EFHV metric.

title: An Objective Performance Evaluation of the LSTM Networks in Time Series Classification
comment: Accepted in 2026 29th International Conference on Information Fusion
summary: The rapid adoption of deep learning has increasingly led to data-driven models replacing classical model-based algorithms, even in domains governed by well-understood physical laws. While data-driven models, such as long short-term memory (LSTM) networks, have become a popular choice for time-series analysis, their performance relative to model-based approaches in structured environments is rarely evaluated objectively. This paper presents a performance evaluation framework comparing an LSTM classifier against a model-based expectation maximization (EM) classifier for binary time-series classification. The evaluation is conducted on two scalar linear Gaussian state space models differing only in their noise statistics, where the Kalman filter likelihood ratio test with true parameters serves as a reference for the best achievable classification performance.Through Monte Carlo simulations, the classifiers are evaluated across three axes: task difficulty, controlled by the separation in process or measurement noise between the two models; sequence length; and training dataset size. The results show that the EM classifier, which exploits the known model structure, performs strongly when the data conform to the assumed model class. The LSTM classifier requires a larger separation in noise statistics to achieve reliable classification, and its performance saturates below the reference classifier when the models differ only in measurement noise, regardless of sequence length or training dataset size.

AI.tldr: The paper compares LSTM networks against model-based EM classifiers for time-series classification, finding that EM outperforms LSTM when data matches the assumed model structure, with LSTM requiring larger parameter differences and performing worse especially for measurement noise differences.
AI.motivation: There's a rapid shift toward data-driven deep learning models replacing classical model-based algorithms, even in domains with well-understood physics. However, there's a lack of objective evaluation comparing data-driven models (like LSTM) against model-based approaches in structured environments.
AI.method: The paper presents a performance evaluation framework comparing an LSTM classifier against a model-based EM classifier for binary time-series classification. The evaluation uses two scalar linear Gaussian state space models differing only in noise statistics. Monte Carlo simulations evaluate performance across three axes: task difficulty (separation in noise statistics), sequence length, and training dataset size. The Kalman filter likelihood ratio test with true parameters serves as the reference for best achievable performance.
AI.result: EM classifier, exploiting known model structure, performs strongly when data conforms to assumed model class. LSTM classifier requires larger separation in noise statistics to achieve reliable classification, and its performance saturates below the reference classifier when models differ only in measurement noise, regardless of sequence length or training dataset size.
AI.conclusion: Model-based approaches (EM) remain superior to data-driven methods (LSTM) in structured environments where the underlying physics are well understood, highlighting the importance of leveraging domain knowledge rather than blindly applying deep learning models.

title: Inference-Time Scaling in Diffusion Models through Iterative Partial Refinement
comment: Accepted at the ICLR 2026 Workshop on AI with Recursive Self-Improvement
summary: Inference-time scaling has emerged as a major approach for improving reasoning capabilities, and has been increasingly applied to diffusion models. However, existing inference-time scaling methods for diffusion models typically rely on external verifiers or reward models to rank and select samples, limiting their scalability to settings where such evaluators are available and reliable. Moreover, while recent diffusion models perform sequential inference with region-wise, mixed-noise conditioning, inference-time scaling tailored to this setting remains relatively underexplored. We propose Iterative Partial Refinement (IPR), an inference-time scaling method for sequential diffusion that requires no external verifier. Starting from an already-generated sample, IPR re-noises a subset of regions and regenerates them conditioned on the remaining regions, enabling the model to revise earlier decisions under a richer context than was available during the initial generation. This iterative partial refinement produces more globally consistent samples without external verification. On reasoning tasks requiring global constraint satisfaction, IPR consistently improves performance: on MNIST Sudoku, the valid solution rate increases from 55.8% to 75.0%. These results show that iterative partial refinement alone can serve as an effective inference-time scaling strategy for diffusion models in sequential, mixed-noise settings. Code is available at: https://github.com/ahn-ml/IPR

AI.tldr: IPR is an inference-time scaling method for sequential diffusion models that improves reasoning capabilities by iteratively re-noising and regenerating subsets of regions without needing external verifiers or reward models.
AI.motivation: Existing inference-time scaling methods for diffusion models rely on external verifiers or reward models for sample ranking/selection, which limits scalability to settings where such evaluators are available and reliable. Additionally, there's a gap in inference-time scaling methods specifically designed for sequential diffusion models with region-wise, mixed-noise conditioning.
AI.method: Iterative Partial Refinement (IPR) starts from an already-generated sample and re-noises a subset of regions, then regenerates them conditioned on the remaining regions. This allows the model to revise earlier decisions under a richer context than was available during initial generation, enabling more globally consistent samples without external verification.
AI.result: On MNIST Sudoku reasoning tasks requiring global constraint satisfaction, IPR increases the valid solution rate from 55.8% to 75.0%. The method consistently improves performance on reasoning tasks requiring global constraint satisfaction.
AI.conclusion: Iterative partial refinement alone can serve as an effective inference-time scaling strategy for diffusion models in sequential, mixed-noise settings, demonstrating that significant improvements can be achieved without external verification mechanisms.

title: BrainDyn: A Sheaf Neural ODE for Generative Brain Dynamics
summary: Efficient neural network models that generate brain-like dynamic activity can be a valuable resource for generating synthetic data, analyzing differences in brain transients under conditions such as testing perturbation activity or inferring the underlying generative dynamics. However, large language models (LLMs) or standard recurrent neural networks (RNNs) ignore the anatomical organization and therefore do not produce components that align with brain regions. On the other hand, graph-based networks often have very simple message passing rules that are not sufficiently expressive for brain-like dynamics. To address this, we introduce BrainDyn, a sheaf neural ordinary differential equation (neural ODE) model for continuous-time dynamics on structured brain graphs. BrainDyn encodes the recent activity history of each brain region using a long short-term memory (LSTM) model over a sliding temporal window to produce hidden states, or stalks, that are projected through learnable restriction maps into edge-specific shared spaces. Discrepancies between neighboring nodes in these shared spaces are characterized by a sheaf Laplacian that can facilitate message passing between neuronal units. The output of these messages is then fed to a neural ODE that governs the continuous-time evolution of neuronal activity. We evaluated BrainDyn on resting-state fMRI (PNC dataset), scalp EEG with focal epilepsy (TUSZ dataset), and simulated activity from the NEST spiking network simulator. BrainDyn achieves strong forecasting ability across modalities, and the resulting representations support downstream tasks including in silico perturbation prediction.

AI.tldr: BrainDyn is a sheaf neural ODE model for generating brain-like dynamic activity on structured brain graphs, combining LSTM-based history encoding with graph-based message passing and continuous-time evolution.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches like LLMs/RNNs ignore anatomical organization, while graph-based networks have overly simple message passing that fails to capture complex brain dynamics, creating a gap for models that can generate realistic, region-aligned brain activity.
AI.method: BrainDyn uses: 1) LSTM over sliding windows to encode recent activity history for each brain region, producing hidden states (stalks); 2) Learnable restriction maps projecting stalks into edge-specific shared spaces; 3) Sheaf Laplacian to characterize discrepancies between neighboring nodes; 4) Neural ODE for continuous-time evolution of neuronal activity.
AI.result: BrainDyn achieves strong forecasting performance across resting-state fMRI (PNC dataset), scalp EEG with epilepsy (TUSZ dataset), and simulated spiking activity (NEST simulator). The learned representations enable downstream tasks like in silico perturbation prediction.
AI.conclusion: BrainDyn effectively bridges the gap between anatomical structure and expressive dynamics, providing a valuable resource for generating synthetic brain data, analyzing transients, and studying perturbation effects while maintaining brain region alignment.

title: An Exterior Method for Nonnegative Matrix Factorization
summary: Nonnegative matrix factorization (NMF) seeks a low-rank approximation $X \approx UV^T$ with nonnegative factors and is commonly solved using interior methods that enforce feasibility throughout optimization. We show that such constraint-driven approaches can impede progress in the nonconvex landscape, leading to slow convergence or convergence to suboptimal stationary points. We propose an exterior framework for NMF (eNMF) that separates low-rank approximation from nonnegativity enforcement. Our method initializes from the optimal unconstrained factorization and introduces a rotation procedure that maps unconstrained factors to an exterior point closest to the nonnegative orthant. This viewpoint yields an algorithmic framework in which simple iterative updates converge to KKT-satisfying stationary points on the boundary of the positive orthant. The exterior formulation also enables a geometric interpretation of NMF solutions, clarifying equivalence classes of factorizations under permutation and orthogonal transformations. An intriguing numerical result, involving 400 NMF experiments across both real and synthetic datasets, show that in 99% of the cases, different algorithms tend to converge towards equivalent factor matrices. We benchmark eNMF against 9 state-of-the-art NMF algorithms with 9 initialization schemes across 3 real-world and 2 synthetic datasets. eNMF consistently outperforms all 81 competitors, achieving up to 30% lower reconstruction error under equal-time settings and up to 150% speedup under equal-error settings. The downstream experiments further demonstrate substantial performance gains in audio processing and recommendation tasks, corroborating the practical benefits of the proposed exterior optimization framework. Code is available at https://github.com/roychowdhuryresearch/eNMF

AI.tldr: eNMF proposes an exterior optimization framework for NMF that separates low-rank approximation from nonnegativity enforcement, outperforming existing methods in accuracy, speed, and practical applications.
AI.motivation: Traditional interior methods for NMF enforce nonnegativity constraints throughout optimization, which can impede progress in the nonconvex landscape, leading to slow convergence or convergence to suboptimal stationary points.
AI.method: Proposes an exterior framework that initializes from the optimal unconstrained factorization, then uses a rotation procedure to map unconstrained factors to an exterior point closest to the nonnegative orthant, with simple iterative updates converging to KKT-satisfying stationary points.
AI.result: Outperformed 9 state-of-the-art NMF algorithms with 9 initialization schemes across multiple datasets, achieving up to 30% lower reconstruction error under equal-time settings and up to 150% speedup under equal-error settings. Also discovered that in 99% of cases, different algorithms converge to equivalent factor matrices.
AI.conclusion: The exterior optimization framework provides superior performance over traditional interior methods, enables geometric interpretation of solutions, and demonstrates practical benefits in downstream applications like audio processing and recommendation tasks.

title: What Makes a Representation Good for Single-Cell Perturbation Prediction?
summary: Single-cell perturbation modeling is fundamental for understanding and predicting cellular responses to genetic perturbations. However, existing approaches, from causal representation learning to foundation models, often struggle with an overlooked challenge: gene expression is dominated by perturbation-invariant information, while perturbation-specific signals are intrinsically sparse. As a result, learned representations either entangle invariant and perturbation-specific information, leading to spurious and non-generalizable predictors, or suppress perturbation-specific signals altogether, rendering them ineffective for prediction. To address this, we propose PerturbedVAE, a general framework designed to resolve this signal imbalance. The framework explicitly separates perturbation-specific information from dominant invariant structure and recovers causal representations to effectively utilize such information for prediction. We further provide an identifiability analysis that characterizes the conditions under which sparse perturbation effects can be reliably recovered, thereby clarifying how the framework can be concretely specified under such conditions. Empirically, PerturbedVAE achieves state-of-the-art performance on a widely used benchmark across multiple evaluation settings, yielding significant gains on out-of-distribution combinatorial predictions and uncovering interpretable perturbation-response programs.

AI.tldr: PerturbedVAE is a framework that addresses signal imbalance in single-cell perturbation modeling by separating perturbation-specific information from dominant invariant structure to improve prediction of cellular responses to genetic perturbations.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches for single-cell perturbation modeling struggle because gene expression is dominated by perturbation-invariant information while perturbation-specific signals are sparse. This leads to either entangled representations with spurious predictors or suppression of perturbation signals entirely, making them ineffective for prediction.
AI.method: PerturbedVAE is a general framework that explicitly separates perturbation-specific information from the dominant invariant structure and recovers causal representations to effectively utilize this information for prediction. The authors also provide identifiability analysis characterizing conditions for reliable recovery of sparse perturbation effects.
AI.result: Empirically, PerturbedVAE achieves state-of-the-art performance on a widely used benchmark across multiple evaluation settings, yielding significant gains on out-of-distribution combinatorial predictions and uncovering interpretable perturbation-response programs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PerturbedVAE framework successfully addresses the signal imbalance challenge in single-cell perturbation modeling by separating invariant and perturbation-specific information, leading to improved prediction performance and interpretable results while providing theoretical guarantees through identifiability analysis.

title: Accurate, Efficient, and Explainable Deep Learning Approaches for Environmental Science Problems
summary: Environmental science plays a pivotal role in safeguarding ecosystems, a domain driven by large-scale, heterogeneous data. In the big data era, artificial intelligence (AI) has emerged as a transformative tool for learning patterns and supporting decision-making. This dissertation develops AI-based approaches tailored to complex environmental science problems to achieve Environmental Intelligence, studying three specific challenges. First, we focus on flood prediction and management in coastal river systems. Conventional physics-based models are computationally intensive, limiting real-time application. To overcome this, we propose a deep learning (DL)-based model, WaLeF, for water level forecasting, and a forecast-informed DL model, FIDLAr, to manage water levels. Evaluated in a flood-prone coastal system in South Florida characterized by extreme rainfall and sea level fluctuations, FIDLAr outperforms baselines in accuracy and efficiency while providing interpretable outputs. Second, we target global weather prediction, which is challenged by massive data scale. Traditional physics methods are deterministic and computationally heavy. We propose CoDiCast, a conditional diffusion model tailored for probabilistic weather forecasting. Adapted from generative AI for predictive tasks, experiments show CoDiCast achieves accurate, efficient forecasts with explicit uncertainty quantification. Lastly, we address scientific question-answering in environmental science. When answering in-domain questions, large language models (LLMs) often suffer from hallucinations due to out-of-date or limited knowledge. While retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) retrieves domain-specific knowledge, existing methods trade off accuracy, efficiency, or explainability. We propose Hypercube-RAG, built on a structured text cube framework, which successfully exhibits all three properties simultaneously.

AI.tldr: This dissertation develops three AI-based approaches for environmental science: deep learning models for flood prediction, diffusion models for probabilistic weather forecasting, and a structured RAG framework for scientific QA, collectively advancing Environmental Intelligence.
AI.motivation: Environmental science faces challenges with large-scale heterogeneous data, computationally intensive physics-based models, and AI limitations like LLM hallucinations. There's a need for specialized AI approaches that are efficient, accurate, and interpretable for complex environmental problems.
AI.method: Three approaches: 1) WaLeF (deep learning for water level forecasting) and FIDLAr (forecast-informed DL for water management); 2) CoDiCast (conditional diffusion model for probabilistic weather forecasting); 3) Hypercube-RAG (structured text cube framework for retrieval-augmented generation in scientific QA).
AI.result: FIDLAr outperformed baselines in accuracy and efficiency for flood prediction in South Florida. CoDiCast achieved accurate, efficient weather forecasts with uncertainty quantification. Hypercube-RAG successfully balanced accuracy, efficiency, and explainability for environmental science QA.
AI.conclusion: AI-based approaches can effectively address complex environmental science challenges, providing efficient, accurate, and interpretable solutions for flood management, weather prediction, and scientific question-answering, advancing the field of Environmental Intelligence.

title: The Evaluation Game: Beyond Static LLM Benchmarking
summary:
As jailbreaks, adversarially crafted inputs that bypass safety constraints, continue to be discovered in Large Language Models, practitioners increasingly rely on fine-tuning as a defensive strategy. Yet the theoretical foundations underlying this robustness fine-tuning remain underexplored. We introduce a game-theoretic framework in which the interaction between an evaluator (auditing the model for jailbreaks) and a trainer is formalized as a two-player game.
  A key feature of our approach is the use of group actions, a mathematical structure that captures symmetries and transformations, to formally represent data augmentation. The simplest non-trivial instance is the circle with cyclic translation groups, where we exhibit various regimes depending on the trainer's generalization range. Below a critical threshold, the evaluator maintains a constant miss ratio for linearly many rounds, whereas other settings can yield very different behaviors. We further provide empirical evidence supporting locality-dependence of the model: for the three model families we tested (Llama, Qwen and Mistral), we have significant evidence that fine-tuning on adversarial prompts induces only local generalization, with refusal rates on test examples highly correlated with the distance to the fine-tuning prompts. Our framework recasts the central object of adversarial evaluation: a benchmark is not a static set of prompts but an orbit under the evaluator's group action, and audit protocols that ignore trainer-side adaptation cannot distinguish a genuine fix from a memorized patch.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a game-theoretic framework for understanding jailbreak defense in LLMs, showing that fine-tuning creates only local generalization patterns that depend on distance to training data, revealing benchmarks should be considered as orbits under group actions rather than static sets.
AI.motivation: Jailbreaks continue to be discovered in LLMs, and practitioners rely on fine-tuning as a defensive strategy, but the theoretical foundations of this robustness fine-tuning remain underexplored. There's a need to understand the game dynamics between evaluators auditing for jailbreaks and trainers defending models.
AI.method: The authors introduce a game-theoretic framework formalizing the interaction between evaluator and trainer as a two-player game. They use group actions to mathematically represent data augmentation symmetries and transformations, with the circle and cyclic translation groups as the simplest non-trivial instance. They analyze different regimes based on trainer's generalization range and provide empirical evidence across three model families (Llama, Qwen, Mistral).
AI.result: Below a critical threshold, evaluator maintains constant miss ratio for linearly many rounds, while other settings yield different behaviors. Empirical results show fine-tuning on adversarial prompts induces only local generalization, with refusal rates highly correlated with distance to fine-tuning prompts. Models exhibit locality-dependence across all tested families.
AI.conclusion: The framework reconceptualizes adversarial evaluation: benchmarks should be seen as orbits under evaluator's group actions rather than static prompt sets. Audit protocols that ignore trainer-side adaptation cannot distinguish genuine fixes from memorized patches, highlighting fundamental limitations in current jailbreak defense evaluation approaches.

title: Understanding Dynamics of Adam in Zero-Sum Games: An ODE Approach
summary: The remarkable success of the Adam in training neural networks has naturally led to the widespread use of its descent-ascent counterpart, Adam-DA, for solving zero-sum games. Despite its popularity in practice, a rigorous theoretical understanding of Adam-DA still lags behind. In this paper, we derive ordinary differential equations (ODEs) that serve as continuous-time limits of the Adam-DA. These ODEs closely approximate the discrete-time dynamics of Adam-DA, providing a tractable analytical framework for understanding its behavior in zero-sum games. Using this ODE approach, we investigate two fundamental aspects of Adam-DA: local convergence and implicit gradient regularization. Our analysis reveals that the roles of the first- and second-order momentum parameters in zero-sum games are exactly the opposite of their well-documented effects in minimization problems. We validate these predictions through GAN experiments across multiple architectures and datasets, demonstrating the practical implications of this reversed momentum effect.

AI.tldr: Adam-DA for zero-sum games exhibits opposite momentum effects compared to minimization problems, with first-order momentum acting as gradient regularization and second-order momentum as acceleration.
AI.motivation: While Adam-DA (descent-ascent counterpart of Adam) is widely used for solving zero-sum games like GANs, there's a lack of rigorous theoretical understanding of its behavior and properties in this context.
AI.method: Derived ordinary differential equations (ODEs) as continuous-time limits of Adam-DA to approximate discrete-time dynamics, then used this analytical framework to investigate local convergence and implicit gradient regularization properties.
AI.result: Found that first- and second-order momentum parameters in zero-sum games have opposite effects compared to minimization problems - specifically, first-order momentum acts as implicit gradient regularization while second-order momentum serves as acceleration.
AI.conclusion: The reversed momentum effect in Adam-DA for zero-sum games has practical implications validated through GAN experiments, providing crucial theoretical insights for optimization in adversarial settings.

title: TIDE: Asymmetric Neural Circuits for Stabilized Temporal Inhibitory-Excitatory Dynamics
summary: Recent Continuous Thought Machine architecture decouples internal computation from external inputs via neural dynamics, but relies on multi-layer perceptrons without stability guarantees. We propose to model neural dynamics using asymmetric Excitatory-Inhibitory (E-I) networks, which can be stabilized via principles from network theory and can be expressed as energy-based systems optimized through a game-theoretic loss. Building on this perspective, we introduce Temporal Inhibitory-Excitatory Dynamic Engine (TIDE), a neuro-inspired architecture that computes internal representations through neural dynamics stabilized by incorporating the Wilson-Cowan dynamics and lateral inhibition. TIDE balances biological realism by, for instance, using Hierarchical Receptive Fields and enforcing Dale's principle to ensure a realistic $80:20$ E-I balance ratio with an end-to-end trainable architecture. The aim of this paper is to introduce a new architecture that brings neuro-inspired learning to the forefront. We present proofs of convergence, stability, and complexity bounds, along with empirical ablation studies. Overall, TIDE surpasses CTM with under $50\%$ of the training time and improves $\texttt{top-1}$ accuracy by an average of $+1.65\%$ on ImageNet under various perturbations.

AI.tldr: TIDE is a neuro-inspired architecture using asymmetric E-I networks with Wilson-Cowan dynamics for stable neural computation, achieving better accuracy with less training time than CTM.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses limitations in Continuous Thought Machine (CTM) architecture which decouples internal computation from external inputs but lacks stability guarantees. Current approaches use multi-layer perceptrons without proper stability mechanisms.
AI.method: Proposes Temporal Inhibitory-Excitatory Dynamic Engine (TIDE) that models neural dynamics using asymmetric Excitatory-Inhibitory networks stabilized via network theory principles. Incorporates Wilson-Cowan dynamics, lateral inhibition, Hierarchical Receptive Fields, enforces Dale's principle for 80:20 E-I balance ratio, and is end-to-end trainable. Uses energy-based systems optimized through game-theoretic loss.
AI.result: TIDE surpasses CTM with under 50% of training time and improves top-1 accuracy by average +1.65% on ImageNet under various perturbations. Includes proofs of convergence, stability, complexity bounds, and empirical ablation studies.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully introduces a neuro-inspired architecture (TIDE) that combines biological realism with computational efficiency, offering stable neural dynamics and improved performance over existing approaches while bringing neuro-inspired learning to the forefront.

title: A Bitter Lesson for Data Filtering
summary: We investigate data filtering for large model pretraining via new scaling studies that target the high compute, data-scarce regime. In spite of an apparently common belief that filtering data to include only high-quality information is essential, our experiments suggest that with enough compute, the best data filter is no data filter. We find that sufficiently trained large parameter models not only tolerate low-quality and distractor data, but in fact benefit from nominally ``poor'' data.

AI.tldr: Large models don't need data filtering when trained with sufficient compute; they can benefit from "poor" data.
AI.motivation: The paper challenges the common belief that filtering data to include only high-quality information is essential for large model pretraining, particularly in the high compute, data-scarce regime where data quality concerns are most prominent.
AI.method: The authors conduct new scaling studies that specifically target the high compute, data-scarce regime to investigate data filtering for large model pretraining.
AI.result: Their experiments show that with enough compute, the best data filter is no data filter. Sufficiently trained large parameter models not only tolerate low-quality and distractor data, but actually benefit from nominally "poor" data.
AI.conclusion: Data filtering may be unnecessary for large model training when sufficient compute is available, as models can learn effectively from diverse data including low-quality samples, challenging conventional wisdom about data curation.

title: Unlocking the Potential of Continual Model Merging: An ODE Perspective
summary: Continual Model Merging (CMM) enables rapid customization of foundation models across sequentially arriving tasks, offering a scalable alternative to repeated retraining. However, existing merging rules lack explicit controllability over the allocation of learning capacity between previously learned capabilities and newly merged models. Consequently, as tasks are merged sequentially, this deficiency accumulates into severe forgetting, particularly in scenarios with heterogeneous task importance, where performance allocation becomes highly inconsistent. The key reason can be attributed to the fact that previous methods treat each task model as an isolated parameter point and apply fixed algebraic combinations, rather than explicitly constructing a transition that respects how independently trained models can be connected in parameter space. Motivated by mode connectivity, we assume that desirable merged models lie on low loss connecting paths, and that continual merging should follow such paths without crossing loss barriers that induce forgetting. Grounded in these insights, we propose a novel ODE-driven Merging (ODE-M) tailored for CMM that traces such a path by integrating a time-dependent velocity field and enforcing barrier constraints to prevent loss-increasing steps. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ODE-M achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to its competitors across mainstream CMM benchmarks.

AI.tldr: ODE-M is a novel continual model merging method that uses ODE-driven paths and barrier constraints to prevent forgetting in sequential task merging, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Current continual model merging methods lack explicit controllability over learning capacity allocation between old and new tasks, leading to severe forgetting when tasks have heterogeneous importance. Previous methods treat tasks as isolated parameter points with fixed algebraic combinations rather than constructing proper parameter space transitions.
AI.method: Proposes ODE-driven Merging (ODE-M) that traces low-loss connecting paths in parameter space by integrating time-dependent velocity fields and enforcing barrier constraints to prevent loss-increasing steps, leveraging mode connectivity principles.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show ODE-M achieves state-of-the-art performance across mainstream continual model merging benchmarks compared to competitors.
AI.conclusion: ODE-M effectively addresses forgetting in continual model merging by following low-loss connecting paths with barrier constraints, providing a principled approach that respects parameter space connectivity between independently trained models.

title: When to Stop Reusing: Dynamic Gradient Gating for Sample-Efficient RLVR
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become the dominant paradigm for advanced reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs), but rollout samples are expensive to obtain, making sample efficiency a critical bottleneck. A natural remedy is to reuse each rollout batch for multiple gradient updates, a standard practice in classical RL. Yet in RLVR, this amplifies policy shift, leading to severe performance degradation. Detecting the onset of degradation early enough to stop reuse remains an open and challenging problem. We close this gap by identifying the \textit{Disproportionate Weight Divergence (DWD)} phenomenon: performance degradation is synchronized with a sharp surge in the \texttt{lm\_head} weight change, while intermediate layers remain stable. Empirically, we verify that DWD emerges consistently across diverse LLMs and tasks. Theoretically, we prove that (i) harmful gradients concentrate at the \texttt{lm\_head} while intermediate layers are structurally attenuated, and (ii) the \texttt{lm\_head} gradient norm lower-bounds the policy divergence. These results establish the \texttt{lm\_head} gradient norm as a principled, real-time signal of catastrophic policy shift.
