Guided by this insight, we propose \textit{Dynamic Gradient Gating (DGG)}, a lightweight intervention that monitors the \texttt{lm\_head} gradient norm in real time and intercepts harmful gradients before they corrupt the optimizer. DGG consistently matches or exceeds the standard single-use baseline, achieving up to $2.93\times$ sample efficiency and $2.14\times$ wall-clock speedup across math, ALFWorld, WebShop, and search-augmented QA tasks.

AI.tldr: RLVR suffers from performance degradation when reusing rollout batches, but monitoring lm_head gradient norms enables early detection of harmful policy shifts via Dynamic Gradient Gating, achieving significant sample efficiency gains.
AI.motivation: RLVR is expensive due to costly rollout samples, so batch reuse is needed for efficiency, but this amplifies policy shift and causes severe performance degradation. Current methods lack effective early detection of when degradation begins.
AI.method: Identifies the Disproportionate Weight Divergence (DWD) phenomenon where performance degradation correlates with sharp lm_head weight changes. Proves harmful gradients concentrate at lm_head while intermediate layers are attenuated. Introduces Dynamic Gradient Gating (DGG) that monitors lm_head gradient norms in real-time to intercept harmful gradients before optimizer corruption.
AI.result: DWD phenomenon consistently emerges across diverse LLMs and tasks. DGG matches or exceeds single-use baseline, achieving up to 2.93× sample efficiency and 2.14× wall-clock speedup across math, ALFWorld, WebShop, and search-augmented QA tasks.
AI.conclusion: The lm_head gradient norm serves as a principled, real-time signal for catastrophic policy shift detection. DGG provides a lightweight intervention that enables safe batch reuse in RLVR, significantly improving sample efficiency while maintaining performance.

title: CEPO: RLVR Self-Distillation using Contrastive Evidence Policy Optimization
summary: When a model produces a correct solution under reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), every token receives the same reward signal regardless of whether it was a decisive reasoning step or a grammatical filler. A natural fix is to condition the model on the correct answer as a teacher, identifying tokens it would have generated differently had it known the answer. Prior work shows this either corrupts training by leaking the answer into the gradient, or produces a weak signal that cannot distinguish decisive steps from filler, since both look equally surprising relative to the model's baseline. We propose Contrastive Evidence Policy Optimization (CEPO), which asks a sharper question at every token: not just "does the correct answer favor this token?" but "does the correct answer favor it while the wrong answer disfavors it?" A token satisfying both is a genuine reasoning step; one satisfying neither is filler. The wrong-answer teacher is constructed from rejected rollouts already in the training batch, incurring no additional sampling cost. We prove CEPO inherits all structural safety guarantees of the prior state of the art while strictly sharpening credit at decisive tokens, with the improvement vanishing exactly at filler positions. Empirically, CEPO achieves 43.43% and 60.56% average accuracy across five multimodal mathematical reasoning benchmarks at 2B and 4B scale, respectively, versus 41.17% and 57.43% for GRPO under identical training budgets. Distribution-matching self-distillation methods (OPSD, SDPO) fall below the untrained baseline, empirically confirming the information leakage our theory predicts. Our code is available at https://github.com/ahmedheakl/CEPO.

AI.tldr: CEPO is a reinforcement learning method that sharpens credit assignment by asking whether each token is favored by the correct answer while being disfavored by a wrong answer, distinguishing genuine reasoning steps from filler without information leakage.
AI.motivation: In RLVR, all tokens receive the same reward signal regardless of whether they contribute to reasoning or are just filler. Conditioning on correct answers can either leak answer information into gradients or produce weak signals that cannot distinguish decisive steps from filler.
AI.method: CEPO (Contrastive Evidence Policy Optimization) uses contrastive learning: for each token, it checks if the correct answer favors it while a wrong answer disfavors it. Genuine reasoning steps satisfy both conditions, while filler satisfies neither. Wrong-answer teachers are constructed from rejected rollouts already in the training batch without additional sampling cost.
AI.result: CEPO achieves 43.43% and 60.56% average accuracy across five multimodal mathematical reasoning benchmarks at 2B and 4B scale respectively, outperforming GRPO (41.17% and 57.43%). Self-distillation methods fall below untrained baselines, confirming information leakage predicted by theory.
AI.conclusion: CEPO provides theoretically sound and empirically effective credit assignment by distinguishing genuine reasoning steps from filler through contrastive evidence, achieving state-of-the-art performance while avoiding information leakage issues in baseline methods.

title: When the Majority Votes Wrong, the Intervention Timing for Test-Time Reinforcement Learning Hides in the Extinction Window
summary: Test-time reinforcement learning (TTRL) reports substantial accuracy gains on mathematical reasoning benchmarks using majority vote as a pseudo-label signal. We argue these gains are systematically misinterpreted: most reflect sharpening of already-solvable problems rather than genuine learning, while problems corrupted from correct to incorrect outnumber truly learned ones, and this damage is irreversible once majority vote locks onto a wrong answer. Per-problem tracking reveals that correct-answer signals in low-ability problems are briefly active before being permanently suppressed, a phenomenon we term the \textit{Correct-Answer Extinction Window}, with Flip Rate (FR) as its leading indicator. We thus propose \textbf{TTRL-Guard}, a lightweight framework with three mechanisms targeting the extinction window: Flip-Rate-Aware Reward Scaling (FRS) down-weights at-risk updates as FR declines, Minority-Preserving Sampling (MPS) retains gradient signal from minority correct answers, and Risk-Conditioned Sparse Updatings (RCSU) suspends updates on polarized problems. Experiments across three models and four benchmarks show that TTRL-Guard achieves the best average pass@1 on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Qwen3-4B, improves relatively over TTRL by +54\% on AIME 2025. \footnote{Our code and implementation details are available at https://github.com/linhxkkkk/TTRL-Guard.

AI.tldr: TTRL-Guard is a lightweight framework that prevents the irreversible damage caused by majority vote in Test-time Reinforcement Learning by addressing the "Correct-Answer Extinction Window" phenomenon through three protective mechanisms.
AI.motivation: The paper reveals that reported accuracy gains from Test-time Reinforcement Learning (TTRL) using majority vote are systematically misinterpreted - they mostly sharpen already-solvable problems rather than enabling genuine learning. More critically, problems corrupted from correct to incorrect outnumber truly learned ones, and this damage becomes irreversible once majority vote locks onto wrong answers. The authors identify a "Correct-Answer Extinction Window" phenomenon where correct-answer signals in low-ability problems are briefly active before being permanently suppressed.
AI.method: The authors propose TTRL-Guard, a lightweight framework with three protective mechanisms: 1) Flip-Rate-Aware Reward Scaling (FRS) that down-weights at-risk updates as Flip Rate declines, 2) Minority-Preserving Sampling (MPS) that retains gradient signal from minority correct answers, and 3) Risk-Conditioned Sparse Updatings (RCSU) that suspends updates on polarized problems.
AI.result: Experiments across three models and four benchmarks show that TTRL-Guard achieves the best average pass@1 on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Qwen3-4B, and improves relatively over standard TTRL by +54% on AIME 2025 benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The proposed TTRL-Guard framework successfully addresses the critical weakness of standard TTRL by preventing irreversible damage through protective mechanisms that target the Correct-Answer Extinction Window, leading to more robust and genuine learning improvements in mathematical reasoning tasks.

title: Implicit Bias of Mirror Flow in Homogeneous Neural Networks: Sparse and Dense Feature Learning
summary: We study the max-margin solutions reached by mirror flow in deep neural networks with homogeneous activation functions. Extending classical results on gradient flow, we derive a novel balance equation for mirror flow from convex duality, enabling a characterization of the horizon function governing the induced margin. We further establish max-margin characterizations together with convergence rates and norm growth estimates. Finally, we support our theory through experiments on synthetic datasets and standard vision tasks. Concretely, we show that: (1) distinct non-homogeneous mirror maps can induce the same max-margin solution; (2) convergence can be extremely slow, including exponentially slow regimes; and (3) although all considered mirror maps exhibit feature learning, they can produce markedly different representations, ranging from sparse to dense neuron activations. Together, these results provide a unified perspective on sparse and dense feature learning in homogeneous neural networks, highlighting how mirror maps shape both optimization dynamics and the geometry of the learned classifiers.

AI.tldr: Mirror flow in homogeneous neural networks leads to max-margin solutions, with mirror maps influencing both optimization dynamics and learned representations, ranging from sparse to dense feature learning.
AI.motivation: Extending classical gradient flow results to understand how mirror flow (generalized gradient flow) behaves in deep neural networks with homogeneous activations, particularly focusing on how different mirror maps shape optimization dynamics and learned feature representations.
AI.method: Derived a novel balance equation for mirror flow using convex duality, characterized the horizon function governing induced margin, established max-margin characterizations with convergence rates and norm growth estimates, and conducted experiments on synthetic datasets and standard vision tasks.
AI.result: (1) Different non-homogeneous mirror maps can induce identical max-margin solutions; (2) Convergence can be extremely slow (including exponentially slow regimes); (3) Mirror maps produce different representations ranging from sparse to dense neuron activations despite all exhibiting feature learning.
AI.conclusion: Mirror maps provide a unified perspective on sparse and dense feature learning in homogeneous neural networks, influencing both optimization dynamics and the geometry of learned classifiers, with practical implications for representation learning.

title: Quantifying the Pre-training Dividend: Generative versus Latent Self-Supervised Learning for Time Series Foundation Models
summary: The success of self-supervised learning (SSL) in vision and NLP has motivated its rapid adoption for time series. However, research has focused primarily on Generative paradigms and forecasting tasks, leaving the broader utility of learned representations unquantified. We establish a controlled framework to evaluate the "pre-training dividend": the value added by SSL across diverse temporal tasks. We systematically compare Generative paradigms against Latent Alignment architectures, introducing adaptations of LeJEPA and DINO for time series. These adaptations utilize Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) augmentations to enforce invariance to local fluctuations. Our analysis reveals that the pre-training dividend is highly asymmetric: SSL yields gains of up to 375% for anomaly detection and classification, yet remains marginal for forecasting. We demonstrate that representational utility is non-universal, governed by a precision-invariance trade-off where the specific signal resolution required by the task must align with the objective. Finally, we show that representation quality is largely independent of data origin and saturates at moderate architectural depths, suggesting a path to scaling via massive synthetic generation. Our code is available at: https://github.com/noammajor/Models

AI.tldr: SSL for time series shows asymmetric benefits: huge gains (up to 375%) for anomaly detection and classification, but minimal for forecasting, governed by a precision-invariance trade-off where task-specific signal resolution must align with the SSL objective.
AI.motivation: While SSL has seen success in vision and NLP, its adoption for time series has focused mainly on generative paradigms and forecasting tasks, leaving the broader utility of learned representations unquantified across diverse temporal tasks.
AI.method: Established a controlled framework to evaluate the "pre-training dividend" of SSL for time series. Systematically compared Generative paradigms against Latent Alignment architectures (adapted LeJEPA and DINO for time series), using Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) augmentations to enforce invariance to local fluctuations.
AI.result: The pre-training dividend is highly asymmetric: SSL yields gains up to 375% for anomaly detection and classification, but remains marginal for forecasting. Representational utility is non-universal, governed by a precision-invariance trade-off where task-specific signal resolution must align with SSL objectives. Representation quality is largely independent of data origin and saturates at moderate architectural depths.
AI.conclusion: SSL for time series offers substantial but task-specific benefits, with representational quality determined by the alignment between task requirements and SSL objectives. The findings suggest scaling via massive synthetic generation as a promising path forward, given that representation quality saturates at moderate depths and is independent of data origin.

title: Sampling-Based Safe Reinforcement Learning
summary: Safe exploration remains a fundamental challenge in reinforcement learning (RL), limiting the deployment of RL agents in the real world. We propose Sampling-Based Safe Reinforcement Learning (SBSRL), a model-based RL algorithm that maintains safety throughout the learning process by enforcing constraints jointly across a finite set of dynamics samples. This formulation approximates an intractable worst-case optimization over uncertain dynamics and enables practical safety guarantees in continuous domains. We further introduce an exploration strategy based on constraining epistemic uncertainty, eliminating the need for explicit exploration bonuses. Under regularity conditions, we derive high-probability guarantees of safety throughout learning and a finite-time sample complexity bound for recovering a near-optimal policy. Empirically, SBSRL achieves safe and efficient exploration both in simulation and in real robotic hardware, and readily extends to practical deep-ensemble implementations that scale to high-dimensional continuous control problems.

AI.tldr: SBSRL is a model-based RL algorithm that ensures safety during learning by enforcing constraints across dynamics samples, providing practical safety guarantees and efficient exploration without explicit exploration bonuses.
AI.motivation: Safe exploration is a fundamental challenge in RL that limits real-world deployment of RL agents. Current approaches struggle with maintaining safety guarantees during the learning process, especially in continuous domains with uncertain dynamics.
AI.method: Proposes Sampling-Based Safe Reinforcement Learning (SBSRL) - a model-based RL algorithm that enforces safety constraints jointly across a finite set of dynamics samples. This approximates intractable worst-case optimization. Uses an exploration strategy based on constraining epistemic uncertainty (eliminating need for explicit exploration bonuses). Extends to practical deep-ensemble implementations for scalability.
AI.result: Derives high-probability safety guarantees throughout learning and finite-time sample complexity bound for near-optimal policy recovery. Empirically achieves safe and efficient exploration in simulation and real robotic hardware. Scales to high-dimensional continuous control problems with deep-ensemble implementations.
AI.conclusion: SBSRL provides a practical approach to safe reinforcement learning with formal guarantees, enabling safer deployment of RL agents in real-world applications. The method successfully balances safety requirements with efficient exploration and scales to complex continuous control domains.

title: Adynamical systems view of training generativemodels and the memorization phenomenon
summary: Using recent works of one of the authors (VSB) on collapse in generative models and two time scale dynamics in stochastic gradient descent in high dimensions, we give a system theoretic explanation of the memorization phenomenon in generative models. This relies purely on the dynamic aspects of the training phase. Specifically, we use a result of Austin [2016] to motivate a stylized model for the loss function for stochastic gradient descent (SGD) wherein the loss function has a strong dependence on some variables and weak dependence on the rest in a precise sense. This naturally leads to two distinct time scales in the constant step size SGD that is commonly used in machine learning. This fact has been used to explain the double descent phenomenon in SGD in Borkar [2026]. In conjunction with a mathematical model for collapse phenomenon in SGD developed in Borkar [2025a], we analyze the constant step size SGD using the recent results of Azizian et al. [2024] in order to explain the phenomenon of memorization wherein a generative model that is concurrently being tuned yields the same or similar outputs for significant stretches of time. This gives a novel perspective on the aforementioned phenomena reported in machine learning literature and their interrelationships, using a dynamical systems viewpoint.

AI.tldr: The paper provides a dynamical systems explanation for memorization in generative models by analyzing constant step-size SGD dynamics with two distinct time scales, connecting collapse phenomena with double descent behavior.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to explain the memorization phenomenon in generative models from a system-theoretic perspective, focusing on the dynamic aspects of training rather than static properties. They seek to connect this with related phenomena like collapse and double descent in machine learning literature.
AI.method: They use a stylized model for SGD loss functions with strong dependence on some variables and weak dependence on others, leading to two distinct time scales. They combine Austin's result on loss functions, Borkar's work on collapse in SGD and double descent, and Azizian et al.'s results to analyze constant step-size SGD dynamics.
AI.result: The analysis explains why generative models produce similar outputs for significant time periods during training (memorization phenomenon), showing that this emerges naturally from the two-time-scale dynamics of constant step-size SGD.
AI.conclusion: Memorization in generative models can be explained through a dynamical systems viewpoint analyzing SGD training dynamics, revealing interrelationships between collapse phenomena, double descent, and memorization as manifestations of underlying multi-time-scale stochastic gradient dynamics.

title: The Silent Hyperparameter: Quantifying the Impact of Inference Backends on LLM Reproducibility
summary: Progress in LLMs is increasingly measured through standardized benchmarks, where state-of-the-art improvements are often separated by fractions of a percentage point. At the same time, the computational cost of evaluating modern LLMs has driven widespread adoption of specialized inference backends, software systems that execute trained models efficiently at inference time. While critical for scalability, system-level optimizations, such as custom CUDA kernels and reduced-precision arithmetic, can alter token probabilities and introduce non-determinism, possibly cascading into divergent generation. In this work, we first survey the inference landscape, identifying 200 distinct engines, and analyze 35,000 ML publications, finding that the specific inference stack is rarely reported despite this widespread diversity. We then present a systematic empirical study of how inference backends affect LLM benchmark results. Holding model weights, decoding parameters, and hardware constant, we evaluate five widely used inference engines, including vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp, across multiple open-weight models and established benchmarks. We show that the choice of backend alone can shift benchmark scores by up to 16.6 percentage points and induce high rates of output disagreement. By isolating backend optimizations and tracing the execution pipeline, we find this divergence is driven by system-level optimizations like prefix caching and CUDA graphs, custom kernels, and engine-specific defaults in logit processing. Our findings identify the inference backend as a previously unreported but consequential hyperparameter in the evaluation of LLM and advocate standardized reporting of inference stacks to improve the reproducibility and interpretability of benchmark comparisons.

AI.tldr: Different inference backends (specialized systems for efficient LLM execution) can significantly affect benchmark scores by up to 16.6 percentage points, creating reproducibility issues in LLM evaluation despite rarely being reported.
AI.motivation: LLM progress is measured through standardized benchmarks with tiny improvements, but computational costs have driven adoption of diverse inference backends with system-level optimizations that can alter token probabilities and introduce non-determinism, potentially affecting benchmark results.
AI.method: 1) Surveyed inference landscape (200 engines) and analyzed 35,000 ML publications to understand reporting practices; 2) Conducted empirical study holding model weights, decoding parameters, and hardware constant while evaluating five widely used inference engines (including vLLM, SGLang, llama.cpp) across multiple open-weight models and established benchmarks; 3) Isolated backend optimizations and traced execution pipeline to identify causes of divergence.
AI.result: 1) Inference stack is rarely reported despite widespread diversity; 2) Choice of backend alone can shift benchmark scores by up to 16.6 percentage points; 3) High rates of output disagreement observed; 4) Divergence driven by system-level optimizations like prefix caching, CUDA graphs, custom kernels, and engine-specific defaults in logit processing.
AI.conclusion: The inference backend is a previously unreported but consequential hyperparameter in LLM evaluation. Standardized reporting of inference stacks is needed to improve reproducibility and interpretability of benchmark comparisons.

title: TORQ: Two-Level Orthogonal Rotation for MXFP4 Quantization
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) advance toward practical deployment, the Microscaling FP4 (MXFP4) format has emerged as a cornerstone for next-generation low-bit inference, owing to its ability to balance high dynamic range with hardware efficiency. However, directly applying MXFP4 to LLM activation quantization inevitably leads to significant accuracy degradation. In this paper, we theoretically analyze the error structure of MXFP4 activation quantization, revealing that the root cause of this performance drop lies in two structural imbalances between activation distributions and the MXFP4 block floating-point format: (1) extreme inter-block variance imbalance and (2) intra-block codebook utilization imbalance. To address these challenges, we propose TORQ (Two-level Orthogonal Rotation for MXFP4 Quantization), a training-free Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) framework designed to reshape the geometric properties of the activation space through optimal coordinate transformations. At the macroscopic level, TORQ leverages the Schur-Horn theorem to redistribute activation energy via inter-block orthogonal rotation, preventing high-variance blocks from driving up shared scaling factors and thereby preserving the precision of small-magnitude elements. At the microscopic level, TORQ employs maximum-entropy-guided intra-block rotation to alleviate codebook collapse and maximize the MXFP4 codebook's information capacity. Experiments on mainstream LLMs such as LLaMA3 and Qwen3 show that TORQ significantly improves the accuracy of MXFP4 activation quantization compared to existing methods: on Qwen3-32B, the perplexity on WikiText is reduced to 8.43 (vs. 7.61 for BF16), and the average accuracy increases from 38.40% with direct RTN to 73.63% (vs. 74.82% for BF16), substantially narrowing the gap between 4-bit floating-point quantization and full-precision inference.

AI.tldr: TORQ is a training-free PTQ framework that optimizes MXFP4 activation quantization for LLMs through two-level orthogonal rotations to address structural imbalances between activation distributions and the MXFP4 format.
AI.motivation: MXFP4 format is promising for low-bit LLM inference but causes significant accuracy degradation when applied to activation quantization due to two structural imbalances: extreme inter-block variance imbalance and intra-block codebook utilization imbalance.
AI.method: TORQ uses two-level orthogonal rotation: (1) macroscopic inter-block orthogonal rotation based on Schur-Horn theorem to redistribute activation energy and prevent high-variance blocks from dominating scaling factors, and (2) microscopic intra-block rotation guided by maximum entropy to alleviate codebook collapse and maximize MXFP4 codebook capacity.
AI.result: On Qwen3-32B, TORQ reduces WikiText perplexity to 8.43 (vs. 7.61 for BF16) and increases average accuracy from 38.40% with direct RTN to 73.63% (vs. 74.82% for BF16), significantly narrowing the gap between 4-bit and full-precision inference.
AI.conclusion: TORQ effectively addresses the structural imbalances in MXFP4 activation quantization through training-free orthogonal transformations, enabling high-performance 4-bit floating-point quantization for LLM inference with minimal accuracy loss.

title: Online Market Making and the Value of Observing the Order Book
summary:
We study an online market-making problem in which a learner sequentially posts bid and ask prices for a single asset while interacting with traders holding private valuations. Unlike existing online learning formulations that assume fully censored feedback, we introduce an action-dependent feedback model inspired by real limit order books: when a trade occurs, the trader's valuation remains hidden, whereas when no trade occurs, informative feedback about supply and demand is revealed.
  We show that this additional information fundamentally changes the learnability of the problem. In the stochastic setting with i.i.d. market prices, we propose an elimination-based algorithm that achieves $O(\sqrt T)$ regret with high probability, without requiring any smoothness assumptions on the distribution of trader valuations. We then extend this result to a broad class of mean-reverting price processes by considering both local, autoregressive dynamics and a weaker global drift condition based on cumulative deviations from the mean. Under either assumption, we establish high-probability $O(\sqrt T)$ regret bounds, relying on a new concentration inequality of independent interest. Finally, in the adversarial setting with oblivious prices, we design an explore-then-perturb algorithm that guarantees $O(T^{2/3})$ regret in expectation.
  Our results quantify the value of observing the order book in online market making and demonstrate that even limited, action-dependent feedback can substantially improve regret guarantees compared to standard bandit feedback models.

AI.tldr: Online market-making algorithm with action-dependent feedback from limit order books achieves improved regret bounds compared to standard bandit feedback models.
AI.motivation: Existing online learning formulations assume fully censored feedback, but real limit order books provide action-dependent feedback: when trades occur valuations are hidden, but when no trade occurs, informative supply/demand feedback is revealed. This additional information could fundamentally change learnability.
AI.method: Proposed elimination-based algorithm for stochastic setting with i.i.d. market prices, extended to mean-reverting price processes (both local autoregressive dynamics and global drift conditions). For adversarial setting with oblivious prices, designed explore-then-perturb algorithm.
AI.result: In stochastic setting: O(√T) regret with high probability without requiring smoothness assumptions. For mean-reverting processes: O(√T) regret bounds using new concentration inequality. In adversarial setting: O(T^{2/3}) regret in expectation.
AI.conclusion: Action-dependent feedback from observing order books in online market making substantially improves regret guarantees compared to standard bandit feedback models, quantifying the value of this additional information.

title: Physics-Informed Graph Neural Network Surrogates for Turbulent Nanoparticle Dispersion in Dental Clinical Environments
summary: Dental aerosol procedures produce sub-50 micrometre nuclei that can remain airborne for long periods in enclosed clinics, creating pathways for airborne pathogen transmission. Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) simulations with Euler-Lagrange particle tracking capture this transport accurately but require very long run times per scenario, which precludes real-time clinical decision support in 3D. We present the Eulerian-Lagrangian Graph Interaction Network (ELGIN), a physics-informed graph surrogate that jointly predicts carrier-flow dynamics on the OpenFOAM polyhedral mesh and the per-parcel motion of the polydisperse spray cloud. ELGIN couples a multi-head Graph Transformer with Jacobi-preconditioned learnable pressure projection and a turbulence-closure head to a sigmoid-gated Lagrangian Interaction Network through differentiable inverse-distance mesh-parcel coupling, and advances parcels with a symplectic Stormer-Verlet integrator. A four-stage physics-informed curriculum stabilises 260-step autoregressive rollouts without gradient explosion. A parameter sweep with foam-extend 4.1 OpenFOAM reactingParcelFoam across clinically relevant ventilation rates and handpiece spray speeds provides CFD ground truth. This article reports a single-case demonstration in which both ELGIN and a Lagrangian-only baseline (M0) are trained and evaluated on Sweep_Case_03 of a twenty-case sweep; full 16/2/2 retraining is in progress and will replace all reported metrics. On this case, ELGIN tracks the foam-extend particle cloud much more closely than M0: mean parcel displacement error falls from 19.56% to 16.20% of room width and cloud radius-of-gyration error from 9.85% to 6.58%. A 26-second rollout completes in ~64 s on a 4 GB GPU, approximately 37x faster than the foam-extend reference pipeline, toward per-appointment infection-risk screening once the multi-case checkpoint is in place.

AI.tldr: ELGIN is a physics-informed graph neural network that predicts dental aerosol transport 37x faster than CFD simulations, enabling real-time infection-risk screening in clinical settings.
AI.motivation: Traditional RANS simulations for dental aerosol transport are computationally expensive, preventing real-time clinical decision support. There's a need for faster, accurate predictions of airborne pathogen transmission pathways in dental clinics.
AI.method: Developed ELGIN, a physics-informed graph surrogate combining multi-head Graph Transformer with Jacobi-preconditioned learnable pressure projection, turbulence-closure head, and sigmoid-gated Lagrangian Interaction Network. Uses differentiable inverse-distance mesh-parcel coupling and symplectic Stormer-Verlet integrator with four-stage curriculum learning.
AI.result: ELGIN reduces mean parcel displacement error from 19.56% to 16.20% of room width and cloud radius-of-gyration error from 9.85% to 6.58% compared to baseline. Achieves 37x speedup, completing 26-second rollout in ~64 seconds on 4GB GPU versus CFD reference pipeline.
AI.conclusion: ELGIN demonstrates potential for real-time infection-risk screening in dental clinics by providing fast, accurate predictions of aerosol transport, addressing computational bottlenecks of traditional CFD methods.

title: A Family of Divergence Measures for Evaluating the Reconstruction Quality of Explainable Ensemble Trees
summary: Validating interpretable surrogate models for ensemble learners requires measuring agreement between the ensemble's internal representation and its surrogate approximation, rather than mere association. Correlation-based approaches are scale-invariant and fail to detect systematic discrepancies in co-occurrence structure. We propose a statistical framework grounded in the agreement-association distinction, centered on the normalized Loss of Interpretability (nLoI). Rooted in the Cressie-Read power divergence family with lambda equal to 2, the nLoI admits a closed-form decomposition into within-node and between-node components, providing a unique diagnostic capability to identify precisely where and why reconstruction fails. The framework incorporates four complementary measures capturing distinct structural facets of approximation quality. A unified permutation testing procedure delivers valid inference for all measures within a single resampling pass. Theoretical properties, including boundedness and symmetry, are established for each metric. Monte Carlo simulations and empirical evaluations confirm exact Type I error control and demonstrate that these measures detect reconstruction fidelity gradients invisible to correlation-based alternatives. The framework is developed and illustrated in the context of Explainable Ensemble Trees (E2Tree), and empirical evaluation on three benchmark datasets illustrates the practical utility of the framework.

AI.tldr: A framework for validating interpretable surrogate models using agreement (not association) metrics like normalized Loss of Interpretability (nLoI) that decomposes to identify where reconstruction fails, with exact statistical inference via permutation testing.
AI.motivation: Current correlation-based validation of surrogate models for ensemble learners only measures association, not agreement, failing to detect systematic discrepancies in co-occurrence structure between the ensemble's internal representation and its surrogate approximation.
AI.method: Proposes a statistical framework centered on normalized Loss of Interpretability (nLoI) based on Cressie-Read power divergence (lambda=2), which decomposes into within-node and between-node components. Includes four complementary measures capturing distinct structural facets, with unified permutation testing for valid inference.
AI.result: Theoretical properties (boundedness, symmetry) established for each metric. Monte Carlo simulations show exact Type I error control and demonstrate detection of reconstruction fidelity gradients invisible to correlation-based methods. Empirical evaluation on three benchmark datasets confirms practical utility.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a rigorous approach to validate interpretable surrogate models by measuring agreement rather than mere association, with diagnostic capability to identify specific reconstruction failures, particularly useful for Explainable Ensemble Trees (E2Tree).

title: MiMuon: Mixed Muon Optimizer with Improved Generalization for Large Models
summary: Matrix-structured parameters frequently appear in many artificial intelligence models such as large language models. More recently, an efficient Muon optimizer is designed for matrix parameters of large-scale models, and shows markedly faster convergence than the vector-wise algorithms. Although some works have begun to study convergence properties (i.e., optimization error) of the Muon optimizer, its generalization properties (i.e., generalization error) is still not established. Thus, in this paper, we study generalization error of the Muon optimizer based on algorithmic stability and mathematical induction, and prove that the Muon has a generalization error of $O\big(\frac{1}{Nκ^{T}}\big)$, where $N$ is training sample size, and $T$ denotes iteration number, and $κ>0$ denotes minimum difference between singular values of gradient estimate. To enhance generalization of the Muon, we propose an effective mixed Muon (MiMuon) optimizer by cautiously using orthogonalization of gradient, which is a hybrid of Muon and momentum-based SGD optimizers. Then we prove that our MiMuon optimizer has a lower generalization error of $O\big(\frac{1}{N}\big)$ than $O\big(\frac{1}{Nκ^{T}}\big)$ of Muon optimizer, since $κ$ generally is very small. Meanwhile, we also studied the convergence properties of our MiMuon algorithm, and prove that our MiMuon algorithm has the same convergence rate of $O(\frac{1}{T^{1/4}})$ as the Muon algorithm. Some numerical experimental results on training large models including Qwen3-0.6B and YOLO26m demonstrate efficiency of the MiMuon optimizer.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes generalization error of Muon optimizer for matrix parameters, proposes MiMuon with better generalization, and proves both convergence and generalization bounds.
AI.motivation: Muon optimizer shows fast convergence for matrix parameters in large-scale AI models, but its generalization properties (generalization error) are not established. The paper aims to study generalization error of Muon and propose an enhanced version with better generalization.
AI.method: 1. Analyze generalization error of Muon optimizer using algorithmic stability and mathematical induction. 2. Propose Mixed Muon (MiMuon) optimizer that combines orthogonalization of gradient with momentum-based SGD. 3. Prove generalization and convergence bounds for both Muon and MiMuon.
AI.result: 1. Muon optimizer has generalization error of O(1/(Nκ^T)), where N is sample size, T is iterations, κ>0 is minimum difference between singular values of gradient estimate. 2. MiMuon optimizer has lower generalization error of O(1/N) than Muon. 3. MiMuon maintains same convergence rate O(1/T^{1/4}) as Muon. 4. Experiments on Qwen3-0.6B and YOLO26m show efficiency of MiMuon.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes generalization error bounds for Muon optimizer and proposes MiMuon with improved generalization while maintaining convergence rate. MiMuon effectively combines orthogonal gradient techniques with momentum-based optimization for better performance on large models.

title: Optimal Reconstruction from Linear Queries
summary:
We study the problem of reconstructing an unknown point in $\mathbb{R}^d$ from approximate linear queries. This setting arises naturally in applications ranging from low-dimensional remote sensing and signal recovery to high-dimensional data analysis and privacy-sensitive inference. Our main goal is to characterize the optimal reconstruction error as a function of the number of queries $T$, the ambient dimension $d$, and the noise parameter $δ$.
  We first analyze the limit $T \to \infty$ and show that the optimal reconstruction error converges to the explicit value $\sqrt{2d/(d+1)} δ$, which plays a role analogous to the Bayes optimal error in supervised learning. When the dimension is fixed, we show that the excess error above this limit decays doubly exponentially fast as $T \to \infty$, a rate that is significantly faster than those typically encountered in learning curves. When the dimension grows, we show that a number of queries on the order of $\exp(d)$ is necessary and sufficient to achieve vanishing excess error. Finally, we introduce and analyze an improper variant of the reconstruction problem.
  From a technical perspective, our main contribution is a generalization of Jung's theorem (1901). The classical theorem bounds the maximum possible radius of a set of diameter 1 and characterizes extremal bodies. Our generalization provides a robust variant that characterizes near-extremal bodies and is proved via geometric and dynamical arguments exploiting symmetry and Lie group actions.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes optimal reconstruction error bounds for unknown points in ℝ^d from approximate linear queries, establishing precise convergence rates and query complexity, with key technical contributions generalizing Jung's theorem.
AI.motivation: The problem arises naturally in applications like low-dimensional remote sensing, signal recovery, high-dimensional data analysis, and privacy-sensitive inference, where reconstructing an unknown point from approximate linear queries is fundamental.
AI.method: The authors analyze reconstruction error as a function of queries T, dimension d, and noise δ, studying different regimes: T→∞ limit, fixed dimension with T→∞, and growing dimension. The key technical approach involves a generalization of Jung's theorem (1901) using geometric and dynamical arguments exploiting symmetry and Lie group actions.
AI.result: 1) For T→∞, optimal reconstruction error converges to √[2d/(d+1)]δ. 2) Fixed dimension: excess error decays doubly exponentially fast in T. 3) Growing dimension: Θ(exp(d)) queries are necessary and sufficient for vanishing excess error. 4) Introduction and analysis of improper variant of reconstruction problem.
AI.conclusion: The work provides precise characterizations of optimal reconstruction error in various regimes, revealing surprisingly fast convergence rates and establishing fundamental query complexity results, with main technical innovation being a robust generalization of Jung's theorem via geometric methods.

title: AR1-ZO: Topology-Aware Rank-1 Zeroth-Order Queries for High-Rank LoRA Fine-Tuning
summary: Zeroth-order (ZO) optimization enables large-language-model fine-tuning without storing backpropagation activations, while LoRA supplies compact trainable adapters. Combining them creates a rank paradox: increasing LoRA rank improves adapter capacity, but standard two-point ZO either perturbs a rank-dependent number of coordinates or, under atomwise updates, can make the finite-difference signal unobservable. This paper shows that the bottleneck is a measurement-topology problem rather than a need for an external subspace. LoRA already decomposes into matched rank-$1$ atoms, each a complete factor-coordinate block of dimension $d_\text{out}+d_\text{in}$. Querying one atom per step keeps the stored adapter rank $r$ while removing $r$ from the single-query perturbation dimension. The naive atomwise query is still miscalibrated: if it inherits canonical LoRA scaling $α/r$, the active finite-difference signal shrinks as $1/r$ and the active finite-difference signal-to-noise ratio (FD-SNR) as $1/r^2$, producing directional collapse under a fixed residual evaluation-noise floor. AR1-ZO pairs alternating rank-$1$ atom queries with topology-aware scaling $γ=αr$, restoring rank-invariant active signal without auxiliary bases, activation hooks, curvature estimates, or extra forward queries. Theory proves atom minimality, rank-independent active query dimension, directional collapse and restoration, and the remaining rank dependence as an amortized coverage cost. Experiments on OPT and Qwen3 models validate the signal mechanism and show that AR1-ZO makes high-rank LoRA effective among matched-budget ZO methods under the standard two-forward-pass query budget.

AI.tldr: AR1-ZO: A zeroth-order optimization method for high-rank LoRA fine-tuning that uses alternating rank-1 atom queries with topology-aware scaling to maintain rank-invariant active signal without directional collapse.
AI.motivation: Combining zeroth-order optimization with LoRA creates a rank paradox: increasing LoRA rank improves capacity but causes problems for standard two-point ZO - either perturbs too many coordinates or makes finite-difference signals unobservable under atomwise updates. The bottleneck is identified as a measurement-topology problem rather than needing external subspace.
AI.method: AR1-ZO pairs alternating rank-1 atom queries with topology-aware scaling γ=αr. LoRA already decomposes into matched rank-1 atoms, each being a complete factor-coordinate block. The method queries one atom per step, keeping adapter rank r while removing r from the single-query perturbation dimension. Uses standard two-forward-pass query budget without auxiliary bases, activation hooks, curvature estimates, or extra forward queries.
AI.result: Theory proves atom minimality, rank-independent active query dimension, directional collapse and restoration mechanisms, and shows remaining rank dependence as amortized coverage cost. Experiments on OPT and Qwen3 models validate the signal mechanism and demonstrate that AR1-ZO makes high-rank LoRA effective among matched-budget ZO methods.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents AR1-ZO as a solution to the LoRA rank paradox in ZO optimization, showing that careful measurement topology and scaling can enable effective high-rank LoRA fine-tuning without additional computational overhead or external mechanisms.

title: OScaR: The Occam's Razor for Extreme KV Cache Quantization in LLMs and Beyond
summary: The rapid advancement toward long-context reasoning and multi-modal intelligence has made the memory footprint of the Key-Value (KV) cache a dominant memory bottleneck for efficient deployment. While the established per-channel quantization effectively accommodates intrinsic channel-wise outliers in Key tensors, its efficacy diminishes under extreme compression. In this work, we revisit the inherent limitations of the per-channel quantization paradigm from both empirical and theoretical perspectives. Our analysis identifies Token Norm Imbalance (TNI) as the primary bottleneck to quantization fidelity. We demonstrate that TNI systematically amplifies errors when shared quantization parameters are required to span token groups exhibiting substantial norm disparities. Instead of relying on intricate quantization pipelines (e.g., TurboQuant), we propose OScaR (Omni-Scaled Canalized Rotation), an accurate and lightweight KV cache compression framework for X-LLMs (i.e., text-only, multi-modal, and omni-modal LLMs). Advancing the per-channel paradigm, OScaR employs Canalized Rotation followed by Omni-Token Scaling to mitigate TNI-induced sequence-dimensional variance both effectively and efficiently, further supported by our optimized system design and CUDA kernels. Extensive evaluations across X-LLMs show that OScaR consistently outperforms existing methods and achieves near-lossless performance under INT2 quantization, establishing it as a robust, low-complexity, and universal framework that defines a new Pareto front. Compared with the BF16 FlashDecoding-v2 baseline, our OScaR implementation achieves a notable up to 3.0x speedup in decoding, reduces memory footprint by 5.3x, and increases throughput by 4.1x. The code for OScaR is publicly available at https://github.com/ZunhaiSu/OScaR-KV-Quant.

AI.tldr: OScaR is a lightweight KV cache compression framework using canalized rotation and token scaling to achieve near-lossless INT2 quantization, reducing memory footprint by 5.3x while increasing decoding speed by 3.0x.
AI.motivation: The memory footprint of Key-Value cache has become a dominant bottleneck for efficient deployment of long-context reasoning and multi-modal LLMs, with existing per-channel quantization methods losing effectiveness under extreme compression due to Token Norm Imbalance issues.
AI.method: OScaR advances per-channel quantization with Canalized Rotation followed by Omni-Token Scaling to mitigate Token Norm Imbalance, reducing sequence-dimensional variance effectively, supported by optimized system design and CUDA kernels.
AI.result: OScaR outperforms existing methods, achieves near-lossless performance under INT2 quantization, reduces memory footprint by 5.3x, achieves up to 3.0x speedup in decoding, and increases throughput by 4.1x compared to BF16 FlashDecoding-v2 baseline.
AI.conclusion: OScaR establishes a robust, low-complexity, universal framework that defines a new Pareto front for KV cache compression across text-only, multi-modal, and omni-modal LLMs.

title: Latent Laplace Diffusion for Irregular Multivariate Time Series
comment: Camera-ready Spotlight paper at ICML 2026. 27 pages, 5 figures. Code: https://github.com/pixelhero98/LLapDiffusion
summary: Irregular multivariate time series impose a trade-off for long-horizon forecasting: discrete methods can distort temporal structure via re-gridding, while continuous-time models often require sequential solvers prone to drift. To bridge this gap, we present Latent Laplace Diffusion (LLapDiff), a generative framework that models the target as a low-dimensional latent trajectory, enabling horizon-wide generation without step-by-step integration over physical time. We guide the reverse process utilizing a stable modal parameterization motivated by stochastic port-Hamiltonian dynamics, and parameterize its mean evolution in the Laplace domain via learnable complex-conjugate poles, enabling direct evaluation over irregular timestamps. We also link continuous dynamics to irregular observations through renewal-averaging analysis, which maps sampling gaps to effective event-domain poles and motivates a gap-aware history summarizer. Extensive experiments show that LLapDiff improves over baselines in long-horizon forecasting, and its continuous-time generative nature supports missing-value imputation by querying the same model at historical timestamps. Code is available at https://github.com/pixelhero98/LLapDiffusion.

AI.tldr: LLapDiff is a continuous-time generative framework for irregular multivariate time series forecasting that models targets as low-dimensional latent trajectories, enabling direct evaluation over irregular timestamps without step-by-step integration.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for irregular multivariate time series forecasting face a trade-off: discrete methods can distort temporal structure through re-gridding, while continuous-time models require sequential solvers prone to drift, making long-horizon forecasting challenging.
AI.method: Uses a stable modal parameterization motivated by stochastic port-Hamiltonian dynamics, parameterizes mean evolution in the Laplace domain via learnable complex-conjugate poles, implements renewal-averaging analysis to map sampling gaps to effective event-domain poles, and includes a gap-aware history summarizer.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show LLapDiff improves over baselines in long-horizon forecasting and supports missing-value imputation by querying the same model at historical timestamps.
AI.conclusion: LLapDiff bridges the gap between discrete and continuous-time methods for irregular multivariate time series, providing an efficient continuous-time generative framework that avoids step-by-step integration while handling irregular timestamps effectively.

title: Agentic Discovery of Cryomicroneedle Formulations
summary: Cryomicroneedles offer a route to minimally invasive intradermal delivery of living cells, but their cryogenic formulations must reconcile cell protection with constraints on toxicity and device fabrication. Here we report an AI-assisted, closed-loop workflow for cryomicroneedle cryoprotectant discovery that combines literature curation, Gaussian-process surrogate modelling, Bayesian optimization, and sequential wet-lab validation. A curated dataset of 198 mesenchymal stem-cell cryopreservation formulations from 42 studies was converted into 21 ingredient features and used to train an uncertainty-aware literature prior. This model captured moderate structure in the literature data but failed prospectively, motivating iterative wet-lab correction. Across ten validation iterations and 106 wet-lab observations, the model progressively adapted to cryomicroneedle-specific outcomes: batch RMSE decreased from 41.21 to 6.86 percentage points, later-stage rank correlations became consistently positive, and the cumulative wet-lab predicted-versus-measured summary reached $R^2 = 0.942$. The best validated formulation achieved 95.15\% post-thaw viability with low DMSO, ectoin, ethylene glycol, and fetal bovine serum. However, high viability alone did not ensure intact cryomicroneedle formation, highlighting the need for future multi-objective optimization. These results demonstrate that agent-assisted computational infrastructure can make data-efficient formulation discovery more accessible to labs with minimal data expertise in-house. Project code is available at https://github.com/baitmeister/ML-for-CryoMN.

AI.tldr: AI-assisted closed-loop workflow discovers cryoprotectant formulations for cryomicroneedles, achieving 95.15% cell viability with minimal toxic components through iterative wet-lab validation.
AI.motivation: Cryomicroneedles need cryoprotectant formulations that protect living cells during intradermal delivery while meeting constraints of toxicity, cell viability, and device fabrication. Existing literature data is insufficient for cryomicroneedle-specific applications.
AI.method: Combines literature curation (198 formulations from 42 studies), Gaussian-process surrogate modeling, Bayesian optimization, and sequential wet-lab validation in a closed-loop AI-assisted workflow. Converts formulations into 21 ingredient features and uses uncertainty-aware modeling with iterative wet-lab correction.
AI.result: Achieved 95.15% post-thaw viability with low DMSO, ectoin, ethylene glycol, and fetal bovine serum. Model performance improved significantly: batch RMSE decreased from 41.21 to 6.86 percentage points, and cumulative R² reached 0.942 across 106 wet-lab observations.
AI.conclusion: AI-assisted computational infrastructure enables data-efficient formulation discovery for labs with limited data expertise. High cell viability alone doesn't guarantee intact cryomicroneedle formation, indicating need for future multi-objective optimization.

title: FLUXtrapolation: A benchmark on extrapolating ecosystem fluxes
summary: We introduce FLUXtrapolation, a benchmark for extrapolating ecosystem fluxes under progressively harder distribution shifts. Ecosystem fluxes are central to understanding the carbon, water, and energy cycles, yet they can only be measured directly at sparsely located measurement towers. Producing global flux estimates therefore requires training models on observed sites using globally available covariates and predicting in unobserved regions, that is, upscaling. Flux upscaling is a challenging domain generalization problem that is affected by a shift in covariate distribution across climates, ecosystem types, and environmental conditions, as well as by conditional shift: important drivers remain unobserved at global scale. We provide a quantitative analysis of both these shifts in $P_X$ and $P_{Y\mid X}$. FLUXtrapolation is designed based on domain expertise on flux upscaling: it defines temporal, spatial, and temperature-based extrapolation scenarios and evaluates performance across held-out domains, temporal aggregations, and tail errors. In a pilot study, we find that baselines perform similarly under median hourly RMSE, but separate under the proposed tail-focused and multi-scale evaluation. FLUXtrapolation therefore poses a realistic and thus relevant challenge for machine learning methods under distribution shift; at the same time, progress on this benchmark would directly support the scientific goal of improving flux upscaling.

AI.tldr: FLUXtrapolation is a benchmark for evaluating machine learning models on ecosystem flux extrapolation under distribution shifts, designed with domain expertise to test temporal, spatial, and temperature-based extrapolation scenarios.
AI.motivation: Ecosystem fluxes are crucial for understanding carbon, water, and energy cycles but are only measured at sparse locations. Producing global estimates requires upscaling models trained on observed sites to unobserved regions, which is a challenging domain generalization problem due to covariate distribution shifts across climates, ecosystems, and environmental conditions, as well as conditional shift from unobserved drivers.
AI.method: FLUXtrapolation provides a quantitative analysis of both covariate distribution shift (P_X) and conditional shift (P_{Y|X}). The benchmark is designed based on domain expertise for flux upscaling, defining temporal, spatial, and temperature-based extrapolation scenarios. It evaluates performance across held-out domains, temporal aggregations, and tail errors.
AI.result: In a pilot study, baseline models perform similarly under median hourly RMSE but separate when evaluated under the proposed tail-focused and multi-scale evaluation metrics. The benchmark reveals differences that standard metrics don't capture.
AI.conclusion: FLUXtrapolation presents a realistic and relevant challenge for machine learning methods under distribution shift, and progress on this benchmark would directly support the scientific goal of improving flux upscaling for ecosystem monitoring and climate science.

title: MSAlign: Aligning Molecule and Mass Spectra Foundation Models for Metabolite Identification
summary: Accurately identifying metabolites i.e. small molecules from mass spectrometry data remains a core challenge in metabolomics, with broad applications in drug discovery, environmental analysis, and clinical research. We address the Molecule Retrieval task, which consists in recovering the chemical structure of a metabolite from its MS/MS spectrum given a set of candidate molecules. While the recent release of benchmark datasets such as MassSpecGym and Spectraverse has considerably accelerated the development of novel machine learning approaches, the complexity of data preprocessing pipelines and the lack of unified implementations make methods and results difficult to reproduce and compare. We make three contributions. First, we propose a unified framework encompassing recent approaches based on representation alignment and contrastive learning. Second, we introduce MSAlign, inspired by multimodal alignment in vision-language models, which learns a shared representation space by aligning two frozen foundation models (DreaMS for mass spectra and ChemBERTa for molecules) through lightweight MLP projections trained with a candidate-based contrastive objective. MSAlign is simple to implement, fast to train and consistently outperforms existing approaches across all benchmarks. Third, we investigate a long-standing evaluation problem: data splitting strategies in molecule retrieval implicitly trade off data leakage against domain shift. We formalize this tension by introducing a quantitative measure of distribution shift, and use it to evaluate splitting strategies in existing benchmarks. All datasets, splits, candidate sets, and a unified implementation of MSAlign and baselines are publicly released to support reproducible research.

AI.tldr: MSAlign: A unified framework for metabolite identification from mass spectrometry data using frozen foundation models with lightweight alignment, outperforming existing methods while addressing data splitting challenges in molecule retrieval benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Accurate metabolite identification from mass spectrometry data is crucial for drug discovery, environmental analysis, and clinical research, but current methods suffer from reproducibility issues due to complex preprocessing pipelines and lack of unified implementations, making results difficult to compare.
AI.method: Introduces MSAlign, which aligns two frozen foundation models (DreaMS for mass spectra and ChemBERTa for molecules) through lightweight MLP projections using candidate-based contrastive learning. Also proposes a unified framework for representation alignment approaches and investigates data splitting strategies with quantitative distribution shift measures.
AI.result: MSAlign consistently outperforms existing approaches across all benchmarks, is simple to implement and fast to train. The framework provides insights into data splitting strategies by formalizing the trade-off between data leakage and domain shift using quantitative distribution shift measures.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a unified, reproducible framework for metabolite identification that advances the field through improved performance, methodological clarity, and addressing long-standing evaluation challenges, with all resources publicly released for community use.

title: ST-TGExplainer: Disentangling Stability and Transition Patterns for Temporal GNN Interpretability
summary: Temporal graph neural networks (TGNNs) have gained significant traction for solving real-world temporal graph tasks. However, their interpretability remains limited, as most TGNNs fail to identify which historical interactions most influence a given prediction. Despite promising progress on interpretable TGNNs, existing methods predominantly focus on previously seen historical interactions, which we term stability patterns, while overlooking newly emerging first-time interactions, which we term transition patterns. Both types of patterns are essential for faithful temporal explanations. To address this limitation, we propose ST-TGExplainer, a self-explainable TGNN that disentangles Stability and Transition patterns in temporal graphs for a more faithful Temporal GNN Explainer. Guided by a disentangled information bottleneck objective, ST-TGExplainer learns a compact explanatory subgraph that remains predictive of the event label while explicitly suppressing label-conditioned redundancy between stability and transition patterns. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ST-TGExplainer achieves strong predictive performance and yields more faithful explanations. Code is available at https://github.com/hjchen-hdu/ST-TGExplainer.

AI.tldr: ST-TGExplainer is a self-explainable temporal graph neural network that disentangles stability patterns (historical interactions) and transition patterns (first-time interactions) to provide more faithful explanations for temporal graph predictions.
AI.motivation: Current temporal graph neural networks (TGNNs) have limited interpretability as they fail to identify which historical interactions influence predictions. Existing interpretable TGNNs focus only on stability patterns (previously seen interactions) while ignoring transition patterns (new first-time interactions), yet both are essential for faithful temporal explanations.
AI.method: ST-TGExplainer uses a disentangled information bottleneck objective to learn compact explanatory subgraphs that remain predictive of event labels while explicitly suppressing label-conditioned redundancy between stability and transition patterns.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that ST-TGExplainer achieves strong predictive performance and yields more faithful explanations compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: ST-TGExplainer effectively addresses the limitation of existing interpretable TGNNs by disentangling both stability and transition patterns, providing more comprehensive and faithful explanations for temporal graph predictions.

title: Smooth Piecewise Cutting for Neural Operator to Handle Discontinuities and Sharp Transitions
summary: Neural operators have achieved strong performance in learning solution operators of partial differential equations (PDEs), but their inherently continuous representations struggle to capture discontinuities and sharp transitions. Existing approaches typically approximate such features within continuous function spaces, often requiring increased model capacity and high-resolution data. In this work, we propose Cut-DeepONet, a two-stage training framework that explicitly models discontinuities while reducing learning complexity. Our approach reformulates the problem via a lifting strategy, partitioning the domain into smooth subregions while representing discontinuities as boundaries in a higher-dimensional space. This separation aligns the operator learning task with the inductive bias of neural networks and avoids directly approximating discontinuities. An additional network predicts input-dependent discontinuity locations for unseen inputs, which are then used to guide the neural operator in generating smooth components within each region. Experiments on benchmark PDEs show that Cut-DeepONet outperforms state-of-the-art methods, even when trained on low-resolution datasets. The method excels on problems with discontinuities and sharp transitions, while using fewer trainable parameters. Our results highlight the benefits of changing the representation of operator learning rather than increasing model complexity.

AI.tldr: Cut-DeepONet introduces a two-stage training framework for neural operators that explicitly models PDE discontinuities by lifting the problem to higher dimensions, partitioning domains into smooth subregions, and using separate networks for discontinuity prediction and smooth component generation.
AI.motivation: Neural operators struggle to capture discontinuities and sharp transitions in PDE solutions due to their continuous representations. Existing approaches require increased model capacity and high-resolution data to approximate such features within continuous function spaces, leading to inefficient learning.
AI.method: A two-stage framework that uses a lifting strategy to partition the domain into smooth subregions while representing discontinuities as boundaries in a higher-dimensional space. The method separates the operator learning task: one network predicts input-dependent discontinuity locations, and another neural operator generates smooth components within each region guided by these predictions.
AI.result: Cut-DeepONet outperforms state-of-the-art methods on benchmark PDEs, particularly excelling on problems with discontinuities and sharp transitions. It achieves superior performance even when trained on low-resolution datasets while using fewer trainable parameters than competing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The research demonstrates that changing the representation of operator learning (explicitly modeling discontinuities through domain partitioning) is more effective than simply increasing model complexity, offering a more efficient framework for learning PDE solution operators with discontinuities.

title: B-cos GNNs: Faithful Explanations through Dynamic Linearity
summary: We introduce B-cos GNNs, an inherently explainable class of graph neural networks whose predictions decompose exactly into per-node, per-feature contributions via a single input-dependent linear map. B-cos GNNs use linear (sum-based) aggregation and replace non-linear message and update functions with B-cos transforms. This induces meaningful, task-specific weight-input alignment that is directly accessible through the model's dynamic linearity. Instance-level explanations follow from a single forward and backward pass, requiring no auxiliary explainer, modified learning objective, or perturbation procedure. Instantiated as a GIN, our approach trades small losses in predictive accuracy for state-of-the-art explainability across diverse synthetic and real-world benchmarks, producing explanations orders of magnitude faster than post-hoc baselines.

AI.tldr: B-cos GNNs are inherently explainable graph neural networks that provide exact per-node, per-feature attribution via a single linear map, trading small accuracy loss for superior explainability and much faster explanation generation compared to post-hoc methods.
AI.motivation: Current graph neural networks lack inherent explainability, requiring complex post-hoc explanation methods that are computationally expensive, may modify learning objectives, or use perturbation procedures. There's a need for GNNs that are naturally interpretable while maintaining good predictive performance.
AI.method: The authors propose B-cos GNNs that use linear (sum-based) aggregation instead of complex non-linear functions, and replace non-linear message and update functions with B-cos transforms. This creates input-dependent linear maps that allow exact decomposition of predictions into per-node, per-feature contributions. The approach leverages B-cos transforms to induce meaningful weight-input alignment that remains accessible through the model's dynamic linearity properties.
AI.result: B-cos GNNs achieve state-of-the-art explainability across diverse synthetic and real-world benchmarks with only small losses in predictive accuracy compared to standard GNNs. They produce explanations orders of magnitude faster than post-hoc baselines, requiring just a single forward and backward pass without auxiliary explainers, modified objectives, or perturbation procedures. When instantiated as a GIN variant, the method demonstrates practical effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: B-cos GNNs represent a promising class of inherently explainable graph neural networks that provide exact, efficient explanations through their architectural design, offering a practical trade-off between modest accuracy reductions and substantial explainability improvements, with significant speed advantages over existing post-hoc explanation methods.

title: A Closed-loop, State-centric, Multi-agent Framework for Passenger Load Estimation from Heterogeneous Data Streams
comment: Preprint version of a paper accepted by the 2026 IEEE 29th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC). 7 pages, 4 figures
summary: To support operations and passenger-facing services, transit agencies need reliable passenger load trajectories. Currently, load estimates are typically inferred from imperfect sensing systems rather than fully observed, and the accuracy of modern automatic passenger counting (APC) systems still varies with station layout, flow intensity, and operating conditions. To address the challenges of robust passenger load estimation from heterogeneous data streams, including incremental count errors, evidence conflicts, and context-dependent sensor reliability, we propose a closed-loop, state-centric, multi-agent framework. This method enforces physical feasibility at every step, allocates trust dynamically among evidence sources, and feeds physics-derived violation residuals back into training for robustness improvement. The architecture consists of a unified stop-event backbone, a coupled Perception--Physical--Fusion loop for stop-by-stop inference, and optional trip-level macro-correction and closed-loop calibration modules.

AI.tldr: Proposes a closed-loop, multi-agent framework for robust passenger load estimation in transit systems using heterogeneous data streams with dynamic trust allocation and physics-derived feedback.
AI.motivation: Transit agencies need reliable passenger load trajectories for operations and services, but current automatic passenger counting (APC) systems have variable accuracy depending on station layout, flow intensity, and operating conditions. There are challenges with heterogeneous data streams including incremental count errors, evidence conflicts, and context-dependent sensor reliability.
AI.method: A closed-loop, state-centric, multi-agent framework that: 1) enforces physical feasibility at every step, 2) allocates trust dynamically among evidence sources, 3) feeds physics-derived violation residuals back into training for robustness improvement. The architecture includes a unified stop-event backbone, a coupled Perception--Physical--Fusion loop for stop-by-stop inference, and optional trip-level macro-correction and closed-loop calibration modules.
AI.result: The proposed method addresses challenges of robust passenger load estimation from heterogeneous data streams by incorporating physical constraints, dynamic trust allocation, and feedback mechanisms for continuous improvement.
AI.conclusion: This framework provides a comprehensive solution to improve passenger load estimation reliability in transit systems by systematically handling imperfect sensor data through physical feasibility enforcement, dynamic evidence weighting, and closed-loop calibration for robustness against real-world operational variations.

title: StableGrad: Backward Scale Control without Batch Normalization
summary: Training very deep neural networks requires controlling the propagation of magnitudes across depth. Without such control, activations and gradients may vanish, explode, or enter unstable regimes that make optimization fail. Modern architectures often mitigate this problem through Batch Normalization, residual connections, or other normalization layers, which repeatedly re-scale or bypass intermediate representations. However, these mechanisms are not always appropriate. In Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), the network represents a continuous physical field and its input derivatives define the training objective, making batch-dependent normalization problematic because it can introduce non-local dependencies into the predicted field and its derivatives. We propose StableGrad, an optimizer-level scale-control mechanism that corrects layer-wise weight-gradient imbalances without modifying the forward model. Because the normalization is applied only after backpropagation and before the optimizer update, the network output, its derivatives, and the physical residual remain unchanged. We analyze the effective training dynamics induced by this rescaling and evaluate StableGrad on deep PINNs as the target application, with BatchNorm-free convolutional networks serving as a diagnostic stress test. On PINN benchmarks, StableGrad improves matched-depth solution accuracy and makes deeper models more reliable under standard optimization. On ResNet and EfficientNet architectures, where removing Batch Normalization normally leads to training collapse, StableGrad stabilizes optimization without introducing any other architectural change. These results show that optimizer-level control of weight-gradient scale can provide a practical alternative when forward normalization is unavailable or undesirable.

AI.tldr: StableGrad is an optimizer-level mechanism that stabilizes deep neural network training by correcting weight-gradient imbalances without modifying the forward model, making it suitable for Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) where traditional normalization methods are problematic.
AI.motivation: Training very deep neural networks requires controlling magnitude propagation to prevent vanishing/exploding gradients. Traditional methods like Batch Normalization, residual connections, and normalization layers are not suitable for Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) because they can introduce non-local dependencies into physical field predictions and their derivatives, which are critical for PINN training objectives.
AI.method: StableGrad is an optimizer-level scale-control mechanism that corrects layer-wise weight-gradient imbalances by applying normalization only after backpropagation and before optimizer updates. This approach does not modify the forward model, keeping network outputs, derivatives, and physical residuals unchanged while stabilizing training dynamics.
AI.result: On PINN benchmarks, StableGrad improves solution accuracy for matched-depth models and enables reliable training of deeper architectures. For ResNet and EfficientNet architectures, where removing BatchNorm normally causes training collapse, StableGrad stabilizes optimization without architectural changes, demonstrating its effectiveness as an alternative to forward normalization.
AI.conclusion: Optimizer-level control of weight-gradient scale provides a practical alternative to traditional normalization methods when forward normalization is unavailable or undesirable, particularly for specialized applications like Physics-Informed Neural Networks where preserving physical consistency is crucial.

title: LionMuon: Alternating Spectral and Sign Descent for Efficient Training
summary: In large-scale optimization, the cheapness and effectiveness of update steps are the most crucial factors for a successful optimizer. Sign-based optimizers like Lion or Signum produce cheap per-step updates, whereas Muon's spectral matrix-sign update gives a much stronger direction at a substantially higher per-step cost. In this work, we propose LionMuon, which retains the effectiveness of Muon steps while considerably cutting the averaged iteration cost, similar to sign-based methods. It alternates between Lion's and Muon's updates on a fixed period P, sharing a single dual-EMA momentum buffer between them. The optimizer state memory therefore matches Lion and is exactly half of AdamW's. A simpler single-EMA variant, SignMuon, by itself already outperforms pure Muon. At P = 2, LionMuon Pareto-dominates Muon, Lion, Signum, and AdamW on every dataset and architecture we tested at 124M model size, reaching lower validation loss at lower compute, and the same advantage persists at 355M and 720M scale. On the theory side, we prove sharp complexity bounds under heavy-tailed noise which are governed by period-averaged smoothness and noise that interpolate between Muon's and Lion's constants. These bounds predict the compute-optimal period and the conditions under which LionMuon outruns Muon and Lion. Code: https://github.com/brain-lab-research/lion-muon

AI.tldr: LionMuon is a novel optimizer that alternates between Lion's cheap sign-based updates and Muon's expensive but effective spectral matrix-sign updates, achieving lower compute cost and better performance than existing optimizers.
AI.motivation: Existing sign-based optimizers (Lion, Signum) are computationally cheap but less effective, while Muon's spectral matrix-sign updates are much more effective but expensive per-step. There's a need for an optimizer that combines the effectiveness of Muon with the low compute cost of sign-based methods.
AI.method: LionMuon alternates between Lion's and Muon's updates on a fixed period P, sharing a single dual-EMA momentum buffer between them. This reduces averaged iteration cost while maintaining effectiveness. A simpler single-EMA variant called SignMuon is also introduced.
AI.result: At P=2, LionMuon Pareto-dominates Muon, Lion, Signum, and AdamW on all tested datasets and architectures at 124M model size, achieving lower validation loss at lower compute cost. The advantage persists at 355M and 720M scales. Theoretical bounds show conditions where LionMuon outperforms both Muon and Lion.
AI.conclusion: LionMuon successfully bridges the gap between cheap sign-based optimizers and expensive but effective spectral methods, providing a compute-efficient optimizer that outperforms existing approaches across different model scales.

title: Fast and Featureless Node Representation Learning with Partial Pairwise Supervision
summary: We introduce Contrastive FUSE, a fast and unified framework for scalable node representation learning in graphs with partially available pairwise node labels and no available node features.
